title: Deep Predictive Coding Networks for Video Prediction and Unsupervised Learning
searchable_text:
Deep Predictive Coding Networks for Video Prediction and Unsupervised Learning Unsupervised learning of visual-world structure from unlabeled video sequences, including synthetic object motion and car-mounted camera videos. A predictive coding neural network predicts future video frames, with layers making local predictions and forwarding only prediction errors upward. It instantiates neuroscience-inspired predictive coding in a deep video-prediction network where each layer predicts locally and passes deviations rather than raw activations. TITLE: Deep Predictive Coding Networks for Video Prediction and Unsupervised Learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
While great strides have been made in using deep learning algorithms to solve supervised learning tasks, the problem of unsupervised learning - leveraging unlabeled examples to learn about the structure of a domain - remains a difficult unsolved challenge. Here, we explore prediction of future frames in a video sequence as an unsupervised learning rule for learning about the structure of the visual world. We describe a predictive neural network ("PredNet")

title: Deep Recurrent Q-Learning for Partially Observable MDPs
searchable_text:
Deep Recurrent Q-Learning for Partially Observable MDPs Atari reinforcement-learning control under partial observability, including single-frame inputs and flickering game screens where complete game state is not always visible. Add recurrency to DQN by replacing the first post-convolutional fully connected layer with an LSTM, producing a Deep Recurrent Q-Network that integrates information through time. Instead of stacking a history of frames in DQN’s input layer, DRQN uses recurrent memory by replacing DQN’s first post-convolutional fully connected layer with an LSTM. TITLE: Deep Recurrent Q-Learning for Partially Observable MDPs

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep Reinforcement Learning has yielded proficient controllers for complex tasks. However, these controllers have limited memory and rely on being able to perceive the complete game screen at each decision point. To address these shortcomings, this article investigates the effects of adding recurrency to a Deep Q-Network (DQN) by replacing the first post-convolutional fully-connected layer with a recurrent LSTM. The resulting \textit{Deep Recurrent Q-Network} (DRQN), although cap

title: Deep reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation with asynchronous off-policy updates
searchable_text:
Deep reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation with asynchronous off-policy updates Autonomous learning of complex 3D robotic manipulation skills, including door opening on real physical robots, without demonstrations or hand-designed representations. Off-policy deep Q-function training for neural network policies, parallelized across multiple robots that asynchronously pool policy updates. Scaled off-policy deep Q-learning from simulated/simple tasks to complex 3D manipulation and real robots, adding asynchronous pooled updates across multiple robots. TITLE: Deep reinforcement learning for robotic manipulation with asynchronous off-policy updates

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Reinforcement learning holds the promise of enabling autonomous robots to learn large repertoires of behavioral skills with minimal human intervention. However, robotic applications of reinforcement learning often compromise the autonomy of the learning process in favor of achieving training times that are practical for real physical systems. This typically involves introducing hand-engineered policy representations and human-supplied demonstrati

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning framework for Autonomous Driving
searchable_text:
Deep Reinforcement Learning framework for Autonomous Driving Autonomous driving, especially maneuvering under environmental interaction with other vehicles, pedestrians, roadworks, and partially observable road scenarios. A deep reinforcement learning autonomous-driving framework incorporating recurrent neural networks for partial observability and attention models to focus computation on relevant information. Extends DeepMind-style deep reinforcement learning successes from Atari games and Go to autonomous driving, adding RNN-based information integration and attention for embedded deployment. TITLE: Deep Reinforcement Learning framework for Autonomous Driving

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Reinforcement learning is considered to be a strong AI paradigm which can be used to teach machines through interaction with the environment and learning from their mistakes. Despite its perceived utility, it has not yet been successfully applied in automotive applications. Motivated by the successful demonstrations of learning of Atari games and Go by Google DeepMind, we propose a framework for autonomous driving using deep reinforcement learning. This is of particular rele

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences
searchable_text:
Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences Complex reinforcement-learning tasks where reward functions are unavailable or hard to specify, including Atari games, simulated robot locomotion, and novel behavior training. Learn a reward signal from non-expert human preference comparisons between pairs of trajectory segments, then use it to train RL agents without direct access to the environment reward. Compared with prior human-feedback learning, it scales to considerably more complex behaviors and environments while using sparse non-expert preference feedback. TITLE: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
For sophisticated reinforcement learning (RL) systems to interact usefully with real-world environments, we need to communicate complex goals to these systems. In this work, we explore goals defined in terms of (non-expert) human preferences between pairs of trajectory segments. We show that this approach can effectively solve complex RL tasks without access to the reward function, including Atari games and simulated robot locomotion, while providing feedback on less than one percent

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Self-Play in Imperfect-Information Games
searchable_text:
Deep Reinforcement Learning from Self-Play in Imperfect-Information Games Large-scale imperfect-information games, especially poker variants such as Leduc poker and Limit Texas Holdem. Combines fictitious self-play with deep reinforcement learning to learn approximate Nash equilibria end-to-end without prior domain knowledge. Replaces prior handcrafted-abstraction Nash-equilibrium computation with scalable end-to-end self-play deep reinforcement learning without prior domain knowledge. TITLE: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Self-Play in Imperfect-Information Games

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many real-world applications can be described as large-scale games of imperfect information. To deal with these challenging domains, prior work has focused on computing Nash equilibria in a handcrafted abstraction of the domain. In this paper we introduce the first scalable end-to-end approach to learning approximate Nash equilibria without prior domain knowledge. Our method combines fictitious self-play with deep reinforcement learning. When applied to Leduc po

title: Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters
searchable_text:
Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters Reproducing and interpreting state-of-the-art deep reinforcement learning results on standard benchmark environments. Investigates reproducibility challenges in deep RL, illustrates variability in reported baseline comparisons, and proposes experimental-reporting guidelines for significance metrics and tighter standardization. Shifts from reporting new deep RL performance to auditing reproducibility practices, metric variability, baseline comparisons, and guidelines for more reliable claims. TITLE: Deep Reinforcement Learning that Matters

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 In recent years, significant progress has been made in solving challenging problems across various domains using deep reinforcement learning (RL). Reproducing existing work and accurately judging the improvements offered by novel methods is vital to sustaining this progress. Unfortunately, reproducing results for state-of-the-art deep RL methods is seldom straightforward. In particular, non-determinism in standard benchmark environments, combined with variance intrinsic to the methods, can

title: Deep spatial autoencoders for visuomotor learning
searchable_text:
Deep spatial autoencoders for visuomotor learning Automated acquisition of robotic motion skills requiring object-configuration state from vision, including pushing a toy block, using a spatula to pick up rice, and hanging rope on a hook. Deep spatial autoencoder learns task-relevant feature points directly from camera images, then efficient reinforcement learning with local linear models learns closed-loop visuomotor skills from those features. Replaced manually supplied detailed state representations with automatically learned image-based feature points for reinforcement-learning controllers. TITLE: Deep spatial autoencoders for visuomotor learning

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Reinforcement learning provides a powerful and flexible framework for automated acquisition of robotic motion skills. However, applying reinforcement learning requires a sufficiently detailed representation of the state, including the configuration of task-relevant objects. We present an approach that automates state-space construction by learning a state representation directly from camera images. Our method uses a deep spatial autoencoder to acquire a set of feature points that descr

title: Deep Spatio-Temporal Residual Networks for Citywide Crowd Flows Prediction
searchable_text:
Deep Spatio-Temporal Residual Networks for Citywide Crowd Flows Prediction Citywide crowd-flow forecasting: predicting inflow and outflow for every region of a city under traffic, event, weather, and calendar influences. End-to-end ST-ResNet uses residual convolutional branches for temporal closeness, period, and trend, dynamically aggregating them by region and combining external factors. Compared with prior well-known methods, the approach adds residual spatio-temporal branches for closeness, period, and trend plus dynamic branch/region aggregation with external factors. TITLE: Deep Spatio-Temporal Residual Networks for Citywide Crowd Flows Prediction

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 Forecasting the flow of crowds is of great importance to traffic management and public safety, and very challenging as it is affected by many complex factors, such as inter-region traffic, events, and weather. We propose a deep-learning-based approach, called ST-ResNet, to collectively forecast the inflow and outflow of crowds in each and every region of a city. We design an end-to-end structure of ST-ResNet based on unique properties of spatio-temporal dat

title: Deep Speech 2 : End-to-End Speech Recognition in English and Mandarin
searchable_text:
Deep Speech 2 : End-to-End Speech Recognition in English and Mandarin Automatic speech recognition for English and Mandarin Chinese, including diverse speech across noisy environments, accents, and languages. End-to-end deep learning speech recognition replacing hand-engineered pipelines with neural networks, accelerated by HPC and GPU Batch Dispatch for scalable online deployment. Moved from pipelines of hand-engineered speech components to neural-network end-to-end learning, with HPC techniques reducing experiment time from weeks to days. TITLE: Deep Speech 2 : End-to-End Speech Recognition in English and Mandarin

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We show that an end-to-end deep learning approach can be used to recognize either English or Mandarin Chinese speech-two vastly different languages. Because it replaces entire pipelines of hand-engineered components with neural networks, end-to-end learning allows us to handle a diverse variety of speech including noisy environments, accents and different languages. Key to our approach is our application of HPC techniques, enabling experiments that previously took w

title: Deep Supervised, but Not Unsupervised, Models May Explain IT Cortical Representation
searchable_text:
Deep Supervised, but Not Unsupervised, Models May Explain IT Cortical Representation Explaining human and monkey inferior temporal cortex representational geometry during visual object recognition. Use supervised deep convolutional network representations, evaluated against IT representational dissimilarity matrices, with weighted feature combinations and linear margins for key category divisions. Moved beyond unsupervised and neuroscientific object-recognition models by using supervised deep convolutional features trained on over a million category-labeled images. TITLE: Deep Supervised, but Not Unsupervised, Models May Explain IT Cortical Representation

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Inferior temporal (IT) cortex in human and nonhuman primates serves visual object recognition. Computational object-vision models, although continually improving, do not yet reach human performance. It is unclear to what extent the internal representations of computational models can explain the IT representation. Here we investigate a wide range of computational model representations (37 in total), testing their categorization performance and their a

title: Deep visual foresight for planning robot motion
searchable_text:
Deep visual foresight for planning robot motion Robot learning for nonprehensile manipulation, especially pushing objects in varied environments without human supervision. Combine deep action-conditioned video prediction models with model-predictive control trained on entirely unlabeled robot interaction data. Shifted from supervised, calibrated, instrumented robot training toward unlabeled video-prediction-based model-predictive control without calibrated cameras or precise sensing and actuation. TITLE: Deep visual foresight for planning robot motion

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A key challenge in scaling up robot learning to many skills and environments is removing the need for human supervision, so that robots can collect their own data and improve their own performance without being limited by the cost of requesting human feedback. Model-based reinforcement learning holds the promise of enabling an agent to learn to predict the effects of its actions, which could provide flexible predictive models for a wide range of tasks and environments, without detailed h

title: DeepArchitect: Automatically Designing and Training Deep Architectures
searchable_text:
DeepArchitect: Automatically Designing and Training Deep Architectures Automatic design and training of deep neural network architectures and hyperparameters for image classification benchmarks such as CIFAR-10 and MNIST. An extensible modular language defines tree-structured architecture and hyperparameter search spaces, compiles sampled models to computational graphs, and supports search via random search, MCTS, and SMBO. Moves beyond automatic hyperparameter optimization for simple spaces by representing complex deep-architecture spaces as traversable tree-structured programs with pluggable architecture search algorithms. TITLE: DeepArchitect: Automatically Designing and Training Deep Architectures

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In deep learning, performance is strongly affected by the choice of architecture and hyperparameters. While there has been extensive work on automatic hyperpa- rameter optimization for simple spaces, complex spaces such as the space of deep architectures remain largely unexplored. As a result, the choice of architecture is done manually by the human expert through a slow trial and error process guided mainly by intuition. In this paper we describe a framework for a

title: DeepCodec: Adaptive sensing and recovery via deep convolutional neural networks
searchable_text:
DeepCodec: Adaptive sensing and recovery via deep convolutional neural networks Sensing and recovering structured signals from undersampled measurements. Learn adaptive undersampled measurement and recovery mappings with a deep convolutional neural network trained on representative structured signals. Replaces conventional compressive sensing’s random linear measurements and convex or iterative recovery with learned adaptive sensing and CNN-based reconstruction. TITLE: DeepCodec: Adaptive sensing and recovery via deep convolutional neural networks

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We develop a novel computational sensing framework for sensing and recovering structured signals. When trained on a set of representative signals, our framework learns to take undersampled measurements and recover signals from these measurements using a deep convolutional neural network. In other words, it learns a transformation from the original signals to a near-optimal number of undersampled measurements and the inverse transformation from measurements

title: DeepCoder: Learning to Write Programs
searchable_text:
DeepCoder: Learning to Write Programs Solving programming competition-style problems from input-output examples. Train a neural network to predict program properties from input-output examples, then use those predictions to guide enumerative search and an SMT-based solver. Changed from standalone enumerative/SMT or RNN approaches to neural prediction of program properties that augments traditional program-search solvers. TITLE: DeepCoder: Learning to Write Programs

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We develop a first line of attack for solving programming competition-style problems from input-output examples using deep learning. The approach is to train a neural network to predict properties of the program that generated the outputs from the inputs. We use the neural network’s predictions to augment search techniques from the programming languages community, including enumerative search and an SMT-based solver. Empirically, we show that our approach leads to an order of magnitude speedup ove

title: DeepMath - Deep Sequence Models for Premise Selection
searchable_text:
DeepMath - Deep Sequence Models for Premise Selection Premise selection in automated theorem proving on the Mizar corpus. A two-stage neural sequence-model approach for premise selection that avoids hand-engineered features. Replaces existing state-of-the-art models’ hand-engineered features with deep neural sequence models. TITLE: DeepMath - Deep Sequence Models for Premise Selection

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the effectiveness of neural sequence models for premise selection in automated theorem proving, one of the main bottlenecks in the formalization of mathematics. We propose a two stage approach for this task that yields good results for the premise selection task on the Mizar corpus while avoiding the hand-engineered features of existing state-of-the-art models. To our knowledge, this is the first time deep learning has been applied to theorem proving on a large scale.

title: DeepSD: Generating High Resolution Climate Change Projections through Single Image Super-Resolution
searchable_text:
DeepSD: Generating High Resolution Climate Change Projections through Single Image Super-Resolution Statistical downscaling of daily precipitation and climate variables from coarse Earth System Model outputs to localized high-resolution projections. A stacked SRCNN framework augmented with multi-scale input channels to map low-resolution climate variables to high-resolution projections. DeepSD changes SRCNN by generalizing it into a stacked climate downscaling framework and adding multi-scale input channels. TITLE: DeepSD: Generating High Resolution Climate Change Projections through Single Image Super-Resolution

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The impacts of climate change are felt by most critical systems, such as infrastructure, ecological systems, and power-plants. However, contemporary Earth System Models (ESM) are run at spatial resolutions too coarse for assessing effects this localized. Local scale projections can be obtained using statistical downscaling, a technique which uses historical climate observations to learn a low-resolution to high-resolution mapping. Depen

title: Dense magnetized plasma associated with a fast radio burst
searchable_text:
Dense magnetized plasma associated with a fast radio burst Environmental diagnosis and progenitor discrimination for fast radio bursts, specifically FRB 110523. Examine archival FRB 110523 data for Faraday rotation, dispersion, and scattering signatures to infer magnetized plasma and source environment. Unlike a prior burst with circular polarization but no linear polarization or rotation measure, this work detects Faraday rotation in FRB 110523. TITLE: Dense magnetized plasma associated with a fast radio burst

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Fast radio bursts are bright, unresolved, non-repeating, broadband, millisecond flashes, found primarily at high Galactic latitudes, with dispersion measures much larger than expected for a Galactic source. The inferred all-sky burst rate is comparable to the core-collapse supernova rate out to redshift 0.5. If the observed dispersion measures are assumed to be dominated by the intergalactic medium, the sources are at cosmological distances with redshifts of 0.2 to 1 (refs 10

title: Densely Connected Convolutional Networks
searchable_text:
Densely Connected Convolutional Networks Object recognition benchmarks including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, and ImageNet. Connect each layer to every other layer feed-forward; each layer consumes all preceding feature maps and provides its feature maps to all subsequent layers. Replaces sequential L-layer connectivity with dense all-to-all feed-forward connections, increasing direct connections from L to L(L+1)/2. TITLE: Densely Connected Convolutional Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recent work has shown that convolutional networks can be substantially deeper, more accurate, and efficient to train if they contain shorter connections between layers close to the input and those close to the output. In this paper, we embrace this observation and introduce the Dense Convolutional Network (DenseNet), which connects each layer to every other layer in a feed-forward fashion. Whereas traditional convolutional networks with L layers have L connections—one between each layer

title: Density Functional Theory and Optimal Transportation with Coulomb Cost
searchable_text:
Density Functional Theory and Optimal Transportation with Coulomb Cost Exchange-correlation functionals in density functional theory for strongly correlated electrons in the semiclassical Coulomb limit. Formulate the semiclassical exchange-correlation limit as an optimal transport problem with a density-dependent optimal map T. Recasts a previously formal physics limit as an optimal transport problem and adds existence, uniqueness, and radial-density explicit-map results. TITLE: Density Functional Theory and Optimal Transportation with Coulomb Cost

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present here novel insight into exchange‐correlation functionals in density functional theory, based on the viewpoint of optimal transport. We show that in the case of two electrons and in the semiclassical limit, the exact exchange‐correlation functional reduces to a very interesting functional that depends on an optimal transport map T associated with a given density ρ. The limit problem has been suggested, on grounds of formal arguments, in the physics litera

title: Density-matrix simulation of small surface codes under current and projected experimental noise
searchable_text:
Density-matrix simulation of small surface codes under current and projected experimental noise Predicting quantum memory and computation performance of small superconducting surface-code logical qubits under current and projected experimental noise. Density-matrix simulation of Surface-17, with approximate extension to Surface-49, using experimental transmon error parameters to optimize QEC cycle choices and relaxation-asymmetry feedback. Moved from a recently proposed surface-code circuit to density-matrix performance simulation with experimental planar circuit-QED transmon noise and QEC design optimization. TITLE: Density-matrix simulation of small surface codes under current and projected experimental noise

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a density-matrix simulation of the quantum memory and computing performance of the distance-3 logical qubit Surface-17, following a recently proposed quantum circuit and using experimental error parameters for transmon qubits in a planar circuit QED architecture. We use this simulation to optimize components of the QEC scheme (e.g., trading off stabilizer measurement infidelity for reduced cycle time) and to investigate the benef

title: Describing Textures in the Wild
searchable_text:
Describing Textures in the Wild Describing and recognizing semantic texture attributes in natural images and material datasets. Build a 47-term semantic texture vocabulary and DTD, then recognize describable texture attributes using IFV and DeCAF representations. Moved object-recognition representations, especially IFV and DeCAF, into texture recognition and paired them with a new describable texture attribute dataset. TITLE: Describing Textures in the Wild

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Patterns and textures are key characteristics of many natural objects: a shirt can be striped, the wings of a butterfly can be veined, and the skin of an animal can be scaly. Aiming at supporting this dimension in image understanding, we address the problem of describing textures with semantic attributes. We identify a vocabulary of forty-seven texture terms and use them to describe a large dataset of patterns collected "in the wild". The resulting Describable Textures Dataset (DTD) is a basis to seek t

title: Designing Neural Network Architectures using Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Designing Neural Network Architectures using Reinforcement Learning Automatic design of convolutional neural network architectures for image classification and other given learning tasks. MetaQNN uses Q-learning with epsilon-greedy exploration and experience replay to sequentially choose CNN layers from a finite architecture space. CNN architecture design shifts from handcrafted experimentation or modification of existing networks to reinforcement-learning-based automatic generation. TITLE: Designing Neural Network Architectures using Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
At present, designing convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures requires both human expertise and labor. New architectures are handcrafted by careful experimentation or modified from a handful of existing networks. We introduce MetaQNN, a meta-modeling algorithm based on reinforcement learning to automatically generate high-performing CNN architectures for a given learning task. The learning agent is trained to sequentially choose CNN layers using $Q$-learning

title: Detection Algorithms for Communication Systems Using Deep Learning
searchable_text:
Detection Algorithms for Communication Systems Using Deep Learning Detection in communication systems whose transmitted-to-received signal relationship is unknown or difficult to model, especially chemical/molecular communication platforms. Train communication-system detectors using deep learning without requiring mathematical knowledge of the underlying channel model. Replaced a previous simple detector that assumed no channel knowledge with trained deep learning detectors that also require no channel model. TITLE: Detection Algorithms for Communication Systems Using Deep Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The design and analysis of communication systems typically rely on the development of mathematical models that describe the underlying communication channel, which dictates the relationship between the transmitted and the received signals. However, in some systems, such as molecular communication systems where chemical signals are used for transfer of information, it is not possible to accurately model this relationship. In these scenarios, because of the lack of mathe

title: Detection of dispersed radio pulses: a machine learning approach to candidate identification and classification
searchable_text:
Detection of dispersed radio pulses: a machine learning approach to candidate identification and classification Identifying and classifying dispersed pulse groups in single-pulse radio search outputs for extraterrestrial/transient astronomical signals. Two-stage pipeline: identify dispersed pulse groups via peak tracking around SNR–dispersion-measure maxima, extract features, then classify groups using supervised machine learning. Introduced a new two-stage single-pulse candidate identification/classification approach where the literature lacked machine learning techniques for single-pulse detection. TITLE: Detection of dispersed radio pulses: a machine learning approach to candidate identification and classification

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Searching for extraterrestrial, transient signals in astronomical data sets is an active area of current research. However, machine learning techniques are lacking in the literature concerning single-pulse detection. This paper presents a new, two-stage approach for identifying and classifying dispersed pulse groups (DPGs) in single-pulse search output. title: Differentially Private Federated Learning: A Client Level Perspective
searchable_text:
Differentially Private Federated Learning: A Client Level Perspective Client-level privacy in federated learning where model updates can reveal a participant's contribution or dataset information. Client-sided differential privacy preserving federated optimization hides individual clients' training contributions while managing privacy-performance trade-offs. Moves federated learning from implicit data non-sharing privacy toward explicit client-level differential privacy against attacks on distributed model contributions. TITLE: Differentially Private Federated Learning: A Client Level Perspective

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Federated learning is a recent advance in privacy protection. In this context, a trusted curator aggregates parameters optimized in decentralized fashion by multiple clients. The resulting model is then distributed back to all clients, ultimately converging to a joint representative model without explicitly having to share the data. However, the protocol is vulnerable to differential attacks, which could originate from any party contributing during federated optimiz

title: Discrete Distribution Estimation under Local Privacy
searchable_text:
Discrete Distribution Estimation under Local Privacy Estimating the distribution of a categorical statistic without collecting users’ underlying data. Hashed K-ary Randomized Response for locally private categorical distribution estimation. Introduces hashed K-ary Randomized Response and new theory clarifying when KRR or RAPPOR is order-optimal versus existing mechanisms. TITLE: Discrete Distribution Estimation under Local Privacy

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The collection and analysis of user data drives improvements in the app and web ecosystems, but comes with risks to privacy. This paper examines discrete distribution estimation under local privacy, a setting wherein service providers can learn the distribution of a categorical statistic of interest without collecting the underlying data. We present new mechanisms, including hashed K-ary Randomized Response (KRR), that empirically meet or exceed the utility of existing mechanisms at

title: Discrete Wasserstein barycenters: optimal transport for discrete data
searchable_text:
Discrete Wasserstein barycenters: optimal transport for discrete data Computing and characterizing Wasserstein barycenters for probability measures with finite support in discrete data and multi-marginal transportation settings. Use polyhedral theory enabled by finite-support marginals to characterize discrete Wasserstein barycenters, including sparsity and non-mass-splitting optimal transports. Extends barycenter theory beyond the well-developed absolutely continuous case by proving analogous discrete results using polyhedral structure, with added sparsity and non-mass-splitting properties. TITLE: Discrete Wasserstein barycenters: optimal transport for discrete data

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Wasserstein barycenters correspond to optimal solutions of transportation problems for several marginals, and as such have a wide range of applications ranging from economics to statistics and computer science. When the marginal probability measures are absolutely continuous (or vanish on small sets) the theory of Wasserstein barycenters is well-developed [see the seminal paper (Agueh and Carlier in SIAM J Math Anal 43(2):904–924, 2011)]. However, exact continuous c

title: Discretization of the 3D Monge-Ampere operator, between Wide Stencils and Power Diagrams
searchable_text:
Discretization of the 3D Monge-Ampere operator, between Wide Stencils and Power Diagrams Discretizing and solving the 3D Monge-Ampere operator on domains represented by Cartesian grids. A monotone degenerate-elliptic Cartesian-grid Monge-Ampere discretization combining Wide Stencil simplicity with power-diagram-inspired optimal-transport accuracy improvements. Compared with Wide Stencil methods, it retains simplicity while improving accuracy using power-diagram optimal-transport discretization ideas. TITLE: Discretization of the 3D Monge-Ampere operator, between Wide Stencils and Power Diagrams

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce a monotone (degenerate elliptic) discretization of the Monge-Ampere operator, on domains discretized on cartesian grids. The scheme is consistent provided the solution hessian condition number is uniformly bounded. Our approach enjoys the simplicity of the Wide Stencil method, but significantly improves its accuracy using ideas from discretizations of optimal transport based on power diagrams. title: Discriminative Embeddings of Latent Variable Models for Structured Data
searchable_text:
Discriminative Embeddings of Latent Variable Models for Structured Data Scalable classification and regression representations for structured data such as sequences, trees, and graphs. Embed latent variable models into feature spaces and learn those feature spaces using discriminative information, extracting features via sequential function mappings similar to graphical model inference. Replaced predesigned structured-data kernels followed by convex learning with discriminatively learned embeddings of latent variable models into feature spaces. TITLE: Discriminative Embeddings of Latent Variable Models for Structured Data

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Kernel classifiers and regressors designed for structured data, such as sequences, trees and graphs, have significantly advanced a number of interdisciplinary areas such as computational biology and drug design. Typically, kernels are designed beforehand for a data type which either exploit statistics of the structures or make use of probabilistic generative models, and then a discriminative classifier is learned based on the kernels via convex optimization. title: Distributed Compressive CSIT Estimation and Feedback for FDD Multi-User Massive MIMO Systems
searchable_text:
Distributed Compressive CSIT Estimation and Feedback for FDD Multi-User Massive MIMO Systems CSIT estimation and feedback overhead reduction for FDD multi-user massive MIMO systems. Distributed compressive CSIT estimation: users collect compressed measurements locally; the base station jointly recovers CSIT using joint orthogonal matching pursuit exploiting hidden joint sparsity. Replaces naïve conventional CS with distributed local compressed measurements and joint base-station CSIT recovery exploiting hidden joint sparsity across users. TITLE: Distributed Compressive CSIT Estimation and Feedback for FDD Multi-User Massive MIMO Systems

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
To fully utilize the spatial multiplexing gains or array gains of massive MIMO, the channel state information must be obtained at the transmitter side (CSIT). However, conventional CSIT estimation approaches are not suitable for FDD massive MIMO systems because of the overwhelming training and feedback overhead. In this paper, we consider multi-user massive MIMO systems and deploy the compressive sensing (CS) technique to reduce the training a

title: Distributed delayed stochastic optimization
searchable_text:
Distributed delayed stochastic optimization Large-scale stochastic statistical optimization over datasets too large for one computer in distributed asynchronous systems. Gradient-based distributed optimization where a master updates parameters while workers compute delayed stochastic gradients in parallel. Shows delayed asynchronous distributed stochastic gradients can match the optimal no-delay distributed convergence rate. TITLE: Distributed delayed stochastic optimization

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We analyze the convergence of gradient-based optimization algorithms that base their updates on delayed stochastic gradient information. The main application of our results is to gradient-based distributed optimization algorithms where a master node performs parameter updates while worker nodes compute stochastic gradients based on local information in parallel, which may give rise to delays due to asynchrony. We take motivation from statistical problems where the size of the data is so larg

title: Distributed Mean Estimation with Limited Communication
searchable_text:
Distributed Mean Estimation with Limited Communication Communication-efficient distributed mean estimation for d-dimensional data across n clients without probabilistic assumptions. Stochastic quantization augmented with structured random rotation and an improved coding strategy to communicate distributed client vectors using few bits while preserving mean estimates. Unlike previous works, removes probabilistic data assumptions and upgrades naive stochastic rounding with structured random rotation plus better coding to achieve lower MSE. TITLE: Distributed Mean Estimation with Limited Communication

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Motivated by the need for distributed learning and optimization algorithms with low communication cost, we study communication efficient algorithms for distributed mean estimation. Unlike previous works, we make no probabilistic assumptions on the data. We first show that for d dimensional data with n clients, a naive stochastic rounding approach yields a mean squared error (MSE) of Θ (d/n) and uses a constant number of bits per dimension per client. We then extend this naive algo

title: Distribution-free cumulative sum control charts using bootstrap-based control limits
searchable_text:
Distribution-free cumulative sum control charts using bootstrap-based control limits Phase II univariate statistical process control when in-control data exist but both in-control and out-of-control process distributions are unknown. Use CUSUM charts with a sequence of control limits conditioned on the last zero time, estimating those limits by bootstrap. Replaces the traditional single Normal-theory CUSUM control limit with bootstrap-estimated sequential limits based on conditional CUSUM distributions. TITLE: Distribution-free cumulative sum control charts using bootstrap-based control limits

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper deals with phase II, univariate, statistical process control when a set of in-control data is available, and when both the in-control and out-of-control distributions of the process are unknown. Existing process control techniques typically require substantial knowledge about the in-control and out-of-control distributions of the process, which is often difficult to obtain in practice. We propose (a) using a sequence of control limits for t

title: Distribution Free Prediction Bands
searchable_text:
Distribution Free Prediction Bands Distribution-free, nonparametric prediction bands with emphasis on finite-sample coverage behavior. Combine conformal prediction with nonparametric conditional density estimation to form COPS, with finite-sample guarantees, oracle-band convergence, fast approximation, and data-driven bandwidth selection. Strengthens original conformal prediction by optimizing prediction sets using conditional density estimation and providing a stronger finite-sample guarantee. TITLE: Distribution Free Prediction Bands

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study distribution free, nonparametric prediction bands with a special focus on their finite sample behavior. First we investigate and develop different notions of finite sample coverage guarantees. Then we give a new prediction band estimator by combining the idea of "conformal prediction" (Vovk et al. 2009) with nonparametric conditional density estimation. The proposed estimator, called COPS (Conformal Optimized Prediction Set), always has finite sample guarantee in a stronger sense than the or

title: Distribution-Free Predictive Inference for Regression
searchable_text:
Distribution-Free Predictive Inference for Regression Distribution-free predictive inference for regression: constructing valid prediction bands or intervals for response variables without relying on distributional assumptions. A conformal inference framework constructs prediction bands around any regression-function estimator while retaining estimator consistency and finite-sample marginal coverage. Generalized conformal regression to work with any estimator, added split/full comparisons, rank-one-out intervals, locally varying bands, and model-free LOCO variable importance. TITLE: Distribution-Free Predictive Inference for Regression

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
ABSTRACT We develop a general framework for distribution-free predictive inference in regression, using conformal inference. The proposed methodology allows for the construction of a prediction band for the response variable using any estimator of the regression function. The resulting prediction band preserves the consistency properties of the original estimator under standard assumptions, while guaranteeing finite-sample marginal coverage even when these assumptions do not hold.

title: Distribution's template estimate with Wasserstein metrics
searchable_text:
Distribution's template estimate with Wasserstein metrics Comparing distributions of random variables and defining a mean pattern across a sample of random events. Estimate a mean distribution using an iterative Wasserstein-space barycenter of measures. Introduces an iterative Wasserstein-barycenter estimate for mean distribution/template recovery. TITLE: Distribution's template estimate with Wasserstein metrics

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper we tackle the problem of comparing distributions of random variables and defining a mean pattern between a sample of random events. Using barycenters of measures in the Wasserstein space, we propose an iterative version as an estimation of the mean distribution. Moreover, when the distributions are a common measure warped by a centered random operator, then the barycenter enables to recover this distribution template.

title: Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Quantile Regression
searchable_text:
Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Quantile Regression Reinforcement learning agents estimating long-term return under random state transitions, rewards, and actions, evaluated on Atari 2600 games. Model the full distribution over returns and learn value distributions using a quantile-regression distributional reinforcement learning algorithm. Extends Bellemare, Dabney, and Munos (2017) to approximate distributions and introduces a novel distributional RL algorithm aligned with the theory. TITLE: Distributional Reinforcement Learning with Quantile Regression

ABSTRACT:

When sampled probabilistically, these state transitions, rewards, and actions can all induce randomness in the observed long-term return. Traditionally, reinforcement learning algorithms average over this randomness to estimate the value function. title: DNF – Galaxy photometric redshift by Directional Neighbourhood Fitting
searchable_text:
DNF – Galaxy photometric redshift by Directional Neighbourhood Fitting Photometric redshift estimation for galaxies from wide-field images measured across several photometric bands. Directional Neighbourhood Fitting combines a new directional neighbourhood metric, neighbourhood fitting photo-z estimation, and probability distribution generation. Compared with prior empirical nearest-neighbour photometric redshift tools, DNF introduces a Directional Neighbourhood metric, Neighbourhood Fitting strategy, and photo-z probability distribution method. TITLE: DNF – Galaxy photometric redshift by Directional Neighbourhood Fitting

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Wide field images taken in several photometric bands allow simultaneous measurement of redshifts for thousands of galaxies. title: Domain Adaptation: Learning Bounds and Algorithms
searchable_text:
Domain Adaptation: Learning Bounds and Algorithms Domain adaptation where the labeled training-sample distribution differs from the test-data distribution. Introduce discrepancy distance for arbitrary-loss domain adaptation, estimate it via Rademacher bounds, derive adaptation/generalization bounds, and minimize empirical discrepancy with algorithms for several loss functions. Extends Ben-David et al. (2007) by introducing discrepancy distance tailored to arbitrary loss functions and deriving new bounds and algorithms around empirical discrepancy. TITLE: Domain Adaptation: Learning Bounds and Algorithms

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

Building on previous work by Ben-David et al. (2007), we introduce a novel distance between distributions, discrepancy distance, that is tailored to adaptation problems with arbitrary loss functions. We give Rademacher complexity bounds for estimating the discrepancy distance from fini

title: Doubly Robust Off-policy Value Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Doubly Robust Off-policy Value Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning Estimating the value of a new reinforcement-learning policy from data collected by a different policy. Extend the doubly robust estimator from bandits to sequential reinforcement-learning off-policy value evaluation. Changed the closest predecessor by extending doubly robust bandit estimation to sequential decision-making problems. TITLE: Doubly Robust Off-policy Value Evaluation for Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the problem of off-policy value evaluation in reinforcement learning (RL), where one aims to estimate the value of a new policy based on data collected by a different policy. This problem is often a critical step when applying RL in real-world problems. Despite its importance, existing general methods either have uncontrolled bias or suffer high variance. In this work, we extend the doubly robust estimator for bandits to sequential decision-making problems,

title: Doubly Robust Policy Evaluation and Optimization
searchable_text:
Doubly Robust Policy Evaluation and Optimization Evaluating and optimizing new contextual bandit policies from historic data of contexts, actions, and received rewards when action proportions differ from the new policy. Apply doubly robust estimation combining reward-model and past-policy-model information for contextual bandit policy evaluation and optimization. Instead of relying solely on reward models or past-policy models, it combines both to reduce the bias-variance weaknesses of prior approaches. TITLE: Doubly Robust Policy Evaluation and Optimization

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study sequential decision making in environments where rewards are only partially observed, but can be modeled as a function of observed contexts and the chosen action by the decision maker. This setting, known as contextual bandits, encompasses a wide variety of applications such as health care, content recommendation and Internet advertising. title: Downlink Training Techniques for FDD Massive MIMO Systems: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Training With Memory
searchable_text:
Downlink Training Techniques for FDD Massive MIMO Systems: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Training With Memory Reducing downlink training and CSI feedback overhead for FDD massive MIMO systems with many base-station transmit antennas. Shared preset training signals enable open-loop round-robin transmission with memory-based channel estimation, or closed-loop user feedback selecting future training signals via prediction. Replaces currently standardized high-overhead FDD massive-MIMO training and CSI feedback with practical open-loop and closed-loop training frameworks using memory and small feedback. TITLE: Downlink Training Techniques for FDD Massive MIMO Systems: Open-Loop and Closed-Loop Training With Memory

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

Most literature on massive MIMO systems assumes time division duplexing (TDD), although frequency division duplexing (FDD) dominates current cellular systems. Due to the large num

title: DR2-Net: Deep Residual Reconstruction Network for Image Compressive Sensing
searchable_text:
DR2-Net: Deep Residual Reconstruction Network for Image Compressive Sensing Image compressive sensing reconstruction from CS measurements. A fully connected linear mapping network reconstructs a preliminary image, then residual learning blocks enhance reconstruction quality. Expands a linear mapping reconstruction network by adding several residual learning blocks. TITLE: DR2-Net: Deep Residual Reconstruction Network for Image Compressive Sensing

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract Most traditional algorithms for compressive sensing image reconstruction suffer from the intensive computation. title: Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation: Representing Model Uncertainty in Deep Learning
searchable_text:
Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation: Representing Model Uncertainty in Deep Learning Representing model uncertainty for deep-learning regression and classification, including MNIST and deep reinforcement learning settings. Cast dropout training in deep neural networks as approximate Bayesian inference in deep Gaussian processes, using dropout at prediction time to extract model uncertainty. Instead of treating dropout only as regularization, it reinterprets dropout neural networks as Bayesian approximations that retain uncertainty information previously discarded. TITLE: Dropout as a Bayesian Approximation: Representing Model Uncertainty in Deep Learning

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

In comparison, Bayesian models offer a mathematically grounded framework to reason about model uncertainty, but usually come with a prohibitive computational cost. In this paper we develop a new theoretical framework casting dropout training in deep neural networks (NNs) as approximate

title: Dynamic Evaluation of Neural Sequence Models
searchable_text:
Dynamic Evaluation of Neural Sequence Models Improving neural sequence modeling performance on word-level and character-level language modeling benchmarks. Adapt neural sequence models to recent history using a gradient-descent mechanism that increases probability assigned to recurring sequential patterns. Compared with existing adaptation approaches, it applies gradient-descent-based dynamic adaptation to recent sequence history. TITLE: Dynamic Evaluation of Neural Sequence Models

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present methodology for using dynamic evaluation to improve neural sequence models. Models are adapted to recent history via a gradient descent based mechanism, causing them to assign higher probabilities to re-occurring sequential patterns. Dynamic evaluation outperforms existing adaptation approaches in our comparisons. title: Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks
searchable_text:
Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks Classification on structured graph data whose vertices or edges change over time, including vertex-based semi-supervised and graph-based supervised settings. Combine Long Short-Term Memory networks with Graph Convolutional Networks to jointly learn temporal dependencies and changing graph structure. Extends neural graph modeling from static graph structures to dynamic graphs by integrating recurrent temporal modeling with graph convolution. TITLE: Dynamic Graph Convolutional Networks

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

title: Dynamic Mechanisms of Neocortical Focal Seizure Onset
searchable_text:
Dynamic Mechanisms of Neocortical Focal Seizure Onset Human neocortical focal seizure initiation and propagation mechanisms across spatial scales. Multiscale mathematical model of cortical electric activity with realistic mesoscopic connectivity, using simulations to classify seizure-onset dynamics. YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Recent experimental and clinical studies have provided diverse insight into the mechanisms of human focal seizure initiation and propagation. Often these findings exist at different scales of observation, and are not reconciled into a common understanding. Here we develop a new, multiscale mathematical model of cortical electric activity with realistic mesoscopic connectivity. title: Dynamic Routing Between Capsules
searchable_text:
Dynamic Routing Between Capsules Recognition of visual entities and object parts, especially MNIST digits and highly overlapping digits. Capsules output activity vectors; lower-level capsules predict higher-level capsule parameters via transformation matrices and iteratively route outputs by agreement using scalar-product similarity. ABSTRACT:
A capsule is a group of neurons whose activity vector represents the instantiation parameters of a specific type of entity such as an object or an object part. We use the length of the activity vector to represent the probability that the entity exists and its orientation to represent the instantiation parameters. Active capsules at one level make predictions, via transformation matrices, for the instantiation parameters of higher-level capsules. When multiple predictions agree, a higher level capsule

title: Dynamic Thresholding and Pruning for Regret Minimization
searchable_text:
Dynamic Thresholding and Pruning for Regret Minimization Regret minimization for large imperfect-information games and online learning where full decision-tree traversal makes iterations slow. Set a per-iteration probability threshold; actions below it are forced to zero probability, enabling pruning during regret-minimization iterations. Unlike CFR regret-matching pruning, dynamic thresholding extends pruning to algorithms such as Hedge by zeroing sufficiently low-probability actions. TITLE: Dynamic Thresholding and Pruning for Regret Minimization

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 Regret minimization is widely used in determining strategies for imperfect-information games and in online learning. In large games, computing the regrets associated with a single iteration can be slow. For this reason, pruning — in which parts of the decision tree are not traversed in every iteration — has emerged as an essential method for speeding up iterations in large games. title: Dynamical Synapses Enhance Neural Information Processing: Gracefulness, Accuracy, and Mobility
searchable_text:
Dynamical Synapses Enhance Neural Information Processing: Gracefulness, Accuracy, and Mobility Neural manipulation of temporal information: sensory memory retention, graceful shutdown of persistent activity, noisy population decoding, and tracking time-varying stimuli. Incorporate short-term depression and facilitation into continuous attractor neural networks to shape plateau decay, facilitated interaction memory, and state mobility. Moves beyond fast signaling and rapid learning by exploiting short-term synaptic plasticity timescales to control continuous attractor network dynamics. TITLE: Dynamical Synapses Enhance Neural Information Processing: Gracefulness, Accuracy, and Mobility

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Experimental data have revealed that neuronal connection efficacy exhibits two forms of short-term plasticity: short-term depression (STD) and short-term facilitation (STF). They have time constants residing between fast neural signaling and rapid learning and may serve as substrates for neural systems manipulating temporal information on relevant timescales. title: E pur si muove: Galilean-invariant cosmological hydrodynamical simulations on a moving mesh
searchable_text:
E pur si muove: Galilean-invariant cosmological hydrodynamical simulations on a moving mesh Cosmological hydrodynamical simulations of structure growth with shocks, contact discontinuities, self-gravity, collisionless dark matter, and highly supersonic bulk flows. Finite-volume ideal hydrodynamics on a Voronoi moving unstructured mesh, using a second-order unsplit Godunov scheme with exact Riemann solver; mesh-generating points may follow local flow for Lagrangian, Galilean-invariant evolution. Replaces SPH or stationary Cartesian AMR with a Voronoi mesh whose generating points move with the fluid, combining Lagrangian adaptivity with finite-volume Godunov shock-capturing. TITLE: E pur si muove: Galilean-invariant cosmological hydrodynamical simulations on a moving mesh

YEAR: 2009

ABSTRACT:
Hydrodynamic cosmological simulations at present usually employ either the Lagrangian smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH) technique or Eulerian hydrodynamics on a Cartesian mesh with (optional) adaptive mesh refinement (AMR). Both of these methods have disadvantages that negatively impact their accuracy in certain situations, for example the suppression of fluid instabilities in the case of SPH, and the lack of Galilean invariance and the pre

title: Easy-First Dependency Parsing with Hierarchical Tree LSTMs
searchable_text:
Easy-First Dependency Parsing with Hierarchical Tree LSTMs English and Chinese dependency parsing. A compositional parse-tree vector representation built through recursive combination of recurrent-neural network encoders, used as the backbone of a greedy bottom-up dependency parser. Introduces hierarchical tree LSTM-style compositional representations for parse trees within an easy-first greedy bottom-up dependency parser. TITLE: Easy-First Dependency Parsing with Hierarchical Tree LSTMs

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We suggest a compositional vector representation of parse trees that relies on a recursive combination of recurrent-neural network encoders. To demonstrate its effectiveness, we use the representation as the backbone of a greedy, bottom-up dependency parser, achieving very strong accuracies for English and Chinese, without relying on external word embeddings. The parser’s implementation is available for download at the first author’s webpage.

title: Effect of eccentricity on binary neutron star searches in advanced LIGO
searchable_text:
Effect of eccentricity on binary neutron star searches in advanced LIGO Advanced LIGO/Virgo detection of binary neutron star gravitational waves from potentially eccentric dynamical-formation systems. Moves beyond searches assuming field binaries circularized before 15 Hz by assessing non-negligible eccentricity from n-body formation channels. TITLE: Effect of eccentricity on binary neutron star searches in advanced LIGO

ABSTRACT:
Binary neutron stars (BNSs) are the primary source of gravitational waves for the Laser Interferometer Gravitational-Wave Observatory (LIGO) and its international partners Virgo and KAGRA. Current BNS searches target field binaries whose orbits will have circularized by radiation reaction before their gravitational waves enter the advanced LIGO sensitive band at 15 Hz. It has been suggested that a population of BNSs may form by n-body interactions near supermassiv

title: Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs
searchable_text:
Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs Approximate K-nearest neighbor search in general metric spaces and vector search settings. Incrementally build a multi-layer hierarchy of proximity graphs, assigning each element a random maximum layer with exponentially decaying probability, then search from upper layers using scale-separated links and neighbor-selection heuristics. Compared with NSW, HNSW adds a controllable hierarchy that separates links by characteristic distance scales and removes the need for additional coarse-stage search structures. TITLE: Efficient and Robust Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search Using Hierarchical Navigable Small World Graphs

YEAR: 2016

ABSTRACT:
We present a new approach for the approximate K-nearest neighbor search based on navigable small world graphs with controllable hierarchy (Hierarchical NSW, HNSW). The proposed solution is fully graph-based, without any need for additional search structures (typically used at the coarse search stage of the most proximity graph techniques). title: Efficient Entity Resolution on Heterogeneous Records
searchable_text:
Efficient Entity Resolution on Heterogeneous Records Entity resolution for raw records stored in heterogeneous environments where record schemas may differ. Design a heterogeneous-record similarity function plus an iterative ER framework, using indexing to generate candidates and accelerate similarity computation. Moves beyond assuming schema matching and data exchange into a predefined schema, resolving entities directly over heterogeneous records with a new similarity function and framework. TITLE: Efficient Entity Resolution on Heterogeneous Records

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Entity resolution (ER) is the problem of identifying and merging records that refer to the same real-world entity. In many scenarios, raw records are stored under heterogeneous environment. Specifically, the schemas of records may differ from each other. To leverage such records better, most existing work assume that schema matching and data exchange have been done to convert records under different schemas to those under a predefined schema. title: Efficient Monte Carlo Integration Using Boosted Decision Trees and Generative Deep Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Efficient Monte Carlo Integration Using Boosted Decision Trees and Generative Deep Neural Networks Monte Carlo integration for non-factorizable integrands and complex collider physics matrix element generation. Monte Carlo integration using generative Boosted Decision Trees and Deep Neural Networks to learn improved sampling strategies. ABSTRACT:
New machine learning based algorithms have been developed and tested for Monte Carlo integration based on generative Boosted Decision Trees and Deep Neural Networks. title: Efficient Nonparametric Conformal Prediction Regions

ABSTRACT:

Approximations for simplifying implementation and data driven bandwidth selection method

title: Efficient Variational Quantum Simulator Incorporating Active Error Minimization
searchable_text:
Efficient Variational Quantum Simulator Incorporating Active Error Minimization Simulation of quantum systems arising in chemistry, materials science, and related discovery tasks on near-future, modest-size, imperfectly controlled quantum processors. A variational quantum simulation method using closely integrated classical and quantum coprocessors, with active error minimization by artificially boosting errors and extrapolating to zero error. Replaces conventional optimized Trotterisation with a variational classical-quantum coprocessor protocol incorporating active zero-error extrapolation. ABSTRACT:

title: Eigenpath traversal by phase randomization

Eigenpath traversal by phase randomization Adiabatic quantum computing paths of nondegenerate eigenstates, including discrete-time unitary families, unstructured search, quantum sampling, and quantum simulated annealing. A computation in adiabatic quantum computing is implemented by traversing a path of nondegenerate eigenstates of a continuous family of Hamiltonians. We introduce a method that traverses a discretized form of the path: At each step we apply the instantaneous Hamiltonian for a random time. The resulting decoherence approximates a projective measurement onto the desired eigenstate, achieving a version of the quantum Zeno effect. title: Elaboration in Dependent Type Theory

Elaboration in Dependent Type Theory Making interactive theorem provers usable by turning quasi-formal, partially specified mathematical expressions, definitions, and proofs into completely precise formal terms. title: Elucidating reaction mechanisms on quantum computers
searchable_text:
Elucidating reaction mechanisms on quantum computers Elucidating reaction mechanisms for complex chemical systems, especially biological nitrogen fixation in nitrogenase. Augment classical reaction-mechanism simulations with quantum-computer estimates of relative and activation energies, while accounting for error-correction and gate-synthesis overheads in modular parallel architectures. title: Embed to Control: A Locally Linear Latent Dynamics Model for Control from Raw Images
searchable_text:
Embed to Control: A Locally Linear Latent Dynamics Model for Control from Raw Images Model learning and control of non-linear dynamical systems from raw pixel images. title: emcee: The MCMC Hammer

The algorithm behind emcee has several advantages over traditional MCMC sampling methods and it has excellent performance as measured by the autocorrelation time (or function calls per independent sample). title: Emergence of Grounded Compositional Language in Multi-Agent Populations
searchable_text:
Emergence of Grounded Compositional Language in Multi-Agent Populations Grounded compositional language emergence for achieving goals in multi-agent populations, enabling more intelligent interaction beyond corpus statistical pattern capture. A multi-agent learning environment and learning methods induce goal-oriented agents to communicate via temporal streams of abstract discrete symbols. Shifts from corpus-based statistical NLP toward grounded, goal-driven language emergence in interacting multi-agent populations. TITLE: Emergence of Grounded Compositional Language in Multi-Agent Populations

title: Emergence of Language with Multi-agent Games: Learning to Communicate with Sequences of Symbols
searchable_text:
Emergence of Language with Multi-agent Games: Learning to Communicate with Sequences of Symbols Emergent communication for multi-agent referential games where agents must exchange language-like sequences of discrete symbols to succeed. title: Emphatic TD Bellman Operator is a Contraction

Emphatic TD Bellman Operator is a Contraction Off-policy evaluation in Markov decision processes under general target and behavior policies. Prove the ETD projected fixed-point Bellman operator is a contraction with sqrt-gamma modulus, enabling approximation-error bounds. title: End-to-End Driving Via Conditional Imitation Learning
searchable_text:
End-to-End Driving Via Conditional Imitation Learning Vision-based autonomous driving in urban and intersection settings where an imitation-learned vehicle must follow roads, avoid obstacles, and obey route-level navigation commands. Condition imitation learning policies on high-level navigational command inputs so an end-to-end vision-based driving network handles sensorimotor control while selecting maneuvers such as turns at test time. Adds high-level command conditioning to end-to-end imitation learning, changing a passive expert imitator into a command-responsive chauffeur. ABSTRACT:
Deep networks trained on demonstrations of human driving have learned to follow roads and avoid obstacles. However, driving policies trained via imitation learning cannot be controlled at test time. A vehicle trained end-to-end to imitate an expert cannot be guided to take a specific turn at an upcoming intersection. This limits the utility of such systems. We propose to condition imitation learning on high-level command input. At test time, the learned driving policy functions as

title: End to End Learning for Self-Driving Cars
searchable_text:
End to End Learning for Self-Driving Cars Autonomous road driving from camera input, including local roads, highways, parking lots, unpaved roads, and conditions with or without lane markings. A CNN maps raw pixels from a single front-facing camera directly to steering commands, trained end-to-end using human steering angle supervision. Replaced explicit decomposition into lane detection, path planning, and control with a single end-to-end system optimizing all processing steps simultaneously. TITLE: End to End Learning for Self-Driving Cars

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We trained a convolutional neural network (CNN) to map raw pixels from a single front-facing camera directly to steering commands. This end-to-end approach proved surprisingly powerful. With minimum training data from humans the system learns to drive in traffic on local roads with or without lane markings and on highways. It also operates in areas with unclear visual guidance such as in parking lots and on unpaved roads. 
The system automatically learns internal representations of the necessa

title: Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information
searchable_text:
Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information Learning continuous word representations for NLP, especially in morphologically rich languages, large vocabularies, rare words, and unseen words. Extend skip-gram by representing each word as a bag of character n-grams, associating vectors to n-grams and summing them into word representations. Changed from one distinct vector per word to subword-based word vectors built from character n-gram representations. TITLE: Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Continuous word representations, trained on large unlabeled corpora are useful for many natural language processing tasks. Popular models that learn such representations ignore the morphology of words, by assigning a distinct vector to each word. This is a limitation, especially for languages with large vocabularies and many rare words. In this paper, we propose a new approach based on the skipgram model, where each word is represented as a bag of character n-grams. A vector representati

title: Ensemble Adversarial Training: Attacks and Defenses
searchable_text:
Ensemble Adversarial Training: Attacks and Defenses Defending large-scale image classifiers, especially ImageNet models, against adversarial examples and black-box transferred attacks. Augment adversarial training data with perturbations transferred from other models, creating ensemble adversarial training for robustness against black-box attacks. Instead of crafting adversarial examples only on the defended model, training is augmented with perturbations transferred from other models. TITLE: Ensemble Adversarial Training: Attacks and Defenses

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Adversarial examples are perturbed inputs designed to fool machine learning models. Adversarial training injects such examples into training data to increase robustness. To scale this technique to large datasets, perturbations are crafted using fast single-step methods that maximize a linear approximation of the model's loss. We show that this form of adversarial training converges to a degenerate global minimum, wherein small curvature artifacts near the data points obfuscate a line

title: Entanglement in the stabilizer formalism
searchable_text:
Entanglement in the stabilizer formalism Multipartite entanglement quantification for stabilizer states across qubits, qudits, and continuous variables. Define a multipartite entanglement measure for stabilizer states, computable efficiently from stabilizer group generators.  TITLE: Entanglement in the stabilizer formalism

YEAR: 2004

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We define a multi-partite entanglement measure for stabilizer states, which can be computed efficiently from a set of generators of the stabilizer group. Our measure applies to qubits, qudits and continuous variables.

title: EPOpt: Learning Robust Neural Network Policies Using Model Ensembles
searchable_text:
EPOpt: Learning Robust Neural Network Policies Using Model Ensembles Robust neural-network policy learning for real-world reinforcement-learning tasks where simulated training domains differ from the target domain. Train reinforcement-learning policies on an ensemble of simulated source domains using adversarial training, with optional Bayesian adaptation of the source-domain distribution from target-domain data. Moves beyond a single simulated source domain by using a source-domain ensemble plus adversarial robust training and adapting the ensemble distribution with target-domain data. TITLE: EPOpt: Learning Robust Neural Network Policies Using Model Ensembles

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Sample complexity and safety are major challenges when learning policies with reinforcement learning for real-world tasks, especially when the policies are represented using rich function approximators like deep neural networks. Model-based methods where the real-world target domain is approximated using a simulated source domain provide an avenue to tackle the above challenges by augmenting real data with simulated data. However, discrepancies between the simulated

title: Error Mitigation for Short-Depth Quantum Circuits.
searchable_text:
Error Mitigation for Short-Depth Quantum Circuits. Accurate expectation-value estimation for near-term short-depth quantum circuits affected by decoherence and gate errors. Mitigate short-depth circuit errors using zero-noise extrapolation via Richardson's deferred limit and quasiprobability resampling of randomized circuits, without extra qubits. Introduces deliberately simple error-mitigation schemes that avoid additional qubit resources for current experiments. TITLE: Error Mitigation for Short-Depth Quantum Circuits.

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Two schemes are presented that mitigate the effect of errors and decoherence in short-depth quantum circuits. The size of the circuits for which these techniques can be applied is limited by the rate at which the errors in the computation are introduced. Near-term applications of early quantum devices, such as quantum simulations, rely on accurate estimates of expectation values to become relevant. Decoherence and gate errors lead to wrong estimates of the expectation values of observ

title: Estimating high-dimensional intervention effects from observational data
searchable_text:
Estimating high-dimensional intervention effects from observational data High-dimensional estimation of covariate causal effects on a response from observational data generated by an unknown DAG model. Estimate a DAG equivalence class; apply intervention calculus across candidate DAGs; use local graph information to consistently estimate distinct possible causal effects and summarize variable importance. Combines consistent DAG-equivalence-class estimation with intervention-calculus effect estimation across each DAG, while using a local graph algorithm instead of relying on global graph information. TITLE: Estimating high-dimensional intervention effects from observational data

YEAR: 2008

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We assume that we have observational data generated from an unknown underlying directed acyclic graph (DAG) model. A DAG is typically not identifiable from observational data, but it is possible to consistently estimate the equivalence class of a DAG. Moreover, for any given DAG, causal effects can be estimated using intervention calculus. In this paper, we combine these two parts. For each DAG in the estimated equivalence class, we use intervention calculus to e

title: Estimating matching affinity matrices under low-rank constraints
searchable_text:
Estimating matching affinity matrices under low-rank constraints Inverse estimation of transport surplus or matching affinity in high-dimensional optimal transport problems from observed joint distributions. Estimate matching affinity from observed joint distributions using entropic regularization plus nuclear norm regularization to enforce a low-rank affinity matrix. Moves from classical optimal transport, which assumes known matching affinity, to estimating matching affinity from observed joint distributions under low-rank constraints. TITLE: Estimating matching affinity matrices under low-rank constraints

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we address the problem of estimating transport surplus (a.k.a. matching affinity) in high-dimensional optimal transport problems. Classical optimal transport theory specifies the matching affinity and determines the optimal joint distribution. In contrast, we study the inverse problem of estimating matching affinity based on the observation of the joint distribution, using an entropic regularization of the problem. To accommodate high dimensionality of the

title: Estimating or Propagating Gradients Through Stochastic Neurons for Conditional Computation
searchable_text:
Estimating or Propagating Gradients Through Stochastic Neurons for Conditional Computation Estimating gradients through stochastic or non-smooth neural units, especially sparse stochastic gating units for conditional computation in deep networks. Compare four gradient-propagation families for stochastic/hard neurons: REINFORCE-style unbiased estimators, a decomposed stochastic-plus-smooth binary neuron, differentiable noise injection, and a straight-through gradient-copy heuristic. Beyond existing REINFORCE estimators, the paper introduces a decomposed stochastic binary neuron with a smooth differentiable part and names the straight-through estimator heuristic. TITLE: Estimating or Propagating Gradients Through Stochastic Neurons for Conditional Computation

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
