Stochastic neurons and hard non-linearities can be useful for a number of reasons in deep learning models, but in many cases they pose a challenging problem: how to estimate the gradient of a loss function with respect to the input of such stochastic or non-smooth neurons? I.e., can we "back-propagate" through these stochastic neurons? We examine this question, existing approaches, and compare four families of solutions, applicable in different

title: Estimation of brain network ictogenicity predicts outcome from epilepsy surgery
searchable_text:
Estimation of brain network ictogenicity predicts outcome from epilepsy surgery Predicting optimal surgical resection strategies and prognoses for patients undergoing surgery for pharmacologically intractable epilepsy. An in silico, model-based framework estimates ictogenic brain-network effects of candidate epilepsy surgeries. Shifted from conventional lesion or epileptiform-rhythm-based resection guidance to quantitative model predictions of optimal strategies within ictogenic networks. TITLE: Estimation of brain network ictogenicity predicts outcome from epilepsy surgery

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Surgery is a valuable option for pharmacologically intractable epilepsy. However, significant post-operative improvements are not always attained. This is due in part to our incomplete understanding of the seizure generating (ictogenic) capabilities of brain networks. Here we introduce an in silico, model-based framework to study the effects of surgery within ictogenic brain networks. We find that factors conventionally determining the region of tissue to resect, su

title: Estimation of synthetic accessibility score of drug-like molecules based on molecular complexity and fragment contributions
searchable_text:
Estimation of synthetic accessibility score of drug-like molecules based on molecular complexity and fragment contributions Estimating and ranking the synthetic accessibility of drug-like molecules for drug discovery workflows. Compute SAscore from fragment contributions learned from PubChem molecules plus penalties for structural complexity such as rings, stereocomplexity, and molecule size. Introduced a synthetic-accessibility score combining historical fragment-frequency knowledge from PubChem with explicit molecular-complexity penalties. TITLE: Estimation of synthetic accessibility score of drug-like molecules based on molecular complexity and fragment contributions

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
BackgroundA method to estimate ease of synthesis (synthetic accessibility) of drug-like molecules is needed in many areas of the drug discovery process. The development and validation of such a method that is able to characterize molecule synthetic accessibility as a score between 1 (easy to make) and 10 (very difficult to make) is described in this article.ResultsThe method for estimation of the synthetic access

title: EuroSAT: A Novel Dataset and Deep Learning Benchmark for Land Use and Land Cover Classification
searchable_text:
EuroSAT: A Novel Dataset and Deep Learning Benchmark for Land Use and Land Cover Classification Land use and land cover classification from openly accessible Sentinel-2 satellite imagery. Patch-based land use and land cover classification using Sentinel-2 images and deep convolutional neural network benchmarks over 13 spectral bands. Introduces a publicly available geo-referenced Sentinel-2 dataset with 13 spectral bands, ten classes, and 27,000 labeled images for benchmarking. TITLE: EuroSAT: A Novel Dataset and Deep Learning Benchmark for Land Use and Land Cover Classification

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we present a patch-based land use and land cover classification approach using Sentinel-2 satellite images. The Sentinel-2 satellite images are openly and freely accessible, and are provided in the earth observation program Copernicus. We present a novel dataset, based on these images that covers 13 spectral bands and is comprised of ten classes with a total of 27 000 labeled and geo-referenced images. Benchmarks are provided

title: Event generator tuning using Bayesian optimization
searchable_text:
Event generator tuning using Bayesian optimization Tuning many parameters of Monte Carlo event generators against experimental data when brute-force grid searches are computationally prohibitive. Bayesian optimization for black-box tuning of Monte Carlo event generator parameters, comparing generator output with experimental data while reducing expensive event-generation evaluations. Replaces simple brute-force grid-based parameter tuning with Bayesian optimization for CPU-intensive event generator tuning. TITLE: Event generator tuning using Bayesian optimization

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Monte Carlo event generators contain a large number of parameters that must be determined by comparing the output of the generator with experimental data. Generating enough events with a fixed set of parameter values to enable making such a comparison is extremely CPU intensive, which prohibits performing a simple brute-force grid-based tuning of the parameters. Bayesian optimization is a powerful method designed for such black-box tuning applications. In this article, we show that Mo

title: Evidence Aggregation for Answer Re-Ranking in Open-Domain Question Answering
searchable_text:
Evidence Aggregation for Answer Re-Ranking in Open-Domain Question Answering Open-domain question answering where answers may require evidence from multiple question-related passages. Answer-candidate re-ranking using aggregated evidence from multiple passages, via strength-based and coverage-based re-ranking. Changed from independently extracting answers from single passages to reordering existing QA answer candidates using evidence aggregated from different passages. TITLE: Evidence Aggregation for Answer Re-Ranking in Open-Domain Question Answering

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A popular recent approach to answering open-domain questions is to first search for question-related passages and then apply reading comprehension models to extract answers. Existing methods usually extract answers from single passages independently. But some questions require a combination of evidence from across different sources to answer correctly. In this paper, we propose two models which make use of multiple passages to generate their answers. Both use

title: Evidence for nine planets in the HD 10180 system
searchable_text:
Evidence for nine planets in the HD 10180 system Determining how many periodic exoplanet signals are present in HD 10180 HARPS radial-velocity data. Bayesian re-analysis of HARPS radial velocities using posterior sampling, model probabilities, priors, and comparisons of models with up to nine periodicities. Moves beyond the previously reported six, possibly seven, planets by testing models up to nine periodicities and adding two significant candidate signals. TITLE: Evidence for nine planets in the HD 10180 system

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Aims. We re-analyse the HARPS radial velocities of HD10180 and calculate the probabilities of models with differing numbers of periodic signals in the data. We test the significance of the seven signals, corresponding to seven exoplanets orbiting the star, in the Bayesian framework and perform comparisons of models with up to nine periodicities. Methods. We used posterior samplings and Bayesian model probabilities in our analyses together with suitable prior probability densities and pr

title: Evidence for single top-quark production in the $s$-channel in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}=$8 TeV with the ATLAS detector using the Matrix Element Method
searchable_text:
Evidence for single top-quark production in the $s$-channel in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}=$8 TeV with the ATLAS detector using the Matrix Element Method Evidence for single top-quark production in the s-channel in 8 TeV proton-proton collisions with exactly two b-tagged jets. Maximum-likelihood fit of a matrix-element-method discriminant optimized to separate s-channel single-top signal from top-pair and W+heavy-flavour backgrounds.  TITLE: Evidence for single top-quark production in the $s$-channel in proton-proton collisions at $\sqrt{s}=$8 TeV with the ATLAS detector using the Matrix Element Method

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This Letter presents evidence for single top-quark production in the $s$-channel using proton-proton collisions at a centre-of-mass energy of 8 TeV with the ATLAS detector at the CERN Large Hadron Collider. The analysis is performed on events containing one isolated electron or muon, large missing transverse momentum and exactly two $b$-tagged jets in the final state. The a

title: Evolution of entanglement entropy in one-dimensional systems
searchable_text:
Evolution of entanglement entropy in one-dimensional systems Entanglement entropy between an interval in a one-dimensional system and its complement after starting from a pure state that is not a Hamiltonian eigenstate. Path-integral quantum field theory plus explicit transverse Ising spin-chain computations analyze unitary entanglement-entropy evolution under finite signal propagation.  TITLE: Evolution of entanglement entropy in one-dimensional systems

YEAR: 2005

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the unitary time evolution of the entropy of entanglement of a one-dimensional system between the degrees of freedom in an interval of length and its complement, starting from a pure state which is not an eigenstate of the Hamiltonian. We use path integral methods of quantum field theory as well as explicit computations for the transverse Ising spin chain. In both cases, there is a maximum speed v of propagation of signals. In general the entanglement entropy increa

title: Evolving Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Image Classification
searchable_text:
Evolving Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Image Classification Image classification with deep convolutional neural networks. A genetic algorithm evolves CNN architectures and connection-weight initialization using variable-length gene encoding, weight-initialization representation, and faster fitness evaluation. Adds variable-length CNN block/depth encoding, a representation for evolving weight initializations, and a fitness evaluation method requiring substantially less computational resource. TITLE: Evolving Deep Convolutional Neural Networks for Image Classification

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Evolutionary paradigms have been successfully applied to neural network designs for two decades. Unfortunately, these methods cannot scale well to the modern deep neural networks due to the complicated architectures and large quantities of connection weights. In this paper, we propose a new method using genetic algorithms for evolving the architectures and connection weight initialization values of a deep convolutional neural network to address image classification p

title: Evolving Deep Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Evolving Deep Neural Networks Automated architecture search for deep learning tasks, including object recognition, language modeling, and image captioning. CoDeepNEAT evolves deep learning architectures by extending neuroevolution to topology, components, and hyperparameters. It extends existing neuroevolution beyond topology to include components and hyperparameters for deep network architecture optimization. TITLE: Evolving Deep Neural Networks

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract The success of deep learning depends on finding an architecture to fit the task. As deep learning has scaled up to more challenging tasks, the architectures have become difficult to design by hand. This paper proposes an automated method, CoDeepNEAT, for optimizing deep learning architectures through evolution. By extending existing neuroevolution methods to topology, components, and hyperparameters, this method achieves results comparable to best human designs in standard benchmarks in object re

title: EXACT AND ASYMPTOTICALLY ROBUST PERMUTATION TESTS
searchable_text:
EXACT AND ASYMPTOTICALLY ROBUST PERMUTATION TESTS Two-sample and k-sample hypothesis testing for equality of distributional parameters such as means or medians when underlying distributions may differ. A general permutation-test procedure using coupling and contiguity arguments to preserve finite-sample exactness under identical distributions and asymptotic validity under parameter-equality nulls. Extends classical exact permutation tests from the null P=Q to tests that remain exact under identical distributions and asymptotically level under equality of parameters. TITLE: EXACT AND ASYMPTOTICALLY ROBUST PERMUTATION TESTS

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Given independent samples from P and Q, two-sample permutation tests allow one to construct exact level tests when the null hypothesis is P=Q. On the other hand, when comparing or testing particular parameters $\theta$ of P and Q, such as their means or medians, permutation tests need not be level $\alpha$, or even approximately level $\alpha$ in large samples. Under very weak assumptions for comparing estimators, we provide a general test procedure whereby the asymptotic validity of t

title: EXOPLANET POPULATION INFERENCE AND THE ABUNDANCE OF EARTH ANALOGS FROM NOISY, INCOMPLETE CATALOGS
searchable_text:
EXOPLANET POPULATION INFERENCE AND THE ABUNDANCE OF EARTH ANALOGS FROM NOISY, INCOMPLETE CATALOGS Estimating exoplanet occurrence rates, especially Earth analog abundance around Sun-like/G dwarf stars, from noisy, incomplete, selection-censored catalogs. Hierarchical probabilistic population inference incorporating survey completeness, observational uncertainties, and a Gaussian-process smooth occurrence-rate density over period and radius. Moves beyond inverse-detection-efficiency weighting and stronger distributional assumptions by modeling observational uncertainties and survey completeness in a hierarchical probabilistic Gaussian-process framework. TITLE: EXOPLANET POPULATION INFERENCE AND THE ABUNDANCE OF EARTH ANALOGS FROM NOISY, INCOMPLETE CATALOGS

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
No true extrasolar Earth analog is known. Hundreds of planets have been found around Sun-like stars that are either Earth-sized but on shorter periods, or else on year-long orbits but somewhat larger. Under strong assumptions, exoplanet catalogs have been used to make an extrapolated estimate of the rate at which Sun-like stars host Earth analogs. These studies are complicated by the fact that every catalog is censored by non-trivial sele

title: Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples
searchable_text:
Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples Neural network vulnerability to adversarial examples: small worst-case input perturbations causing high-confidence misclassification. Use the linear-nature explanation to derive a simple, fast method for generating adversarial examples, then use those examples in adversarial training. Shifted explanation from nonlinearity and overfitting to neural networks' linear nature as the primary cause of adversarial vulnerability. TITLE: Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Several machine learning models, including neural networks, consistently misclassify adversarial examples---inputs formed by applying small but intentionally worst-case perturbations to examples from the dataset, such that the perturbed input results in the model outputting an incorrect answer with high confidence. Early attempts at explaining this phenomenon focused on nonlinearity and overfitting. We argue instead that the primary cause of neural networks' vulnerability to adversarial p

title: Explaining How a Deep Neural Network Trained with End-to-End Learning Steers a Car
searchable_text:
Explaining How a Deep Neural Network Trained with End-to-End Learning Steers a Car Explaining steering decisions for autonomous lane keeping by PilotNet from front-facing road images across varied driving conditions. An end-to-end neural driving system maps road images to steering angles and uses an attribution method to identify image elements influencing each steering decision. Shifted from demonstrating end-to-end lane keeping to interpreting PilotNet by determining which road-image elements most influence steering decisions. TITLE: Explaining How a Deep Neural Network Trained with End-to-End Learning Steers a Car

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
As part of a complete software stack for autonomous driving, NVIDIA has created a neural-network-based system, known as PilotNet, which outputs steering angles given images of the road ahead. PilotNet is trained using road images paired with the steering angles generated by a human driving a data-collection car. It derives the necessary domain knowledge by observing human drivers. This eliminates the need for human engineers to anticipate what is import

title: Exploring Generalization in Deep Learning
searchable_text:
Exploring Generalization in Deep Learning Understanding what drives generalization in deep networks. Analyze norm-based control, sharpness, and robustness as generalization measures, emphasizing scale normalization and connecting sharpness to PAC-Bayes theory. Moved beyond proposing individual explanations by comparing several measures and examining how well they explain observed phenomena. TITLE: Exploring Generalization in Deep Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
With a goal of understanding what drives generalization in deep networks, we consider several recently suggested explanations, including norm-based control, sharpness and robustness. We study how these measures can ensure generalization, highlighting the importance of scale normalization, and making a connection between sharpness and PAC-Bayes theory. We then investigate how well the measures explain different observed phenomena.

title: Exponential algorithmic speedup by a quantum walk
searchable_text:
Exponential algorithmic speedup by a quantum walk A black box graph traversal problem requiring traversal of a graph. Continuous-time quantum walk implemented efficiently in a black-box graph setting to traverse the graph rapidly. Replaces previous quantum Fourier transform-based algorithmic techniques with continuous-time quantum walks. TITLE: Exponential algorithmic speedup by a quantum walk

YEAR: 2002

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We construct a black box graph traversal problem that can be solved exponentially faster on a quantum computer than on a classical computer. The quantum algorithm is based on a continuous time quantum walk, and thus employs a different technique from previous quantum algorithms based on quantum Fourier transforms. We show how to implement the quantum walk efficiently in our black box setting. We then show how this quantum walk solves our problem by rapidly traversing a graph. Finally,

title: Extremal Mechanisms for Local Differential Privacy
searchable_text:
Extremal Mechanisms for Local Differential Privacy Maximizing information-theoretic utility of statistical inferences from locally differentially private released data. Staircase privatization mechanisms reduce optimal local differential privacy utility maximization to selecting an extremal mechanism via a linear program. Introduces staircase mechanisms as an extremal family containing optimal local-privacy mechanisms, replacing ad hoc mechanism choice with an optimization characterization. TITLE: Extremal Mechanisms for Local Differential Privacy

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Local differential privacy has recently surfaced as a strong measure of privacy in contexts where personal information remains private even from data analysts. Working in a setting where the data providers and data analysts want to maximize the utility of statistical inferences performed on the released data, we study the fundamental tradeoff between local differential privacy and information theoretic utility functions. We introduce a family of extremal privatization mechanisms, whic

title: Fairness through awareness
searchable_text:
Fairness through awareness Fair classification decisions, such as university admissions, preventing discrimination against individuals based on group membership while preserving classifier utility. Use a task-specific individual-similarity metric and optimize classifier utility under the constraint that similar individuals receive similar treatment. Moves beyond group-membership discrimination avoidance by introducing individual-level metric fairness and an optimization framework, plus a statistical-parity affirmative-action adaptation. TITLE: Fairness through awareness

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study fairness in classification, where individuals are classified, e.g., admitted to a university, and the goal is to prevent discrimination against individuals based on their membership in some group, while maintaining utility for the classifier (the university). The main conceptual contribution of this paper is a framework for fair classification comprising (1) a (hypothetical) task-specific metric for determining the degree to which individuals are similar with respect to the classification task at ha

title: FALSE POSITIVE PROBABILITIES FOR ALL KEPLER OBJECTS OF INTEREST: 1284 NEWLY VALIDATED PLANETS AND 428 LIKELY FALSE POSITIVES
searchable_text:
FALSE POSITIVE PROBABILITIES FOR ALL KEPLER OBJECTS OF INTEREST: 1284 NEWLY VALIDATED PLANETS AND 428 LIKELY FALSE POSITIVES Validating transiting exoplanet candidates and identifying likely false positives among all Kepler Objects of Interest. Automated astrophysical false positive probability calculations using vespa for Kepler Objects of Interest, with host stars modeled as single, binary, and triple systems. This is the first large-scale demonstration of a fully automated transiting planet validation procedure applied to every KOI. TITLE: FALSE POSITIVE PROBABILITIES FOR ALL KEPLER OBJECTS OF INTEREST: 1284 NEWLY VALIDATED PLANETS AND 428 LIKELY FALSE POSITIVES

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present astrophysical false positive probability calculations for every Kepler Object of Interest (KOI)—the first large-scale demonstration of a fully automated transiting planet validation procedure. Out of 7056 KOIs, we determine that 1935 have probabilities <1% of being astrophysical false positives, and thus may be considered validated planets. Of these, 1284 have not yet been validated or confirmed by ot

title: Fast Computation of Wasserstein Barycenters
searchable_text:
Fast Computation of Wasserstein Barycenters Computing Wasserstein barycenters: means of sets of empirical probability measures under the optimal transport metric. Smooth the Wasserstein barycenter objective with an entropic regularizer, yielding a strictly convex objective whose gradients are computed cheaply via matrix scaling algorithms. Extends Cuturi (2013) from entropically smoothed optimal transport to Wasserstein barycenters, recovering cheap gradient computation through matrix scaling. TITLE: Fast Computation of Wasserstein Barycenters

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present new algorithms to compute the mean of a set of empirical probability measures under the optimal transport metric. This mean, known as the Wasserstein barycenter, is the measure that minimizes the sum of its Wasserstein distances to each element in that set. We propose two original algorithms to compute Wasserstein barycenters that build upon the subgradient method. A direct implementation of these algorithms is, however, too costly because it would require the repeated resolution

title: Fast Exact Conformalization of Lasso using Piecewise Linear Homotopy
searchable_text:
Fast Exact Conformalization of Lasso using Piecewise Linear Homotopy Efficient conformal prediction sets for Lasso and elastic net point predictors in settings where exhaustive search over the output space is computationally costly. Use a piecewise linear homotopy tracking the Lasso solution as one input sample point is perturbed, enabling exact conformalization of Lasso and elastic net. Replaces exhaustive conformal search with an exact, computationally efficient Lasso/elastic-net conformalization based on a novel single-sample perturbation homotopy. TITLE: Fast Exact Conformalization of Lasso using Piecewise Linear Homotopy

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Conformal prediction is a general method that converts almost any point predictor to a prediction set. The resulting set keeps good statistical properties of the original estimator under standard assumptions, and guarantees valid average coverage even when the model is misspecified. A main challenge in applying conformal prediction in modern applications is efficient computation, as it generally requires an exhaustive search over the entire output space. In this pape

title: Fast-forwarding of Hamiltonians and exponentially precise measurements
searchable_text:
Fast-forwarding of Hamiltonians and exponentially precise measurements Rigorous analysis of when known Hamiltonian structure permits time-energy uncertainty violations and exponentially precise energy measurements. Replace elapsed measurement time in TEUR with computational complexity, characterizing violations through fast-forwardable Hamiltonian simulation whose cost is significantly smaller than simulated time. Shifts TEUR from unknown-Hamiltonian elapsed time to a computational-complexity formulation that accounts for prior Hamiltonian knowledge and efficient simulation. TITLE: Fast-forwarding of Hamiltonians and exponentially precise measurements

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The time-energy uncertainty relation (TEUR) $$\Delta {\it{t}}\Delta {\it{E}}\,{\bf{ \ge }}\,{\textstyle{{\bf{1}} \over {\bf{2}}}}$$ΔtΔE≥12 holds if the Hamiltonian is completely unknown, but can be violated otherwise; here we initiate a rigorous study describing when and to what extent such violations can occur. To this end, we propose a computational version of the TEUR (cTEUR), in which Δt is replaced by the computational complexity of simulating the measurement.

title: Fast Learning from Distributed Datasets without Entity Matching
searchable_text:
Fast Learning from Distributed Datasets without Entity Matching Learning a classifier over the cross-product feature space of distributed datasets describing the same entities when no shared identifier is available. Replace example-loss minimization requiring entity resolution with equivalent minimization of a different loss over Rademacher observations, many computable without matching. Instead of performing entity matching before standard classifier learning, the method learns end-to-end by optimizing over Rademacher observations without resolving entities. TITLE: Fast Learning from Distributed Datasets without Entity Matching

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Consider the following data fusion scenario: two datasets/peers contain the same real-world entities described using partially shared features, e.g. banking and insurance company records of the same customer base. Our goal is to learn a classifier in the cross product space of the two domains, in the hard case in which no shared ID is available -- e.g. due to anonymization. Traditionally, the problem is approached by first addressing entity matching and subsequently learn

title: Fast Training of Convolutional Networks through FFTs
searchable_text:
Fast Training of Convolutional Networks through FFTs Accelerating training and inference for large convolutional networks in computer vision and machine learning. Compute convolutions as pointwise products in the Fourier domain, reusing each transformed feature map many times on GPU. Replaces existing state-of-the-art convolution implementations with FFT-based pointwise Fourier products that reuse the same transformed feature map many times. TITLE: Fast Training of Convolutional Networks through FFTs

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Convolutional networks are one of the most widely employed architectures in computer vision and machine learning. In order to leverage their ability to learn complex functions, large amounts of data are required for training. Training a large convolutional network to produce state-of-the-art results can take weeks, even when using modern GPUs. Producing labels using a trained network can also be costly when dealing with web-scale datasets. In this work, we present a simple algorithm

title: FastText.zip: Compressing text classification models
searchable_text:
FastText.zip: Compressing text classification models Text classification models that must fit within limited memory. Adapt product quantization to store fastText word embeddings while avoiding quantization artifacts, yielding a compact text classifier. Replaces the closest predecessor fastText model with a product-quantized embedding storage approach adapted to reduce quantization artifacts. TITLE: FastText.zip: Compressing text classification models

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We consider the problem of producing compact architectures for text classification, such that the full model fits in a limited amount of memory. After considering different solutions inspired by the hashing literature, we propose a method built upon product quantization to store the word embeddings. While the original technique leads to a loss in accuracy, we adapt this method to circumvent the quantization artifacts. As a result, our approach produces a text classifier, derived fro

title: Fault-Tolerant Postselected Quantum Computation: Threshold Analysis
searchable_text:
Fault-Tolerant Postselected Quantum Computation: Threshold Analysis Fault-tolerant postselected qubit-based quantum computation and its scalable error-threshold requirements. Computer-assisted heuristic threshold analysis of fault-tolerant postselected quantum computation schemes to estimate their error tolerance. Extends Knill's postselected fault-tolerant computation schemes by analyzing their error tolerance rather than only presenting the schemes. TITLE: Fault-Tolerant Postselected Quantum Computation: Threshold Analysis

YEAR: 2004

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The schemes for fault-tolerant postselected quantum computation given in [Knill, Fault-Tolerant Postselected Quantum Computation: Schemes, this http URL] are analyzed to determine their error-tolerance. The analysis is based on computer-assisted heuristics. It indicates that if classical and quantum communication delays are negligible, then scalable qubit-based quantum computation is possible with errors above 1% per elementary quantum gate.

title: Fault-tolerant quantum computation for local leakage faults
searchable_text:
Fault-tolerant quantum computation for local leakage faults Fault-tolerant quantum computation when local leakage faults move qubits outside the intended computational subspace. Use leakage-reduction units, especially quantum teleportation, with a coherent microscopic Hamiltonian noise model to convert or control leakage within fault-tolerant quantum circuits. Extends prior general non-Markovian fault-tolerance analyses to local leakage faults by adding explicit leakage-reduction units and teleportation-based leakage control. TITLE: Fault-tolerant quantum computation for local leakage faults

YEAR: 2005

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We provide a rigorous analysis of fault-tolerant quantum computation in the presence of local leakage faults. We show that one can systematically deal with leakage by using appropriate leakage-reduction units such as quantum teleportation. The leakage noise is described microscopically by a Hamiltonian couplings, and the noise is treated coherently, similar to general non-Markovian noise analyzed in Refs. [1] and [2]. We describe ways to limit the use of leakage-reduction uni

title: Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation with Constant Error Rate
searchable_text:
Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation with Constant Error Rate Scalable universal quantum computation in the presence of local noise, gate/qubit errors, inaccuracies, and correlated errors. Recursively encode quantum circuits using CSS/polynomial QECCs with transversal encoded gates, repeated polyloglog times to suppress effective error rates under local noise. Replaces Shor’s polylogarithmically decaying error-rate requirement with a constant error-rate threshold and supplies complete universality proofs. TITLE: Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation with Constant Error Rate

YEAR: 1996

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper shows that quantum computation can be made fault-tolerant against errors and inaccuracies when $\eta$, the probability for an error in a qubit or a gate, is smaller than a constant threshold $\eta_c$. This result improves on Shor's result [Proceedings of the 37th Symposium on the Foundations of Computer Science, IEEE, Los Alamitos, CA, 1996, pp. 56-65], which shows how to perform fault-tolerant quantum computation when the error rate $\eta$ decays polylogarithmical

title: Fault-tolerant quantum computation with high threshold in two dimensions.
searchable_text:
Fault-tolerant quantum computation with high threshold in two dimensions. Quantum computation under preparation, gate, storage, and measurement errors in a local architecture in two spatial dimensions. Fault-tolerant quantum computation scheme for a local two-dimensional spatial architecture. Moves fault-tolerant quantum computation to a local two-dimensional architecture while retaining a high stated threshold. TITLE: Fault-tolerant quantum computation with high threshold in two dimensions.

YEAR: 2006

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a scheme of fault-tolerant quantum computation for a local architecture in two spatial dimensions. The error threshold is 0.75% for each source in an error model with preparation, gate, storage, and measurement errors.

title: Fault-tolerant thresholds for quantum error correction with the surface code
searchable_text:
Fault-tolerant thresholds for quantum error correction with the surface code Fault-tolerant quantum computation using the surface code with nearest-neighbor gates in two spatial dimensions. Numerical simulations varying noise model, measurement circuits, and decoding algorithm to estimate surface-code fault-tolerant thresholds. Refines prior surface-code threshold estimates by explicitly testing dependence on noise model, measurement circuits, and decoding algorithm, yielding lower values. TITLE: Fault-tolerant thresholds for quantum error correction with the surface code

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The surface code is a promising candidate for fault-tolerant quantum computation, achieving a high threshold error rate with nearest-neighbor gates in two spatial dimensions. Here, through a series of numerical simulations, we investigate how the precise value of the threshold depends on the noise model, measurement circuits, and decoding algorithm. We observe thresholds between 0.502(1)% and 1.140(1)% per gate, values which are generally lower than previous

title: Federated Multi-Task Learning
searchable_text:
Federated Multi-Task Learning Training machine learning models over distributed networks of devices in federated learning settings. Use multi-task learning with MOCHA, a systems-aware optimization method robust to communication cost, stragglers, and fault tolerance issues. Distributed multi-task learning is extended to explicitly handle high communication cost, stragglers, and fault tolerance for the first time. TITLE: Federated Multi-Task Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Federated learning poses new statistical and systems challenges in training machine learning models over distributed networks of devices. In this work, we show that multi-task learning is naturally suited to handle the statistical challenges of this setting, and propose a novel systems-aware optimization method, MOCHA, that is robust to practical systems issues. Our method and theory for the first time consider issues of high communication cost, stragglers, and fault tolerance for distributed multi-task l

title: Fidelity of quantum operations
searchable_text:
Fidelity of quantum operations Average fidelity evaluation for quantum operations on finite-dimensional systems, relevant subspaces, multi-component systems, and large qubit registers. Derives and applies a compact explicit formula for average fidelity of finite-dimensional quantum operations, extendable to subspaces and multi-component systems. Provides a compact explicit average-fidelity formula and extends its use to relevant subspaces and multi-component quantum systems. TITLE: Fidelity of quantum operations

YEAR: 2007

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract We present a derivation and numerous applications of a compact explicit formula for the average fidelity of a quantum operation on a finite-dimensional quantum system. The formula can be applied to averages over particularly relevant subspaces; it is easily generalized to multi-component systems, and as a special result, we show that when the same completely positive trace-preserving map is applied to a large number of qubits with one-bit fidelity F close to unity, the average fidelity of the op

title: Fifty years of pulsar candidate selection: from simple filters to a new principled real-time classification approach
searchable_text:
Fifty years of pulsar candidate selection: from simple filters to a new principled real-time classification approach Real-time classification and prioritization of rapidly growing pulsar survey candidate streams and data volumes, especially for large surveys such as SKA and LOTAAS. Online pulsar-candidate selection using a purpose-built tree-based machine-learning classifier, GH-VFDT, plus survey-independent candidate features optimized to separate noise from probable astrophysical signals. Replaced simple historical filters and observation-level candidate caps with an online GH-VFDT classifier and a new, survey-independent feature set for high-throughput candidate selection. TITLE: Fifty years of pulsar candidate selection: from simple filters to a new principled real-time classification approach

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Improving survey specifications are causing an exponential rise in pulsar candidate numbers and data volumes. We study the candidate filters used to mitigate these problems during the past fifty years. We find that some existing methods such as applying constraints on the total number of candidates collected per observation, may have detrimental effects on the success of pulsar searches. Those methods immune to such eff

title: FigureQA: An Annotated Figure Dataset for Visual Reasoning
searchable_text:
FigureQA: An Annotated Figure Dataset for Visual Reasoning Visual reasoning over scientific-style figures: line plots, dot-line plots, bar graphs, and pie charts. Synthetic scientific-style figures paired with template-generated reasoning questions, numerical generation data, and bounding-box annotations for plot elements. Introduces a large annotated corpus of over one million question-answer pairs grounded in over 100,000 synthetic scientific-style figures. TITLE: FigureQA: An Annotated Figure Dataset for Visual Reasoning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce FigureQA, a visual reasoning corpus of over one million question-answer pairs grounded in over 100,000 images. The images are synthetic, scientific-style figures from five classes: line plots, dot-line plots, vertical and horizontal bar graphs, and pie charts. We formulate our reasoning task by generating questions from 15 templates; questions concern various relationships between plot elements and examine characteristics like the maximum, the minimum, area-under-

title: Finding Optimal Abstract Strategies in Extensive-Form Games
searchable_text:
Finding Optimal Abstract Strategies in Extensive-Form Games Computing high-quality strategies for large two-player zero-sum extensive-form games where full Nash equilibrium computation is intractable. An efficient algorithm directly searches an abstraction space for optimal abstract strategies with minimal exploitability in the unabstracted extensive-form game. Instead of solving an abstract game and accepting an arbitrary Nash equilibrium, it efficiently finds the least exploitable strategy representable by the abstraction. TITLE: Finding Optimal Abstract Strategies in Extensive-Form Games

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 Extensive-form games are a powerful model for representing interactions between agents. Nash equilibrium strategies are a common solution concept for extensive-form games and, in two-player zero-sum games, there are efficient algorithms for calculating such strategies. In large games, this computation may require too much memory and time to be tractable. A standard approach in such cases is to apply a lossy state-space abstraction technique to produce a smaller abstract g

title: Finding pulsars with LOFAR
searchable_text:
Finding pulsars with LOFAR Predicting pulsar discovery yields for LOFAR searches in the Galaxy, globular clusters, and nearby external galaxies. Optimize LOFAR pulsar searches by incoherently combining stations for all-sky Galactic surveys and coherently combining stations for compact targets to exploit full sensitivity. Introduces target-dependent LOFAR station combination: incoherent for wide Galactic surveys and coherent for smaller angular-size targets such as globular clusters and galaxies. TITLE: Finding pulsars with LOFAR

YEAR: 2007

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We investigate the number and type of pulsars that will be discovered with the low‐frequency radio telescope LOFAR. We consider different search strategies for the Galaxy, for globular clusters and for galaxies other than our own. We show an all‐sky Galactic survey can be optimally carried out by incoherently combining the LOFAR stations. In a 60‐day all‐sky Galactic survey LOFAR can find over a thousand pulsars, probing the local pulsar population to a very deep luminosity limit. For targets of smaller angu

title: Finite-Sample Analysis of Proximal Gradient TD Algorithms
searchable_text:
Finite-Sample Analysis of Proximal Gradient TD Algorithms Finite-sample performance analysis and improved algorithms for gradient TD reinforcement learning methods, especially off-policy learning. Derive GTD methods as stochastic gradients of primal-dual saddle-point objectives, then analyze saddle-point error and add proximal mirror-map variants. Replaces prior asymptotic stochastic-approximation analysis with finite-sample saddle-point analysis and introduces projected GTD2 and GTD2-MP. TITLE: Finite-Sample Analysis of Proximal Gradient TD Algorithms

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we show for the first time how gradient TD (GTD) reinforcement learning methods can be formally derived as true stochastic gradient algorithms, not with respect to their original objective functions as previously attempted, but rather using derived primal-dual saddle-point objective functions. We then conduct a saddle-point error analysis to obtain finite-sample bounds on their performance. Previous analyses of this class of algorithms use stochastic approximatio

title: First Experiments with PowerPlay
searchable_text:
First Experiments with PowerPlay Open-ended developmental learning of self-invented problems and reusable skills by a general recurrent neural network problem solver. POWERPLAY drives a SLIM RNN whose self-delimiting recurrent weights encode programs, inventing solvable novel tasks while compressing, simplifying, or speeding prior solutions. Compared with the recent conference paper, this work identifies emerging developmental stages and shows automatic self-modularization through frequent reuse of prior skill code. TITLE: First Experiments with PowerPlay

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Like a scientist or a playing child, POWERPLAY (Schmidhuber, 2011) not only learns new skills to solve given problems, but also invents new interesting problems by itself. By design, it continually comes up with the fastest to find, initially novel, but eventually solvable tasks. It also continually simplifies or compresses or speeds up solutions to previous tasks. Here we describe first experiments with POWERPLAY. A self-delimiting recurrent neural network SLIM RNN (Schmidhuber, 2012) is used as a gen

title: First interferometric detections of Fast Radio Bursts
searchable_text:
First interferometric detections of Fast Radio Bursts Real-time discovery and localization of Fast Radio Bursts to clarify origins and enable counterpart searches. Use UTMOST’s upgraded interferometric backend as a fast transient detection system for FRBs. Compared with Parkes FRB discoveries, UTMOST adds interferometric detection, better localization, and near-field exclusion of local interference. TITLE: First interferometric detections of Fast Radio Bursts

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract The class of radio transients called Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) encompasses enigmatic single pulses, each unique in its own way, hindering a consensus for their origin. The key to demystifying FRBs lies in discovering many of them in order to identity commonalities – and in real time, in order to find potential counterparts at other wavelengths. The recently upgraded UTMOST in Australia, is undergoing a backend transformation to rise as a fast transient detection machine. Th

title: Fitness uniform optimization
searchable_text:
Fitness uniform optimization Evolutionary algorithms for optimization, including deceptive artificial problems and NP-hard traveling salesman, set covering, and satisfiability tasks. Use selection uniform over fitness values, creating pressure toward sparsely populated fitness regions; optionally use a deletion scheme achieving a similar diversity-preserving effect. Selection pressure shifts from favoring fitter individuals to favoring sparsely populated fitness regions, unlike standard schemes that bias toward higher fitness. TITLE: Fitness uniform optimization

YEAR: 2006

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In evolutionary algorithms, the fitness of a population increases with time by mutating and recombining individuals and by a biased selection of fitter individuals. The right selection pressure is critical in ensuring sufficient optimization progress on the one hand and in preserving genetic diversity to be able to escape from local optima on the other hand. Motivated by a universal similarity relation on the individuals, we propose a new selection scheme, which is uniform in the fitness values. It generat

title: Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4
searchable_text:
Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4 Estimating parameters in linear mixed-effects models in R, including models with fixed- and random-effects terms. Represent linear mixed-effects models from formula and data, evaluate profiled deviance or REML criterion, then optimize it with constrained R optimization to estimate parameters.  TITLE: Fitting Linear Mixed-Effects Models Using lme4

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Maximum likelihood or restricted maximum likelihood (REML) estimates of the parameters in linear mixed-effects models can be determined using the lmer function in the lme4 package for R. As for most model-fitting functions in R, the model is described in an lmer call by a formula, in this case including both fixed- and random-effects terms. The formula and data together determine a numerical representation of the model from which the profiled deviance or the profiled REML criterion can be

title: Fixed-point quantum search with an optimal number of queries.
searchable_text:
Fixed-point quantum search with an optimal number of queries. Quantum search and amplitude amplification when the target-state fraction λ is not known beforehand. Fixed-point amplitude amplification with an adjustable failure-probability bound, optimized for the widest target-fraction range for a given query budget. Unlike prior fixed-point search algorithms that lost quadratic advantage, this version preserves quantum speedup while adding fixed-point behavior and tunable failure bounds. TITLE: Fixed-point quantum search with an optimal number of queries.

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Grover's quantum search and its generalization, quantum amplitude amplification, provide a quadratic advantage over classical algorithms for a diverse set of tasks but are tricky to use without knowing beforehand what fraction λ of the initial state is comprised of the target states. In contrast, fixed-point search algorithms need only a reliable lower bound on this fraction but, as a consequence, lose the very quadratic advantage that makes Grover's algorithm so appealing.

title: Fixing Weight Decay Regularization in Adam
searchable_text:
Fixing Weight Decay Regularization in Adam Weight decay regularization for adaptive gradient optimization, especially Adam, in image classification settings. Decouple weight decay from loss-gradient optimization steps so adaptive optimizers apply weight decay independently of Adam’s parameter update dynamics. Changed Adam’s weight decay from optimizer-coupled additive shrinkage to decoupled decay, plus normalized weight decay and warm-restart AdamWR. TITLE: Fixing Weight Decay Regularization in Adam

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We note that common implementations of adaptive gradient algorithms, such as Adam, limit the potential benefit of weight decay regularization, because the weights do not decay multiplicatively (as would be expected for standard weight decay) but by an additive constant factor. We propose a simple way to resolve this issue by decoupling weight decay and the optimization steps taken w.r.t. the loss function. We provide empirical evidence that our proposed modification (i) decouples the optimal

title: Flexible statistical inference for mechanistic models of neural dynamics
searchable_text:
Flexible statistical inference for mechanistic models of neural dynamics Quantitatively fitting mechanistic single-neuron dynamics models to empirical membrane-voltage data and inferring biophysical parameter posteriors. Likelihood-free Bayesian inference for neuron simulators using ABC with Bayesian mixture-density networks trained over adaptively chosen simulations, mapping data features to posterior parameter distributions and accommodating missing features/simulator failures. Compared with prior ABC, the change is learning a neural network posterior map, specifically a Bayesian mixture-density network, across multiple rounds of adaptively chosen simulations. TITLE: Flexible statistical inference for mechanistic models of neural dynamics

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Mechanistic models of single-neuron dynamics have been extensively studied in computational neuroscience. However, identifying which models can quantitatively reproduce empirically measured data has been challenging. We propose to overcome this limitation by using likelihood-free inference approaches (also known as Approximate Bayesian Computation, ABC) to perform full Bayesian inference on single-neuron models. Our approach builds on recent advances in ABC by le

title: Flickr30k Entities: Collecting Region-to-Phrase Correspondences for Richer Image-to-Sentence Models
searchable_text:
Flickr30k Entities: Collecting Region-to-Phrase Correspondences for Richer Image-to-Sentence Models Grounded image description: localizing textual entity mentions in images and supporting richer image-to-sentence modeling. Augment Flickr30k captions with entity coreference chains across captions and manually annotated bounding boxes linking textual mentions to image regions. Extends Flickr30k by adding 244k coreference chains and 276k manually annotated bounding boxes for region-to-phrase correspondences. TITLE: Flickr30k Entities: Collecting Region-to-Phrase Correspondences for Richer Image-to-Sentence Models

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The Flickr30k dataset has become a standard benchmark for sentence-based image description. This paper presents Flickr30k Entities, which augments the 158k captions from Flickr30k with 244k coreference chains, linking mentions of the same entities across different captions for the same image, and associating them with 276k manually annotated bounding boxes. Such annotations are essential for continued progress in automatic image descrip

title: Frasian Inference
searchable_text:
Frasian Inference Comparing Bayesian posterior probabilities with confidence levels, especially cases of agreement, disagreement, and extreme mismatch. Construct and analyze frequentistBayesian—or Frasian—methods: Bayes procedures that retain frequentist validity. Moves beyond Fraser’s account by highlighting extreme disagreements and identifying Bayes procedures that can have frequentist validity. TITLE: Frasian Inference

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Don Fraser has given an interesting account of the agreements and disagreements between Bayesian posterior probabilities and confidence levels. In this comment I discuss some cases where the lack of such agreement is extreme. I then discuss a few cases where it is possible to have Bayes procedures with frequentist validity. Such frequentistBayesian—or Frasian—methods deserve more attention.

title: FRBCAT: The Fast Radio Burst Catalogue
searchable_text:
FRBCAT: The Fast Radio Burst Catalogue Community reference database for known Fast Radio Burst sources and their comparable observational properties. An online MySQL catalogue aggregating FRB observation metadata, measured burst quantities, model-derived quantities, and uniformly reprocessed raw-data measurements with downloadable offline formats. Compared with prior scattered or originally derived FRB reports, FRBCAT adds an online community catalogue plus consistent reprocessing and side-by-side original properties. TITLE: FRBCAT: The Fast Radio Burst Catalogue

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract Here, we present a catalogue of known Fast Radio Burst sources in the form of an online catalogue, FRBCAT. The catalogue includes information about the instrumentation used for the observations for each detected burst, the measured quantities from each observation, and model-dependent quantities derived from observed quantities. To aid in consistent comparisons of burst properties such as width and signal-to-noise ratios, we have re-processed all the bursts for which we have access to th

title: Freeze-Thaw Bayesian Optimization
searchable_text:
Freeze-Thaw Bayesian Optimization Rapidly finding good hyperparameter settings for machine learning models during training. Dynamic Bayesian optimization that uses partial training-curve information, a novel positive-definite covariance kernel, scalable Gaussian process prior, and information-theoretic control to pause, start, or resume model training. Changes Bayesian optimization from static evaluations to a dynamic freeze-thaw process exploiting partial training observations to decide whether to pause, resume, or launch model training. TITLE: Freeze-Thaw Bayesian Optimization

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper we develop a dynamic form of Bayesian optimization for machine learning models with the goal of rapidly finding good hyperparameter settings. Our method uses the partial information gained during the training of a machine learning model in order to decide whether to pause training and start a new model, or resume the training of a previously-considered model. We specifically tailor our method to machine learning problems by developing a novel positive-definite covariance kernel to captur

title: From Denoising to Compressed Sensing
searchable_text:
From Denoising to Compressed Sensing Compressed sensing reconstruction of structured signals, especially natural images, from small numbers of randomized measurements. Extend AMP into D-AMP, inserting a generic denoiser into iterations plus an Onsager correction that makes each perturbation resemble white Gaussian noise. Replaced conventional AMP signal-estimation steps with denoising-based AMP able to integrate a wide class of off-the-shelf denoisers, using an appropriate Onsager correction. TITLE: From Denoising to Compressed Sensing

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A denoising algorithm seeks to remove noise, errors, or perturbations from a signal. Extensive research has been devoted to this arena over the last several decades, and as a result, todays denoisers can effectively remove large amounts of additive white Gaussian noise. A compressed sensing (CS) reconstruction algorithm seeks to recover a structured signal acquired using a small number of randomized measurements. Typical CS reconstruction algorithms can be cast as iteratively estimating a signal fr

title: From Knothe's Transport to Brenier's Map and a Continuation Method for Optimal Transport
searchable_text:
From Knothe's Transport to Brenier's Map and a Continuation Method for Optimal Transport Mapping two probability measures in Rd and numerically solving the optimal transport problem. Use weighted quadratic Monge-Kantorovich problems whose coordinate weights asymptotically dominate one another, taking their solution limit to recover the Knothe-Rosenblatt rearrangement. Instead of treating Knothe-Rosenblatt rearrangement separately, it is derived as a limiting case of quadratic-cost Monge-Kantorovich maps, yielding a continuation method for optimal transport. TITLE: From Knothe's Transport to Brenier's Map and a Continuation Method for Optimal Transport

YEAR: 2008

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A simple procedure to map two probability measures in Rd is the so-called Knothe-Rosenblatt rearrangement, which consists in rearranging monotonically the marginal distributions of the last coordinate, and then the conditional distributions, iteratively. We show that this mapping is the limit of solutions to a class of Monge-Kantorovich mass transportation problems with quadratic costs, with the weights of the coordinates asymptotically dominating

title: From optimal transport to generative modeling: the VEGAN cookbook
searchable_text:
From optimal transport to generative modeling: the VEGAN cookbook Unsupervised generative modeling between an unknown true data distribution and a latent variable model distribution. Reformulate OT between data and model distributions using probabilistic encoders constrained to match posterior/prior; relax to penalized optimal transport minimized by SGD. Turns heuristic AAE training into an OT-derived POT objective and connects Wasserstein GAN to the dual of 1-Wasserstein POT. TITLE: From optimal transport to generative modeling: the VEGAN cookbook

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study unsupervised generative modeling in terms of the optimal transport (OT) problem between true (but unknown) data distribution $P_X$ and the latent variable model distribution $P_G$. We show that the OT problem can be equivalently written in terms of probabilistic encoders, which are constrained to match the posterior and prior distributions over the latent space. When relaxed, this constrained optimization problem leads to a penalized optimal transport (POT) obj

title: From Pixels to Torques: Policy Learning with Deep Dynamical Models
searchable_text:
From Pixels to Torques: Policy Learning with Deep Dynamical Models Pixels-to-torques closed-loop continuous control, where an autonomous agent learns policies directly from high-dimensional pixel observations in continuous state-action spaces. Model-based reinforcement learning using a deep dynamical model that jointly learns image embeddings via deep autoencoders and predictive dynamics in the low-dimensional feature space for adaptive MPC. Unlike state-of-the-art reinforcement learning methods, this approach learns directly from pixels with a deep dynamical model and adaptive model predictive control. TITLE: From Pixels to Torques: Policy Learning with Deep Dynamical Models

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Data-efficient learning in continuous state-action spaces using very high-dimensional observations remains a key challenge in developing fully autonomous systems. In this paper, we consider one instance of this challenge, the pixels-totorques problem, where an agent must learn a closed-loop control policy from pixel information only. We introduce a data-efficient, model-based reinforcement learning algorithm that learns such a closed-loop policy directly from pixel inf

title: From the Schr\"odinger problem to the Monge-Kantorovich problem
searchable_text:
From the Schr\"odinger problem to the Monge-Kantorovich problem The limiting relationship between Schrödinger entropy minimization problems and the Monge-Kantorovich optimal transport problem, including static and dynamic formulations. Use convex/functional analysis and Gamma-convergence to prove that entropy minimization with vanishing fluctuations converges to optimal transport. Changes the predecessor view by deriving Monge-Kantorovich as the zero-fluctuation limit of a sequence of entropy minimization problems. TITLE: From the Schr\"odinger problem to the Monge-Kantorovich problem

YEAR: 2010

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The aim of this article is to show that the Monge-Kantorovich problem is the limit of a sequence of entropy minimization problems when a fluctuation parameter tends down to zero. We prove the convergence of the entropic values to the optimal transport cost as the fluctuations decrease to zero, and we also show that the limit points of the entropic minimizers are optimal transport plans. We investigate the dynamic versions of these problems by considering random paths and

title: Functional Map of the World
searchable_text:
Functional Map of the World Predicting the functional purpose of buildings and land use from temporal sequences of satellite images and metadata. A large-scale temporal satellite imagery dataset with metadata and bounding-box category annotations to support models that reason about location, time, sun angles, and physical sizes. Introduces fMoW as a new publicly available dataset combining temporal satellite imagery, rich metadata, bounding boxes, and functional land-use categories at global scale. TITLE: Functional Map of the World

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a new dataset, Functional Map of the World (fMoW), which aims to inspire the development of machine learning models capable of predicting the functional purpose of buildings and land use from temporal sequences of satellite images and a rich set of metadata features. The metadata provided with each image enables reasoning about location, time, sun angles, physical sizes, and other features when making predictions about objects in the image. Our dataset consists of over 1 million images from over

title: Gaussian approximation potentials: the accuracy of quantum mechanics, without the electrons.
searchable_text:
Gaussian approximation potentials: the accuracy of quantum mechanics, without the electrons. Predicting energies, forces, and high-temperature properties of bulk crystals without running long quantum-mechanical molecular dynamics. Automatically generate nonparametric interatomic potentials from quantum-mechanical energy and force data. Replaced fixed-form interatomic potentials with data-generated models lacking a fixed functional form and trained on quantum-mechanical energies and forces. TITLE: Gaussian approximation potentials: the accuracy of quantum mechanics, without the electrons.

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce a class of interatomic potential models that can be automatically generated from data consisting of the energies and forces experienced by atoms, as derived from quantum mechanical calculations. The models do not have a fixed functional form and hence are capable of modeling complex potential energy landscapes. They are systematically improvable with more data. We apply the method to bulk crystals, and test it by calculating prope

title: Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs)
searchable_text:
Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs) Neural network activation functions for computer vision, natural language processing, and speech tasks. Use activation xΦ(x), the input multiplied by the standard Gaussian cumulative distribution function, weighting inputs by value instead of hard sign gating. Replaces ReLU-style sign gating x1_{x>0} with value-dependent Gaussian CDF weighting xΦ(x). TITLE: Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs)

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose the Gaussian Error Linear Unit (GELU), a high-performing neural network activation function. The GELU activation function is $x\Phi(x)$, where $\Phi(x)$ the standard Gaussian cumulative distribution function. The GELU nonlinearity weights inputs by their value, rather than gates inputs by their sign as in ReLUs ($x\mathbf{1}_{x>0}$). We perform an empirical evaluation of the GELU nonlinearity against the ReLU and ELU activations and find performance improvements across all considered comp

title: Generalized Emphatic Temporal Difference Learning: Bias-Variance Analysis
searchable_text:
Generalized Emphatic Temporal Difference Learning: Bias-Variance Analysis Off-policy evaluation in Markov decision processes with function approximation. ETD(λ, β), a generalized emphatic TD framework where β controls decay of an importance-sampling term, analyzed through contraction of the projected fixed-point operator. Adds β to ETD(λ), generalizing original ETD and other off-policy evaluation algorithms by controlling importance-sampling decay. TITLE: Generalized Emphatic Temporal Difference Learning: Bias-Variance Analysis

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 We consider the off-policy evaluation problem in Markov decision processes with function approximation. We propose a generalization of the recently introduced emphatic temporal differences (ETD) algorithm, which encompasses the original ETD(λ), as well as several other off-policy evaluation algorithms as special cases. We call this framework ETD(λ, β), where our introduced parameter β controls the decay rate of an importance-sampling term. We study condition

title: Generalized Wasserstein Distance and its Application to Transport Equations with Source
searchable_text:
Generalized Wasserstein Distance and its Application to Transport Equations with Source Transport equations with a source where both the vector field and source depend on the evolving measure itself. Generalize Wasserstein distance to compare measures with unequal total mass, then use Lipschitz dependence in that metric to analyze measure-dependent transport dynamics with source. Extends classical Wasserstein distance from equal-mass measures to measures with different masses. TITLE: Generalized Wasserstein Distance and its Application to Transport Equations with Source

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this article, we generalize the Wasserstein distance to measures with different masses. We study the properties of this distance. In particular, we show that it metrizes weak convergence for tight sequences. We use this generalized Wasserstein distance to study a transport equation with a source, in which both the vector field and the source depend on the measure itself. We prove the existence and uniqueness of the solution to the Cauchy problem

title: Generating Sentences by Editing Prototypes
searchable_text:
Generating Sentences by Editing Prototypes Generative language modeling for sentences. Samples a prototype sentence from the training corpus, then edits it into a new sentence using a latent edit vector. Replaces generating sentences from scratch with a prototype-then-edit process grounded in retrieved training-corpus sentences. TITLE: Generating Sentences by Editing Prototypes

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose a new generative language model for sentences that first samples a prototype sentence from the training corpus and then edits it into a new sentence. Compared to traditional language models that generate from scratch either left-to-right or by first sampling a latent sentence vector, our prototype-then-edit model improves perplexity on language modeling and generates higher quality outputs according to human evaluation. Furthermore, the model gives rise to a latent edit vector that

title: Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning
searchable_text:
Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning Learning a policy from example expert behavior without interaction with the expert or access to reinforcement signal. Directly extract a policy from expert data via an adversarial imitation-learning framework analogous to generative adversarial networks. Replaced indirect inverse-reinforcement-learning-then-reinforcement-learning with direct policy extraction from data. TITLE: Generative Adversarial Imitation Learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Consider learning a policy from example expert behavior, without interaction with the expert or access to reinforcement signal. One approach is to recover the expert's cost function with inverse reinforcement learning, then extract a policy from that cost function with reinforcement learning. This approach is indirect and can be slow. We propose a new general framework for directly extracting a policy from data, as if it were obtained by reinforcement learning following inverse reinforcement l

title: Generative Adversarial Text to Image Synthesis
searchable_text:
Generative Adversarial Text to Image Synthesis Automatic synthesis of realistic images from detailed text descriptions, demonstrated on birds and flowers. A novel deep architecture and GAN formulation bridges recurrent text representations with deep convolutional GAN image generation, translating visual concepts from characters to pixels. It combines recent discriminative recurrent text feature learning with deep convolutional GAN image synthesis to connect text and image modeling. TITLE: Generative Adversarial Text to Image Synthesis

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Automatic synthesis of realistic images from text would be interesting and useful, but current AI systems are still far from this goal. However, in recent years generic and powerful recurrent neural network architectures have been developed to learn discriminative text feature representations. Meanwhile, deep convolutional generative adversarial networks (GANs) have begun to generate highly compelling images of specific categories, such as faces, album covers, and room interiors. In this

title: Generative Temporal Models with Memory
searchable_text:
Generative Temporal Models with Memory Modeling temporal data with sparse, long-range dependencies where observations are fully or partially predictable from temporally distant past observations. Generative Temporal Models developed in variational inference framework and augmented with external memory systems to store and reuse temporally distant information. Adds external memory systems to generative temporal models within variational inference, rather than relying on well-known recurrent neural networks like LSTMs. TITLE: Generative Temporal Models with Memory

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We consider the general problem of modeling temporal data with long-range dependencies, wherein new observations are fully or partially predictable based on temporally-distant, past observations. A sufficiently powerful temporal model should separate predictable elements of the sequence from unpredictable elements, express uncertainty about those unpredictable elements, and rapidly identify novel elements that may help to predict the future. To create such models, we introduce Generative Temporal

title: Genetic CNN
searchable_text:
Genetic CNN Automatic discovery of deep convolutional neural network architectures for visual recognition, first on CIFAR10 and transferable to ILSVRC2012. Use a genetic algorithm with fixed-length binary encodings of CNN structures, applying selection, mutation, and crossover; each individual’s fitness is its recognition accuracy after standalone training. Replaced manually designed fixed CNN architectures with automatically learned network structures represented as binary strings and optimized by a genetic algorithm. TITLE: Genetic CNN

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The deep convolutional neural network (CNN) is the state-of-the-art solution for large-scale visual recognition. Following some basic principles such as increasing network depth and constructing highway connections, researchers have manually designed a lot of fixed network architectures and verified their effectiveness.,,In this paper, we discuss the possibility of learning deep network structures automatically. Note that the number of possible network structures increases exponentially with the number of layers in the net

title: Genetics Talks to Epigenetics? The Interplay Between Sequence Variants and Chromatin Structure
searchable_text:
Genetics Talks to Epigenetics? The Interplay Between Sequence Variants and Chromatin Structure Genetic variant–phenotype association studies where genotype–phenotype links are unresolved or variants lie outside promoters and protein-coding sequences. Analyze how DNA sequence variants co-regulate transcription by altering local chromatin structure, including DNA methylation patterns or histone type recruitment. Moves from separate genetic or epigenetic explanations of transcriptional regulation to an integrated crosstalk model linking sequence variants with chromatin structure. TITLE: Genetics Talks to Epigenetics? The Interplay Between Sequence Variants and Chromatin Structure

YEAR: 2010

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Transcription is regulated by two major mechanisms. On the one hand, changes in DNA sequence are responsible for genetic gene regulation. On the other hand, chromatin structure regulates gene activity at the epigenetic level. Given the fundamental participation of these mechanisms in transcriptional regulation of virtually any gene, they are likely to co-regulate a significant proportion of the genome. The simple concept behind this idea is

title: Geometric Deep Learning on Graphs and Manifolds Using Mixture Model CNNs
searchable_text:
Geometric Deep Learning on Graphs and Manifolds Using Mixture Model CNNs Geometric deep learning for non-Euclidean structured data, including graphs and manifolds, across image, graph, and 3D shape analysis tasks. A unified mixture-model CNN framework generalizes convolutional architectures to graphs and manifolds, learning local, stationary, compositional task-specific features. It unifies prior non-Euclidean CNN methods as instances of one framework rather than treating them as separate graph or manifold-specific architectures. TITLE: Geometric Deep Learning on Graphs and Manifolds Using Mixture Model CNNs

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep learning has achieved a remarkable performance breakthrough in several fields, most notably in speech recognition, natural language processing, and computer vision. In particular, convolutional neural network (CNN) architectures currently produce state-of-the-art performance on a variety of image analysis tasks such as object detection and recognition. Most of deep learning research has so far focused on dealing with 1D, 2D, or 3D Euclidean-structured data s

title: Glimmers: Resolving the Privacy/Trust Quagmire
searchable_text:
Glimmers: Resolving the Privacy/Trust Quagmire Trustworthy validation of user-contributed data for recommendation, prediction, classification, and interpretation services while preserving user privacy. Use client-side trusted hardware such as Intel SGX to validate privacy-sensitive user context, creating a Glimmer of Trust without exposing private information to the service. Shifts SGX use from cloud services to client-side trusted hardware for privacy-preserving contribution validation. TITLE: Glimmers: Resolving the Privacy/Trust Quagmire

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Users today enjoy access to a wealth of services that rely on user-contributed data, such as recommendation services, prediction services, and services that help classify and interpret data. The quality of such services inescapably relies on trustworthy contributions from users. However, validating the trustworthiness of contributions may rely on privacy-sensitive contextual data about the user, such as a user's location or usage habits, creating a conflict between privacy and trust: user

title: Good debt or bad debt: Detecting semantic orientations in economic texts
searchable_text:
Good debt or bad debt: Detecting semantic orientations in economic texts Detecting contextual semantic orientations in financial and economic news texts, where sentence-level sentiment may diverge from individual word polarities. Linearized phrase-structure sentiment model using domain-specific financial lexicon attributes for expected event direction, trained/evaluated with a human-annotated finance phrase bank. Moves beyond general sentiment models and word-frequency approaches by adding finance phrase annotations, event-direction lexicon attributes, and a linearized phrase-structure model. TITLE: Good debt or bad debt: Detecting semantic orientations in economic texts

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The use of robo‐readers to analyze news texts is an emerging technology trend in computational finance. Recent research has developed sophisticated financial polarity lexicons for investigating how financial sentiments relate to future company performance. However, based on experience from fields that commonly analyze sentiment, it is well known that the overall semantic orientation of a sentence may differ from that of individual words. This article investigates

title: Gossip Dual Averaging for Decentralized Optimization of Pairwise Functions
searchable_text:
Gossip Dual Averaging for Decentralized Optimization of Pairwise Functions Decentralized minimization of pairwise functions over data points distributed across graph-network nodes, with applications to ranking, distance metric learning, graph inference, and AUC maximization. Gossip algorithms based on dual averaging for decentralized pairwise-function minimization, supporting synchronous/asynchronous execution plus constrained and regularized variants. Extends dual averaging from centralized optimization to new gossip algorithms for decentralized pairwise-function objectives in synchronous and asynchronous networks.
