title: Learn&Fuzz: Machine learning for input fuzzing
searchable_text:
Learn&Fuzz: Machine learning for input fuzzing Finding security vulnerabilities in input-parsing code for complex formats, demonstrated on PDF parsing in Microsoft Edge. Neural-network statistical learning infers an input grammar and probability distribution from sample inputs, then uses it to guide where fuzzing should mutate inputs. Moves input fuzzing toward automatically learned grammars and probability-guided mutation choices rather than unguided modification of inputs. TITLE: Learn&Fuzz: Machine learning for input fuzzing

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Fuzzing consists of repeatedly testing an application with modified, or fuzzed, inputs with the goal of finding security vulnerabilities in input-parsing code. In this paper, we show how to automate the generation of an input grammar suitable for input fuzzing using sample inputs and neural-network-based statistical machine-learning techniques. We present a detailed case study with a complex input format, namely PDF, and a large complex security-critical parser for this format, namely, th

title: Learning a Probabilistic Latent Space of Object Shapes via 3D Generative-Adversarial Modeling
searchable_text:
Learning a Probabilistic Latent Space of Object Shapes via 3D Generative-Adversarial Modeling Unconditional 3D object generation and unsupervised 3D shape representation learning for object recognition. 3D-GAN combines volumetric convolutional networks with a generative adversarial objective to map low-dimensional probabilistic latent samples into 3D object volumes. Replaced traditional heuristic generation criteria with an adversarial criterion and learned a probabilistic latent-to-3D-object mapping without reference images or CAD models. TITLE: Learning a Probabilistic Latent Space of Object Shapes via 3D Generative-Adversarial Modeling

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the problem of 3D object generation. We propose a novel framework, namely 3D Generative Adversarial Network (3D-GAN), which generates 3D objects from a probabilistic space by leveraging recent advances in volumetric convolutional networks and generative adversarial nets. The benefits of our model are three-fold: first, the use of an adversarial criterion, instead of traditional heuristic criteria, enables the generator to capture obj

title: Learning and Policy Search in Stochastic Dynamical Systems with Bayesian Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Learning and Policy Search in Stochastic Dynamical Systems with Bayesian Neural Networks Policy learning/control in stochastic dynamical systems, including challenging model-based reinforcement learning benchmarks and real-world gas turbine control. Model-based reinforcement learning combining Bayesian neural networks trained by minimizing alpha-divergences with random roll-outs and stochastic optimization for policy learning. Replaces common transition models with alpha-divergence-trained Bayesian neural networks that capture multi-modality and heteroskedasticity usually missed by other approaches. TITLE: Learning and Policy Search in Stochastic Dynamical Systems with Bayesian Neural Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present an algorithm for model-based reinforcement learning that combines Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) with random roll-outs and stochastic optimization for policy learning. The BNNs are trained by minimizing $\alpha$-divergences, allowing us to capture complicated statistical patterns in the transition dynamics, e.g. multi-modality and heteroskedasticity, which are usually missed by other common modeling approaches. We illustrate the perfor

title: Learning Bayesian Networks with the bnlearn R Package
searchable_text:
Learning Bayesian Networks with the bnlearn R Package Learning Bayesian network structures from data with discrete or continuous variables in R. R package implementing constraint-based and score-based Bayesian network structure learning for discrete or continuous variables, with scores, conditional independence tests, parallel computing, and plotting support.  TITLE: Learning Bayesian Networks with the bnlearn R Package

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
bnlearn is an R package (R Development Core Team 2010) which includes several algorithms for learning the structure of Bayesian networks with either discrete or continuous variables. Both constraint-based and score-based algorithms are implemented, and can use the functionality provided by the snow package (Tierney et al. 2008) to improve their performance via parallel computing. Several network scores and conditional independence algorithms are available for both the learning algo

title: Learning by Transduction
searchable_text:
Learning by Transduction Predicting the classification of a new object from classified training objects drawn i.i.d. from a continuous probability distribution. A transductive modification of Vapnik's support-vector machine that predicts labels and assigns evidence/confidence measures to those predictions. Compared with the support-vector machine, it adds an explicit practicable evidence measure and a procedure for assigning confidence to predictions. TITLE: Learning by Transduction

YEAR: 1998

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We describe a method for predicting a classification of an object given classifications of the objects in the training set, assuming that the pairs object/classification are generated by an i.i.d. process from a continuous probability distribution. Our method is a modification of Vapnik's support-vector machine; its main novelty is that it gives not only the prediction itself but also a practicable measure of the evidence found in support of that prediction. We also describe a procedure for assigning degrees o

title: Learning Continuous Control Policies by Stochastic Value Gradients
searchable_text:
Learning Continuous Control Policies by Stochastic Value Gradients Learning continuous control policies for stochastic, physics-based simulation domains. Backpropagate stochastic value gradients through Bellman equations by representing stochasticity as deterministic functions of exogenous noise, spanning model-free value-function methods to model-based policy learning. Unifies continuous-control policy gradients by reparameterizing stochastic Bellman dynamics with exogenous noise, connecting model-free value-function algorithms and model-based methods without value functions. TITLE: Learning Continuous Control Policies by Stochastic Value Gradients

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a unified framework for learning continuous control policies using backpropagation. It supports stochastic control by treating stochasticity in the Bellman equation as a deterministic function of exogenous noise. The product is a spectrum of general policy gradient algorithms that range from model-free methods with value functions to model-based methods without value functions. title: Learning Convolutional Neural Networks for Graphs
searchable_text:
Learning Convolutional Neural Networks for Graphs Learning from graph data, including undirected or directed graphs with discrete and continuous node and edge attributes. A graph CNN framework extracts locally connected regions from arbitrary graphs and learns feature representations over those regions. Extends convolutional neural networks from image-like locally connected inputs to arbitrary graph-structured data via locally connected graph region extraction. TITLE: Learning Convolutional Neural Networks for Graphs

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Numerous important problems can be framed as learning from graph data. We propose a framework for learning convolutional neural networks for arbitrary graphs. These graphs may be undirected, directed, and with both discrete and continuous node and edge attributes. Analogous to image-based convolutional networks that operate on locally connected regions of the input, we present a general approach to extracting locally connected regions from graphs. Using established benchmark data sets,

title: Learning Cooperative Visual Dialog Agents with Deep Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Learning Cooperative Visual Dialog Agents with Deep Reinforcement Learning Goal-driven visual dialog: enabling a questioner to identify an unseen image from an image lineup through dialog with an answering agent. Deep reinforcement learning trains Q-BOT and A-BOT end-to-end in a cooperative image-guessing game, from pixels through multi-round natural-language dialog to game reward. Introduces first goal-driven training for visual question answering and dialog agents, moving beyond supervised dialog pretraining to cooperative game reward optimization. TITLE: Learning Cooperative Visual Dialog Agents with Deep Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce the first goal-driven training for visual question answering and dialog agents. Specifically, we pose a cooperative ‘image guessing’ game between two agents – Q-BOT and A-BOT– who communicate in natural language dialog so that Q-BOT can select an unseen image from a lineup of images. title: Learning Dynamic Robot-to-Human Object Handover from Human Feedback
searchable_text:
Learning Dynamic Robot-to-Human Object Handover from Human Feedback Dynamic robot-to-human object handover, such as giving water bottles to moving marathon runners. Contextual policy search learns a dynamic robot handover policy through interaction with humans while estimating the latent task reward from noisy human feedback. Recasts dynamic object handover as contextual policy search driven by human interaction and noisy feedback, rather than relying on fixed handover behavior. TITLE: Learning Dynamic Robot-to-Human Object Handover from Human Feedback

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Object handover is a basic, but essential capability for robots interacting with humans in many applications, e.g., caring for the elderly and assisting workers in manufacturing workshops. It appears deceptively simple, as humans perform object handover almost flawlessly. The success of humans, however, belies the complexity of object handover as collaborative physical interaction between two agents with limited communication. This paper presents a learning algorithm

title: Learning Entity and Relation Embeddings for Knowledge Graph Completion
searchable_text:
Learning Entity and Relation Embeddings for Knowledge Graph Completion Knowledge graph completion via link prediction between entities, evaluated on link prediction, triple classification, and relational fact extraction. TransR embeds entities and relations in separate spaces, projects entities into each relation space, then models relations as translations between projected entities. Unlike TransE and TransH, TransR separates entity space from relation-specific spaces before applying translation modeling. TITLE: Learning Entity and Relation Embeddings for Knowledge Graph Completion

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 Knowledge graph completion aims to perform link prediction between entities. In this paper, we consider the approach of knowledge graph embeddings. Recently, models such as TransE and TransH build entity and relation embeddings by regarding a relation as translation from head entity to tail entity. We note that these models simply put both entities and relations within the same semantic space. In fact, an entity may have multiple aspects and various relations m

title: Learning from Logged Implicit Exploration Data
searchable_text:
Learning from Logged Implicit Exploration Data Contextual bandit or partially labeled settings where only the value of the chosen action is observed in offline logged data. A sound, consistent method for learning action-selection policies from historical nonrandom logged exploration data when the logging policy is not explicitly known. It removes prior requirements for controlled actions, recorded random exploration, or repeated oblivious action choices, enabling learning when no randomization occurred or was logged. TITLE: Learning from Logged Implicit Exploration Data

YEAR: 2010

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We provide a sound and consistent foundation for the use of nonrandom exploration data in "contextual bandit" or "partially labeled" settings where only the value of a chosen action is learned. The primary challenge in a variety of settings is that the exploration policy, in which "offline" data is logged, is not explicitly known. Prior solutions here require either control of the actions during the learning process, recorded random exploration, or actions chosen obliviously in a repeated

title: Learning from Scarce Experience
searchable_text:
Learning from Scarce Experience Partially observable reinforcement learning problems where direct policy search needs many environment interactions to evaluate changing target policies. Likelihood-ratio off-policy policy search: reuse experience from one or more behavior policies to estimate another policy’s value, then optimize a non-parametric estimate. Instead of estimating each policy only by executing it, it estimates a different policy’s value using data gathered under another policy or collection of policies. TITLE: Learning from Scarce Experience

YEAR: 2002

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Searching the space of policies directly for the optimal policy has been one popular method for solving partially observable reinforcement learning problems. Typically, with each change of the target policy, its value is estimated from the results of following that very policy. This requires a large number of interactions with the environment as different polices are considered. We present a family of algorithms based on likelihood ratio estimation that use data gathered when executing one policy (or co

title: Learning Generative ConvNets via Multi-grid Modeling and Sampling
searchable_text:
Learning Generative ConvNets via Multi-grid Modeling and Sampling Learning energy-based probabilistic generative ConvNet models of images. Learn energy-based generative ConvNets across multiple image grids using coarse-to-fine finite-step MCMC, starting from a minimal 1 Ã— 1 training-image version and updating grid models separately but simultaneously. Replaces single-grid CD/PCD sampling with multi-grid coarse-to-fine finite-step MCMC initialized from a minimal 1 Ã— 1 version of each training image. TITLE: Learning Generative ConvNets via Multi-grid Modeling and Sampling

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper proposes a multi-grid method for learning energy-based generative ConvNet models of images. For each grid, we learn an energy-based probabilistic model where the energy function is defined by a bottom-up convolutional neural network (ConvNet or CNN). Learning such a model requires generating synthesized examples from the model. Within each iteration of our learning algorithm, for each observed training image, we generate synthesized images at multiple grids b

title: Learning locomotion skills using DeepRL: does the choice of action space matter?
searchable_text:
Learning locomotion skills using DeepRL: does the choice of action space matter? Gait-cycle imitation for multiple planar articulated figures and multiple gaits. Compare four DeepRL action parameterizations—torques, muscle activations, target joint angles, and target joint-angle velocities—on locomotion imitation. Systematically varied the action space instead of treating action representation as fixed in DeepRL locomotion learning. TITLE: Learning locomotion skills using DeepRL: does the choice of action space matter?

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The use of deep reinforcement learning allows for high-dimensional state descriptors, but little is known about how the choice of action representation impacts learning and the resulting performance. We compare the impact of four different action parameterizations (torques, muscle-activations, target joint angles, and target joint-angle velocities) in terms of learning time, policy robustness, motion quality, and policy query rates. Our results are evalua

title: Learning Model Predictive Control for Iterative Tasks. A Data-Driven Control Framework
searchable_text:
Learning Model Predictive Control for Iterative Tasks. A Data-Driven Control Framework Reference-free control for iterative tasks where repeated executions enable learning-based performance improvement. Learning MPC recursively constructs a safe set and terminal cost from previous state/input trajectories to ensure feasibility and improve iteration-to-iteration performance. Introduces reference-free learning MPC that reuses previous task trajectories to build terminal ingredients and improve performance across iterations. TITLE: Learning Model Predictive Control for Iterative Tasks. A Data-Driven Control Framework

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A learning model predictive controller for iterative tasks is presented. The controller is reference-free and is able to improve its performance by learning from previous iterations. A safe set and a terminal cost function are used in order to guarantee recursive feasibility and nondecreasing performance at each iteration. This paper presents the control design approach, and shows how to recursively construct terminal set and terminal cost from stat

title: Learning Nash Equilibrium for General-Sum Markov Games from Batch Data
searchable_text:
Learning Nash Equilibrium for General-Sum Markov Games from Batch Data Learning Nash equilibria in γ-discounted multiplayer general-sum Markov Games from batch data. Minimize empirical Lp norms of two Bellman-like residuals, implemented via NashNetwork, to learn ε-Nash equilibria from batch data in general-sum Markov games. Extends beyond two-player zero-sum Markov-game techniques by defining ε-Nash equilibrium for multiplayer general-sum games and learning it via Bellman-like residual minimization. TITLE: Learning Nash Equilibrium for General-Sum Markov Games from Batch Data

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper addresses the problem of learning a Nash equilibrium in $\gamma$-discounted multiplayer general-sum Markov Games (MG). A key component of this model is the possibility for the players to either collaborate or team apart to increase their rewards. Building an artificial player for general-sum MGs implies to learn more complex strategies which are impossible to obtain by using techniques developed for two-player zero-sum MGs. In this paper, we introduce a

title: Learning Parameterized Skills
searchable_text:
Learning Parameterized Skills Parameterized reinforcement learning problems, evaluated on an underactuated simulated robotic arm learning to throw darts at parameterized target locations. Draw example tasks, learn policies, estimate a lower-dimensional piecewise-smooth policy manifold, identify charts, and fit nonlinear regressions mapping task parameters to policy parameters.  TITLE: Learning Parameterized Skills

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce a method for constructing skills capable of solving tasks drawn from a distribution of parameterized reinforcement learning problems. The method draws example tasks from a distribution of interest and uses the corresponding learned policies to estimate the topology of the lower-dimensional piecewise-smooth manifold on which the skill policies lie. This manifold models how policy parameters change as task parameters vary. The method identifies the number of charts that compose the manifold and

title: Learning Probability Measures with respect to Optimal Transport Metrics
searchable_text:
Learning Probability Measures with respect to Optimal Transport Metrics Estimating probability measures under optimal transport metrics when the measure is supported on a manifold embedded in a Hilbert space. Connect optimal transport metrics, optimal quantization, and learning theory to analyze k-means-derived probability measures and empirical law convergence. Extends beyond existing bounds by providing convergence guarantees applicable to a wider class of measures. TITLE: Learning Probability Measures with respect to Optimal Transport Metrics

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the problem of estimating, in the sense of optimal transport metrics, a measure which is assumed supported on a manifold embedded in a Hilbert space. By establishing a precise connection between optimal transport metrics, optimal quantization, and learning theory, we derive new probabilistic bounds for the performance of a classic algorithm in unsupervised learning (k-means), when used to produce a probability measure derived from the data. In the course

title: Learning quadrotor dynamics using neural network for flight control
searchable_text:
Learning quadrotor dynamics using neural network for flight control Quadrotor flight control for trajectories different from training data, especially simultaneous yaw and position control under nonlinear motion coupling. Learn a neural-network quadrotor dynamics model from translational and rotational trajectories, then use the learned model to synthesize flight control for new combined motions. Shifted from iteratively improving nominal controllers for specific trajectories to using deep neural dynamics generalization for controller synthesis on unseen combined trajectories. TITLE: Learning quadrotor dynamics using neural network for flight control

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Traditional learning approaches proposed for controlling quadrotors or helicopters have focused on improving performance for specific trajectories by iteratively improving upon a nominal controller, for example learning from demonstrations, iterative learning, and reinforcement learning. In these schemes, however, it is not clear how the information gathered from the training trajectories can be used to synthesize controllers for more general trajectories. Recently, t

title: Learning Steerable Filters for Rotation Equivariant CNNs
searchable_text:
Learning Steerable Filters for Rotation Equivariant CNNs Machine learning tasks requiring predictions that transform equivariantly under input translations and rotations, including rotated MNIST and 2D EM segmentation. Steerable Filter CNNs use steerable filters and group convolutions to compute orientation-dependent responses and guarantee joint translation-rotation equivariant mappings. Compared with standard CNNs that only provide translational equivariance by construction, this architecture builds rotation equivariance directly into CNNs via steerable filters and group convolutions. TITLE: Learning Steerable Filters for Rotation Equivariant CNNs

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In many machine learning tasks it is desirable that a model's prediction transforms in an equivariant way under transformations of its input. Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) implement translational equivariance by construction; for other transformations, however, they are compelled to learn the proper mapping. In this work, we develop Steerable Filter CNNs (SFCNNs) which achieve joint equivariance under translations and rotations by design. The proposed architecture employs

title: Learning to Act by Predicting the Future
searchable_text:
Learning to Act by Predicting the Future Sensorimotor control from raw sensory input in immersive three-dimensional environments, including Doom-based simulations with dynamically changing goals. Train a sensorimotor control model from cotemporal high-dimensional sensory streams and low-dimensional measurement streams using supervised learning without extraneous supervision. Compared with prior formulations, it learns without a fixed training-time goal and can pursue dynamically changing test-time goals from raw sensory input. TITLE: Learning to Act by Predicting the Future

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present an approach to sensorimotor control in immersive environments. Our approach utilizes a high-dimensional sensory stream and a lower-dimensional measurement stream. The cotemporal structure of these streams provides a rich supervisory signal, which enables training a sensorimotor control model by interacting with the environment. The model is trained using supervised learning techniques, but without extraneous supervision. It learns to act based on raw sensory input from a complex thre

title: Learning to communicate: Channel auto-encoders, domain specific regularizers, and attention
searchable_text:
Learning to communicate: Channel auto-encoders, domain specific regularizers, and attention Learning efficient, adaptive physical-layer encodings for communicating binary information over impaired wireless channels. An unsupervised channel autoencoder optimizes reconstruction loss through artificial impairment layers, with wireless-channel regularizers and radio transformer attention to recover canonical signal representations before decoding. Unlike traditional physical-layer design, it frames encoding over impaired channels as an unsupervised machine-learning reconstruction problem with differentiable channel impairments and attention. TITLE: Learning to communicate: Channel auto-encoders, domain specific regularizers, and attention

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We address the problem of learning an efficient and adaptive physical layer encoding to communicate binary information over an impaired channel. In contrast to traditional work, we treat the problem an unsupervised machine learning problem focusing on optimizing reconstruction loss through artificial impairment layers in an autoencoder (we term this a channel autoencoder) and introduce several new regularizing layers which emulate common wirele

title: Learning to decode linear codes using deep learning
searchable_text:
Learning to decode linear codes using deep learning Decoding linear codes, especially high-density parity-check codes, by improving belief propagation performance. Generalize belief propagation by assigning trainable weights to Tanner-graph edges and optimizing them with deep learning. Standard belief propagation’s unweighted Tanner-graph message passing is replaced with edge-weighted, trainable belief propagation while preserving codeword-independence. TITLE: Learning to decode linear codes using deep learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A novel deep learning method for improving the belief propagation algorithm is proposed. The method generalizes the standard belief propagation algorithm by assigning weights to the edges of the Tanner graph. These edges are then trained using deep learning techniques. A well-known property of the belief propagation algorithm is the independence of the performance on the transmitted codeword. A crucial property of our new method is that our decoder preserved this property. Furthermor

title: Learning to Execute
searchable_text:
Learning to Execute Evaluating short computer programs that can be processed in a single left-to-right pass with constant memory, plus character-level multi-digit addition. Train LSTM sequence-to-sequence models on character-level program strings, using a new curriculum-learning variant to map programs to outputs. Replaced conventional curriculum learning, which was ineffective, with a new curriculum variant that improved performance across all experimental conditions. TITLE: Learning to Execute

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) with Long Short-Term Memory units (LSTM) are widely used because they are expressive and are easy to train. Our interest lies in empirically evaluating the expressiveness and the learnability of LSTMs in the sequence-to-sequence regime by training them to evaluate short computer programs, a domain that has traditionally been seen as too complex for neural networks. We consider a simple class of programs that can be evaluated with a single left-to-right pass using constant memory. Ou

title: Learning to Play Guess Who? and Inventing a Grounded Language as a Consequence
searchable_text:
Learning to Play Guess Who? and Inventing a Grounded Language as a Consequence Interactive image search via the game Guess Who?, where agents communicate about images containing physical attributes. Use situated interactions between agents plus Deep Recurrent Q-Networks to learn a common language grounded in the game environment. Replaces corpus-only language learning with situated multi-agent interaction grounded in Guess Who? images using DRQN. TITLE: Learning to Play Guess Who? and Inventing a Grounded Language as a Consequence

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Learning your first language is an incredible feat and not easily duplicated. Doing this using nothing but a few pictureless books, a corpus, would likely be impossible even for humans. As an alternative we propose to use situated interactions between agents as a driving force for communication, and the framework of Deep Recurrent 
Q-Networks (DRQN) for learning a common language grounded in the provided environment. We task the agents with interactive imag

title: Learning to Protect Communications with Adversarial Neural Cryptography
searchable_text:
Learning to Protect Communications with Adversarial Neural Cryptography Confidentiality protection for communications in a multiagent neural-network system where an eavesdropper observes messages between legitimate parties. End-to-end adversarial training of neural agents Alice and Bob using secret keys, with Eve as an eavesdropping adversary, rather than prescribing cryptographic algorithms. The approach replaces hand-specified cryptographic algorithms with neural networks trained end-to-end in an adversarial setting. TITLE: Learning to Protect Communications with Adversarial Neural Cryptography

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We ask whether neural networks can learn to use secret keys to protect information from other neural networks. Specifically, we focus on ensuring confidentiality properties in a multiagent system, and we specify those properties in terms of an adversary. Thus, a system may consist of neural networks named Alice and Bob, and we aim to limit what a third neural network named Eve learns from eavesdropping on the communication between Alice and Bob. We do not prescrib

title: Learning to Solve Arithmetic Word Problems with Verb Categorization
searchable_text:
Learning to Solve Arithmetic Word Problems with Verb Categorization Solving simple arithmetic word problems from standard primary school test questions. Analyze each sentence to identify relevant variables and values, categorize verbs, and map extracted information into an equation for solution. Introduces learning results for arithmetic word problem solving without reliance on predefined templates, with publicly available data. TITLE: Learning to Solve Arithmetic Word Problems with Verb Categorization

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper presents a novel approach to learning to solve simple arithmetic word problems. Our system, ARIS, analyzes each of the sentences in the problem statement to identify the relevant variables and their values. ARIS then maps this information into an equation that represents the problem, and enables its (trivial) solution as shown in Figure 1. The paper analyzes the arithmetic-word problems “genre”, identifying seven categories of verbs used in such problems. A

title: Learning Trajectory Preferences for Manipulators via Iterative Improvement
searchable_text:
Learning Trajectory Preferences for Manipulators via Iterative Improvement Learning good trajectories for robotic manipulation tasks where user, task, object, and environment preferences define trajectory quality. A co-active online learning framework learns trajectory preferences from users who iteratively provide slight improvements to the robot’s currently proposed manipulation trajectory. Replaces demonstrations of optimal trajectories with co-active feedback: users only need to provide trajectories slightly better than the system’s current proposal. TITLE: Learning Trajectory Preferences for Manipulators via Iterative Improvement

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We consider the problem of learning good trajectories for manipulation tasks. This is challenging because the criterion defining a good trajectory varies with users, tasks and environments. In this paper, we propose a co-active online learning framework for teaching robots the preferences of its users for object manipulation tasks. The key novelty of our approach lies in the type of feedback expected from the user: the human user does not need to demonstrate op

title: Learning with a Wasserstein Loss
searchable_text:
Learning with a Wasserstein Loss Multi-label output prediction where labels have a natural metric, especially real-data tag prediction on Yahoo Flickr Creative Commons. Use a Wasserstein-distance-based loss for multi-label learning, optimized via an efficient regularized approximation and extended to unnormalized measures. Changed from exact Wasserstein over probability measures to an efficiently computed regularized loss plus a novel extension to unnormalized measures. TITLE: Learning with a Wasserstein Loss

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Learning to predict multi-label outputs is challenging, but in many problems there is a natural metric on the outputs that can be used to improve predictions. In this paper we develop a loss function for multi-label learning, based on the Wasserstein distance. The Wasserstein distance provides a natural notion of dissimilarity for probability measures. Although optimizing with respect to the exact Wasserstein distance is costly, recent work has described a regularized approximation that is efficiently

title: Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness
searchable_text:
Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness Non-stationary multi-agent learning settings including deep multi-agent reinforcement learning, hierarchical reinforcement learning, GANs, decentralised optimisation, iterated prisoners' dilemma, matching pennies, and social-dilemma grid worlds. Agents shape anticipated opponent learning by adding a term for how their policy affects other agents' parameter updates. Compared with independent learning, LOLA explicitly accounts for and shapes the other agents' anticipated learning updates. TITLE: Learning with Opponent-Learning Awareness

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Multi-agent settings are quickly gathering importance in machine learning. This includes a plethora of recent work on deep multi-agent reinforcement learning, but also can be extended to hierarchical reinforcement learning, generative adversarial networks and decentralised optimisation. In all these settings the presence of multiple learning agents renders the training problem non-stationary and often leads to unstable training or undesired final results. We present Learning with Opponent-Lear

title: Leave no Trace: Learning to Reset for Safe and Autonomous Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Leave no Trace: Learning to Reset for Safe and Autonomous Reinforcement Learning Safe autonomous reinforcement learning for real-world tasks, especially robotics, where repeated trials require environment resets that may be difficult or not automatically reversible. Simultaneously learn a forward task policy and a reset policy, using the reset value function to detect irreversible states and trigger uncertainty-aware safety aborts. Instead of relying on human resets between attempts, the method adds a learned reset policy and reset value function to make trial repetition safer and more autonomous. TITLE: Leave no Trace: Learning to Reset for Safe and Autonomous Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep reinforcement learning algorithms can learn complex behavioral skills, but real-world application of these methods requires a large amount of experience to be collected by the agent. In practical settings, such as robotics, this involves repeatedly attempting a task, resetting the environment between each attempt. However, not all tasks are easily or automatically reversible. In practice, this learning process requires extensive human intervention. I

title: Linear Convergence of Gradient and Proximal-Gradient Methods Under the Polyak-Łojasiewicz Condition
searchable_text:
Linear Convergence of Gradient and Proximal-Gradient Methods Under the Polyak-Łojasiewicz Condition Linear convergence analysis for gradient and proximal-gradient optimization methods without requiring strong convexity or convexity, across machine-learning objectives. Use the Polyak-Łojasiewicz inequality, plus a proximal-gradient generalization, to prove linear convergence for gradient, coordinate, stochastic, variance-reduced, sign-based, and nonsmooth proximal-gradient methods. Replaces reliance on newer linear-convergence conditions with the older PL inequality and extends it to nonsmooth proximal-gradient settings. TITLE: Linear Convergence of Gradient and Proximal-Gradient Methods Under the Polyak-Łojasiewicz Condition

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In 1963, Polyak proposed a simple condition that is sufficient to show a global linear convergence rate for gradient descent. This condition is a special case of the \L{}ojasiewicz inequality proposed in the same year, and it does not require strong convexity (or even convexity). In this work, we show that this much-older Polyak-\L{}ojasiewicz (PL) inequality is actually weaker than the main conditions that have been explored to show li

title: Linear Off-Policy Actor-Critic
searchable_text:
Linear Off-Policy Actor-Critic Off-policy reinforcement learning where a target policy is learned from behavior-policy data, especially when explicit stochastic policies or large action spaces are needed. An online incremental off-policy actor-critic algorithm using gradient temporal-difference learning, linear function approximation, and eligibility traces with linear time and space complexity. Extended actor-critic methods from on-policy learning to off-policy learning by incorporating off-policy gradient temporal-difference advances. TITLE: Linear Off-Policy Actor-Critic

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper presents the first actor-critic algorithm for off-policy reinforcement learning. Our algorithm is online and incremental, and its per-time-step complexity scales linearly with the number of learned weights. Previous work on actor-critic algorithms is limited to the on-policy setting and does not take advantage of the recent advances in off-policy gradient temporal-difference learning. Off-policy techniques, such as Greedy-GQ, enable a target policy to be learned while following and obtaining d

title: Local Matching Indicators for Transport Problems with Concave Costs
searchable_text:
Local Matching Indicators for Transport Problems with Concave Costs Optimal transport for arbitrary weighted one-dimensional demand and supply distributions with concave costs, including unbalanced cases. Hierarchical use of local matching indicators to efficiently construct optimal transport plans for concave cost functions on the line. Extends beyond convex-distance or Monge-matrix settings by introducing local indicators tailored to concave transport costs. TITLE: Local Matching Indicators for Transport Problems with Concave Costs

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we introduce a class of local indicators that enable us to compute efficiently optimal transport plans associated with arbitrary weighted distributions of $N$ demands and $M$ supplies in $\mathbb{R}$ in the case where the cost function is concave. Indeed, whereas this problem can be solved linearly when the cost is a convex function of the distance on the line (or more generally when the cost matrix between points is a Monge matrix), to the best of our

title: Logical error rate in the Pauli twirling approximation
searchable_text:
Logical error rate in the Pauli twirling approximation Predicting logical error rates for quantum error correction protocols using classically efficient physical error models. Simulate the 5-qubit code in a 9-qubit circuit, comparing exact realistic decoherence and unitary gate errors against the Pauli twirling approximation. Shifted PTA evaluation from physical-channel simplification to direct testing of logical error-rate prediction under realistic circuit-level errors. TITLE: Logical error rate in the Pauli twirling approximation

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The performance of error correction protocols are necessary for understanding the operation of potential quantum computers, but this requires physical error models that can be simulated efficiently with classical computers. The Gottesmann-Knill theorem guarantees a class of such error models. Of these, one of the simplest is the Pauli twirling approximation (PTA), which is obtained by twirling an arbitrary completely positive error channel over the Pauli basis, resulting in a Paul

title: Low-distance Surface Codes under Realistic Quantum Noise
searchable_text:
Low-distance Surface Codes under Realistic Quantum Noise Performance and implementation targets for low-distance surface codes under realistic quantum noise in early experimental quantum computing architectures. Monte-Carlo evaluation of distance-three surface code layouts under depolarizing, Pauli-twirled, and amplitude/phase damping noise models to estimate thresholds and logical error rates. Moves beyond depolarizing-only or Pauli-twirl estimates by directly comparing twirled amplitude/phase damping with amplitude and phase damping under realistic multi-parameter noise. TITLE: Low-distance Surface Codes under Realistic Quantum Noise

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We study the performance of distance-three surface code layouts under realistic multi-parameter noise models. We first calculate their thresholds under depolarizing noise. We then compare a Pauli-twirl approximation of amplitude and phase damping to amplitude and phase damping. We find the approximate channel results in a pessimistic estimate of the logical error rate, indicating the realistic threshold may be higher than previously estimated. From Monte-Carlo simulations, we id

title: Lower bounds for finding stationary points I
searchable_text:
Lower bounds for finding stationary points I Finding ε-stationary points of smooth, high-dimensional, potentially non-convex functions. Oracle-based lower-bound construction using value-and-derivative queries, proving hard functions with Lipschitz pth-order derivatives.  TITLE: Lower bounds for finding stationary points I

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

title: LSDSem 2017 Shared Task: The Story Cloze Test
searchable_text:
LSDSem 2017 Shared Task: The Story Cloze Test Story understanding and script learning for short narratives requiring selection of the correct story ending. Use the Story Cloze Test: give a system a four-sentence story plus two possible endings, and score whether it chooses the correct ending. Introduces the Story Cloze Test as a new shared-task evaluation for story understanding and script learning. TITLE: LSDSem 2017 Shared Task: The Story Cloze Test

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The LSDSem’17 shared task is the Story Cloze Test, a new evaluation for story understanding and script learning. This test provides a system with a four-sentence story and two possible endings, and the system must choose the correct ending to the story. Successful narrative understanding (getting closer to human performance of 100%) requires systems to link various levels of semantics to commonsense knowledge. A total of eight systems participated in the shared task, with a variety of appr

title: Making the V in VQA Matter: Elevating the Role of Image Understanding in Visual Question Answering
searchable_text:
Making the V in VQA Matter: Elevating the Role of Image Understanding in Visual Question Answering Visual question answering datasets and benchmarks where question-answer language biases let systems answer without genuine image understanding. Balance VQA by collecting complementary image pairs: similar images tied to the same question but yielding different answers, forcing models to use visual evidence rather than language priors. Compared with the original VQA dataset, each question is paired with two similar images producing different answers, yielding a more balanced dataset with about twice as many image-question pairs. TITLE: Making the V in VQA Matter: Elevating the Role of Image Understanding in Visual Question Answering

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The problem of visual question answering (VQA) is of significant importance both as a challenging research question and for the rich set of applications it enables. In this context, however, inherent structure in our world and bias in our language tend to be a simpler signal for learning than visual modalities, resulting in VQA models that ignore visual information, leading to an inflated sense of their capability. We propose to counter

title: Mapping dark matter on the celestial sphere with weak gravitational lensing
searchable_text:
Mapping dark matter on the celestial sphere with weak gravitational lensing Weak gravitational lensing convergence mapping of the integrated matter distribution across wide-area celestial-sphere surveys. Extend Kaiser-Squires weak-lensing mass mapping from the plane to the sphere, recovering convergence fields directly on the celestial sphere while accounting for projection rotations of spin shear fields. Changed Kaiser-Squires convergence recovery from a planar-only technique to a spherical method, replacing projection-dependent planar mass mapping for wide-sky weak-lensing surveys. TITLE: Mapping dark matter on the celestial sphere with weak gravitational lensing

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Convergence maps of the integrated matter distribution are a key science result from weak gravitational lensing surveys. To date, recovering convergence maps has been performed using a planar approximation of the celestial sphere. However, with the increasing area of sky covered by dark energy experiments, such as Euclid, the Large Synoptic Survey Telescope (LSST), and the Wide Field Infrared Survey Telescope (WFIRST), this assumption will no longer be valid.

title: Mapping how local perturbations influence systems-level brain dynamics
searchable_text:
Mapping how local perturbations influence systems-level brain dynamics Predicting how localized cortical stimulation changes large-scale brain activity, functional connectivity, and network stability across core-periphery brain systems. Systematic computational analysis of a large-scale brain-dynamics model, locally perturbing different cortical sites to assess whole-brain dynamics and functional-connectivity effects along a hierarchy of intrinsic timescales. Shifted from reviewing or isolated perturbation studies to a systematic, site-by-site large-scale model linking stimulation effectiveness to cortical hierarchy and intrinsic timescale organization. TITLE: Mapping how local perturbations influence systems-level brain dynamics

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
&NA; The human brain exhibits a distinct spatiotemporal organization that supports brain function and can be manipulated via local brain stimulation. Such perturbations to local cortical dynamics are globally integrated by distinct neural systems. However, it remains unclear how local changes in neural activity affect large‐scale system dynamics. Here, we briefly review empirical and computational studies addressing how localized perturbations affect brain activity. We then

title: Marginal Release Under Local Differential Privacy
searchable_text:
Marginal Release Under Local Differential Privacy Marginal statistics for multidimensional datasets with strong privacy guarantees, supporting analysis, machine learning, modeling, and correlation testing. Algorithms materialize marginal statistics under local differential privacy, especially by releasing information based on local Fourier transformations of the input. Introduces the first tight theoretical accuracy bounds for marginals compiled under each local differential privacy approach. TITLE: Marginal Release Under Local Differential Privacy

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many analysis and machine learning tasks require the availability of marginal statistics on multidimensional datasets while providing strong privacy guarantees for the data subjects. Applications for these statistics range from finding correlations in the data to fitting sophisticated prediction models. In this paper, we provide a set of algorithms for materializing marginal statistics under the strong model of local differential privacy. We prove the first tight theoretical bounds on

title: Markov-Nash equilibria in mean-field games with discounted cost
searchable_text:
Markov-Nash equilibria in mean-field games with discounted cost Discrete-time mean-field dynamic games with finite agents, locally compact Polish state spaces, empirical-distribution coupling, and infinite-horizon discounted costs. Use Markov-Nash equilibrium: player-by-player optimality restricted to Markov policies, derived via mean-field equilibrium and transferred to large finite-agent games. Introduces Markov-Nash equilibrium for discounted-cost mean-field games and links infinite-population mean-field equilibria to approximate finite-population Markov-Nash behavior. TITLE: Markov-Nash equilibria in mean-field games with discounted cost

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we consider discrete-time dynamic games of the mean-field type with a finite number, N, of agents subject to an infinite-horizon discounted-cost optimality criterion. The state space of each agent is a locally compact Polish space. At each time, the agents are coupled through the empirical distribution of their states, which affects both the agents' individual costs and their state transition probabilities. We introduce the solution concept of Markov-Nash e

title: Mask R-CNN
searchable_text:
Mask R-CNN Object instance segmentation and instance-level recognition, including bounding-box object detection and person keypoint detection. Extends Faster R-CNN with a parallel branch that predicts an object mask alongside bounding-box recognition. Adds an object-mask prediction branch to Faster R-CNN in parallel with its existing bounding-box recognition branch. TITLE: Mask R-CNN

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a conceptually simple, flexible, and general framework for object instance segmentation. Our approach efficiently detects objects in an image while simultaneously generating a high-quality segmentation mask for each instance. The method, called Mask R-CNN, extends Faster R-CNN by adding a branch for predicting an object mask in parallel with the existing branch for bounding box recognition. Mask R-CNN is simple to train and adds only a small overhead to Faster R-CNN, running at 5 fps. Moreover, Mask R-CNN is easy

title: Masked Autoregressive Flow for Density Estimation
searchable_text:
Masked Autoregressive Flow for Density Estimation General-purpose neural density estimation tasks. Construct a stack of autoregressive models, each modelling the random numbers used internally by the next, yielding a Masked Autoregressive Flow normalizing flow. Changes autoregressive density estimation by modelling each model’s internal random numbers in a stacked flow; the resulting MAF is closely related to IAF and generalizes Real NVP. TITLE: Masked Autoregressive Flow for Density Estimation

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Autoregressive models are among the best performing neural density estimators. We describe an approach for increasing the flexibility of an autoregressive model, based on modelling the random numbers that the model uses internally when generating data. By constructing a stack of autoregressive models, each modelling the random numbers of the next model in the stack, we obtain a type of normalizing flow suitable for density estimation, which we call Masked Autoregressive Flow. This type

title: Mastering Chess and Shogi by Self-Play with a General Reinforcement Learning Algorithm
searchable_text:
Mastering Chess and Shogi by Self-Play with a General Reinforcement Learning Algorithm Superhuman play in challenging perfect-information board games: chess, shogi, and Go. Tabula rasa reinforcement learning through self-play in a single general AlphaZero algorithm, using only game rules rather than handcrafted evaluation or domain-specific adaptations. Generalised AlphaGo Zero’s tabula rasa self-play approach from Go into one algorithm applicable to chess, shogi, and Go without domain knowledge beyond rules. TITLE: Mastering Chess and Shogi by Self-Play with a General Reinforcement Learning Algorithm

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The game of chess is the most widely-studied domain in the history of artificial intelligence. The strongest programs are based on a combination of sophisticated search techniques, domain-specific adaptations, and handcrafted evaluation functions that have been refined by human experts over several decades. In contrast, the AlphaGo Zero program recently achieved superhuman performance in the game of Go, by tabula rasa reinforcement learning from gam

title: Matched filtering of gravitational waves from inspiraling compact binaries: Computational cost and template placement
searchable_text:
Matched filtering of gravitational waves from inspiraling compact binaries: Computational cost and template placement Estimating template counts, computational power, storage, and template placement for one-step gravitational-wave searches across interferometer detectors. Discrete two-parameter matched-filter template bank using second post-Newtonian inspiral waveforms for nonspinning compact binaries in circular orbits. Costs exceed Owen’s earlier estimates because this work uses improved templates and more realistic higher sampling rates. TITLE: Matched filtering of gravitational waves from inspiraling compact binaries: Computational cost and template placement

YEAR: 1998

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We estimate the number of templates, computational power, and storage required for a one-step matched filtering search for gravitational waves from inspiraling compact binaries. Our estimates for the one-step search strategy should serve as benchmarks for the evaluation of more sophisticated strategies such as hierarchical searches. We use a discrete family of two-parameter wave form templates based on the second post-

title: Matrix-valued Monge-Kantorovich optimal mass transport
searchable_text:
Matrix-valued Monge-Kantorovich optimal mass transport Optimal transport for matrix-valued density functions in spectral analysis of multivariable time-series energy across frequencies. Formulate matrix-valued optimal transport using transportation plans in the tensor product of marginal spaces, with costs for frequency transport and rotation. Extends classical Monge-Kantorovich transport from scalar marginals to matrix-valued marginals, adding rotation cost and tensor-product transportation plans. TITLE: Matrix-valued Monge-Kantorovich optimal mass transport

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We formulate an optimal transport problem for matrix-valued density functions. This is pertinent in the spectral analysis of multivariable time-series. The “mass” represents energy at various frequencies whereas, in addition to a usual transportation cost across frequencies, a cost of rotation is also taken into account. We show that it is natural to seek the transportation plan in the tensor product of the spaces for the two matrix-valued marginals. In contrast to the classical M

title: MAWPS: A Math Word Problem Repository
searchable_text:
MAWPS: A Math Word Problem Repository Evaluation testbed and dataset repository for automatic math word problem solving algorithms across AI subdisciplines. An online math word problem repository enabling automatic dataset construction, lexical/template overlap tuning, grammatical filtering, and community contribution. Changed from separate prior datasets to a unified, online, contributable repository with configurable dataset construction and overlap controls. TITLE: MAWPS: A Math Word Problem Repository

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recent work across several AI subdisciplines has focused on automatically solving math word problems. In this paper we introduce MAWPS, an online repository of Math Word Problems, to provide a unified testbed to evaluate different algorithms. MAWPS allows for the automatic construction of datasets with particular characteristics, providing tools for tuning the lexical and template overlap of a dataset as well as for filtering ungrammatical problems from web-sourced corpora. The online nature of th

title: MCMC Methods for Functions: ModifyingOld Algorithms to Make Them Faster
searchable_text:
MCMC Methods for Functions: ModifyingOld Algorithms to Make Them Faster Sampling probability distributions over functions, especially Bayesian nonparametrics, conditioned diffusions, and inverse problems with Gaussian process or random field reference measures. Design function-space MCMC proposals from time-discretized stochastic dynamics that exactly preserve a Gaussian reference measure, yielding mesh-robust modifications of existing algorithms. Changed finite-dimensional MCMC into function-space-valid algorithms by replacing generic proposals with Gaussian-reference-preserving stochastic-dynamics discretizations. TITLE: MCMC Methods for Functions: ModifyingOld Algorithms to Make Them Faster

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many problems arising in applications result in the need to probe a probability distribution for functions. Examples include Bayesian nonparametric statistics and conditioned diffusion processes. Standard MCMC algorithms typically become arbitrarily slow under the mesh refinement dictated by nonparametric description of the un- known function. We describe an approach to modifying a whole range of MCMC methods, applicable whenever the target measure has density wit

title: MEASURING TRANSIT SIGNAL RECOVERY IN THE KEPLER PIPELINE. III. COMPLETENESS OF THE Q1–Q17 DR24 PLANET CANDIDATE CATALOG WITH IMPORTANT CAVEATS FOR OCCURRENCE RATE CALCULATIONS
searchable_text:
MEASURING TRANSIT SIGNAL RECOVERY IN THE KEPLER PIPELINE. III. COMPLETENESS OF THE Q1–Q17 DR24 PLANET CANDIDATE CATALOG WITH IMPORTANT CAVEATS FOR OCCURRENCE RATE CALCULATIONS Completeness and detection efficiency of the Kepler Q1–Q17 DR24 planet candidate catalog for planet occurrence rate calculations. Inject simulated transiting planets into pixel-level data for 159,013 Kepler targets, then measure pipeline v9.2 recovery rates to estimate detection sensitivity. Compared with previous Kepler pipeline versions, DR24/v9.2 introduces strong period dependence where prior sensitivity could be treated as one-dimensional in signal strength. TITLE: MEASURING TRANSIT SIGNAL RECOVERY IN THE KEPLER PIPELINE. III. COMPLETENESS OF THE Q1–Q17 DR24 PLANET CANDIDATE CATALOG WITH IMPORTANT CAVEATS FOR OCCURRENCE RATE CALCULATIONS

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
With each new version of the Kepler pipeline and resulting planet candidate catalog, an updated measurement of the underlying planet population can only be recovered with a corresponding measurement of the Kepler pipeline detection efficiency. Here we present measurements of the sensitivity of the pipeline (version 9.2) used to generate the Q1–Q17 DR24 planet ca

title: Memory Networks
searchable_text:
Memory Networks Question answering where long-term memory acts as a dynamic knowledge base and the system outputs textual responses. Inference components combined with a long-term memory component that can be read and written to, learned jointly for prediction. Introduced a new class of learning models combining inference with readable/writable long-term memory learned jointly. TITLE: Memory Networks

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Abstract: We describe a new class of learning models called memory networks. Memory networks reason with inference components combined with a long-term memory component; they learn how to use these jointly. The long-term memory can be read and written to, with the goal of using it for prediction. We investigate these models in the context of question answering (QA) where the long-term memory effectively acts as a (dynamic) knowledge base, and the output is a textual response. We evaluate them on a large-scale QA task,

title: Message-passing algorithms for compressed sensing
searchable_text:
Message-passing algorithms for compressed sensing Reconstructing sufficiently sparse high-dimensional signals from undersampled compressed-sensing measurements. Costless belief-propagation-inspired modification to iterative thresholding, with state evolution predicting sparsity–undersampling tradeoffs. Compared with standard fast iterative thresholding, add a simple costless modification that makes the tradeoff equivalent to corresponding convex optimization procedures. TITLE: Message-passing algorithms for compressed sensing

YEAR: 2009

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Compressed sensing aims to undersample certain high-dimensional signals yet accurately reconstruct them by exploiting signal characteristics. Accurate reconstruction is possible when the object to be recovered is sufficiently sparse in a known basis. Currently, the best known sparsity–undersampling tradeoff is achieved when reconstructing by convex optimization, which is expensive in important large-scale applications. Fast iterative thresholding algorithms have been intensively studie

title: Metastability in a Stochastic Neural Network Modeled as a Velocity Jump Markov Process
searchable_text:
Metastability in a Stochastic Neural Network Modeled as a Velocity Jump Markov Process Rare transitions between metastable states in bistable stochastic neural population networks where intrinsic population noise affects macroscopic dynamics. Model neural population dynamics as a velocity jump Markov process with piecewise deterministic synaptic variables and discrete stochastic spiking states analyzed via WKB and singular perturbation theory. Compared with the neural master equation, the model adds explicit synaptic processing dynamics through a velocity jump Markov process while retaining spiking-state stochasticity. TITLE: Metastability in a Stochastic Neural Network Modeled as a Velocity Jump Markov Process

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
One of the major challenges in neuroscience is to determine how noise that is present at the molecular and cellular levels affects dynamics and information processing at the macroscopic level of synapti- cally coupled neuronal populations. Often noise is incorporated into deterministic network models using extrinsic noise sources. An alternative approach is to assume that noise arises intrinsically as a collective population effect, which has led to a master

title: Method for detection and reconstruction of gravitational wave transients with networks of advanced detectors
searchable_text:
Method for detection and reconstruction of gravitational wave transients with networks of advanced detectors Detection, reconstruction, rapid localization, and source-origin identification of gravitational-wave transients in networks of advanced detectors. Analytic GW transient detection/reconstruction using improved network algorithms and polarization reconstruction from detector-response patterns. Compared with the initial-detector transient search, the method uses significantly improved algorithms and adds a novel polarization-reconstruction approach. TITLE: Method for detection and reconstruction of gravitational wave transients with networks of advanced detectors

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a method for detection and reconstruction of the gravitational-wave (GW) transients with the networks of advanced detectors. Originally designed to search for transients with the initial GW detectors, it uses signicantly improved algorithms, which enhances both the low-latency searches with rapid localization of GW events for the electro-magnetic followup and high condence detection of a broad range of the transient

title: Metrics for Matrix-valued Measures via Test Functions
searchable_text:
Metrics for Matrix-valued Measures via Test Functions Comparing matrix-valued measures, especially matrix-valued power spectral density functions. Use dual comparisons of functionals against suitable test-function families to define a weakly-continuous Wasserstein-analog metric for matrix-valued densities. Introduces a test-function-based weakly-continuous Wasserstein-analog metric for matrix-valued densities, compared against standard alternatives and a prior matricial Wasserstein metric. TITLE: Metrics for Matrix-valued Measures via Test Functions

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
It is perhaps not widely recognized that certain common notions of distance between probability measures have an alternative dual interpretation which compares corresponding functionals against suitable families of test functions. This dual viewpoint extends in a straightforward manner to suggest metrics between matrix-valued measures. Our main interest has been in developing weakly-continuous metrics that are suitable for comparing matrix-valued power spectral density functions. T

title: Microsoft COCO Captions: Data Collection and Evaluation Server
searchable_text:
Microsoft COCO Captions: Data Collection and Evaluation Server Large-scale image caption dataset creation and standardized evaluation of automatic caption generation algorithms. Provide five independent human captions for training/validation images and an evaluation server scoring candidate captions with BLEU, METEOR, ROUGE, and CIDEr.  TITLE: Microsoft COCO Captions: Data Collection and Evaluation Server

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper we describe the Microsoft COCO Caption dataset and evaluation server. When completed, the dataset will contain over one and a half million captions describing over 330,000 images. For the training and validation images, five independent human generated captions will be provided. To ensure consistency in evaluation of automatic caption generation algorithms, an evaluation server is used. The evaluation server receives candidate captions and scores them using s

title: Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context
searchable_text:
Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context Object recognition and scene understanding for common objects in complex everyday images. Gather images of complex everyday scenes with common objects in natural context, labeled via per-instance segmentations using crowd-worker interfaces for category detection, instance spotting, and segmentation. Compared with PASCAL, ImageNet, and SUN, the dataset emphasizes common objects in natural scene context with per-instance segmentation at large scale. TITLE: Microsoft COCO: Common Objects in Context

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a new dataset with the goal of advancing the state-of-the-art in object recognition by placing the question of object recognition in the context of the broader question of scene understanding. This is achieved by gathering images of complex everyday scenes containing common objects in their natural context. Objects are labeled using per-instance segmentations to aid in precise object localization. Our dataset contains photos of 91 objects types that would be easily recognizable by a

title: Minimizing finite sums with the stochastic average gradient
searchable_text:
Minimizing finite sums with the stochastic average gradient Optimizing finite sums of smooth convex functions, including strongly-convex finite-sum objectives. Stochastic average gradient optimization: stochastic finite-sum updates augmented with memory of previously computed gradients to accelerate convergence. Compared with earlier SAG work, the faster-rate guarantee expands beyond well-conditioned strongly-convex problems to general convex and strongly-convex finite sums. TITLE: Minimizing finite sums with the stochastic average gradient

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We analyze the stochastic average gradient (SAG) method for optimizing the sum of a finite number of smooth convex functions. Like stochastic gradient (SG) methods, the SAG method’s iteration cost is independent of the number of terms in the sum. However, by incorporating a memory of previous gradient values the SAG method achieves a faster convergence rate than black-box SG methods. The convergence rate is improved from O(1/k)\documentclass[12pt]{minimal} \usepackage{amsmath

title: Mixed Precision Training
searchable_text:
Mixed Precision Training Training large deep neural networks with reduced memory and compute requirements across CNNs, RNNs, GANs, and models exceeding 100 million parameters. Train with half-precision weights, activations, and gradients while maintaining single-precision master weights and applying loss scaling to preserve gradient information. Replaces full single-precision training storage with half-precision tensors plus a single-precision weight copy and scaled loss. TITLE: Mixed Precision Training

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep neural networks have enabled progress in a wide variety of applications. Growing the size of the neural network typically results in improved accuracy. As model sizes grow, the memory and compute requirements for training these models also increases. We introduce a technique to train deep neural networks using half precision floating point numbers. In our technique, weights, activations and gradients are stored in IEEE half-precision format. Half-precision floating numbers have limited numerical range com

title: mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization
searchable_text:
mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization Improving generalization and robustness of large deep neural networks prone to memorization and adversarial sensitivity. Train neural networks on convex combinations of pairs of examples and their labels, encouraging linear behavior between training samples. Moves beyond empirical risk minimization by training on interpolated example-label pairs rather than only observed training examples. TITLE: mixup: Beyond Empirical Risk Minimization

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Large deep neural networks are powerful, but exhibit undesirable behaviors such as memorization and sensitivity to adversarial examples. In this work, we propose mixup, a simple learning principle to alleviate these issues. In essence, mixup trains a neural network on convex combinations of pairs of examples and their labels. By doing so, mixup regularizes the neural network to favor simple linear behavior in-between training examples. Our experiments on the ImageNet-2012, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100,

title: MoCoGAN: Decomposing Motion and Content for Video Generation
searchable_text:
MoCoGAN: Decomposing Motion and Content for Video Generation Video generation where visual signals require separate control over objects/content and their temporal dynamics/motion. Generate videos by decomposing latent vectors into fixed content and stochastic motion, trained unsupervised with both image and video discriminators. Compared with prior video generation approaches, MoCoGAN explicitly decomposes random vectors into content and motion and adds an adversarial scheme using both image and video discriminators. TITLE: MoCoGAN: Decomposing Motion and Content for Video Generation

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Visual signals in a video can be divided into content and motion. While content specifies which objects are in the video, motion describes their dynamics. Based on this prior, we propose the Motion and Content decomposed Generative Adversarial Network (MoCoGAN) framework for video generation. The proposed framework generates a video by mapping a sequence of random vectors to a sequence of video frames. Each random vector consists of a content part and a motion part. While th

title: Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Deep Networks
searchable_text:
Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Deep Networks Fast adaptation to new learning tasks with few training samples across classification, regression, and reinforcement learning. Train initial model parameters so a few gradient steps on small new-task data yield strong generalization; the method is model-agnostic for gradient-descent-trained models. Meta-learning is framed as learning parameters explicitly optimized to be easy to fine-tune, while remaining compatible with any gradient-descent-trained model. TITLE: Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning for Fast Adaptation of Deep Networks

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose an algorithm for meta-learning that is model-agnostic, in the sense that it is compatible with any model trained with gradient descent and applicable to a variety of different learning problems, including classification, regression, and reinforcement learning. The goal of meta-learning is to train a model on a variety of learning tasks, such that it can solve new learning tasks using only a small number of training samples. In our approach, the parameters of

title: Model-driven Development of Complex Software: A Research Roadmap
searchable_text:
Model-driven Development of Complex Software: A Research Roadmap Complex software development where evolving software complexity must be managed through higher-level engineering abstractions. Create abstract software-system models and systematically transform them into concrete implementations. Reframes MDE from an immediate implementation paradigm into a research roadmap emphasizing challenges, wicked problems, and partial value from pursuing the vision. TITLE: Model-driven Development of Complex Software: A Research Roadmap

YEAR: 2007

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The term model-driven engineering (MDE) is typically used to describe software development approaches in which abstract models of software systems are created and systematically transformed to concrete implementations. In this paper we give an overview of current research in MDE and discuss some of the major challenges that must be tackled in order to realize the MDE vision of software development. We argue that full realizations of the MDE vision may not be possible in

title: Modeling Relational Data with Graph Convolutional Networks
searchable_text:
Modeling Relational Data with Graph Convolutional Networks Knowledge base completion: link prediction for missing subject-predicate-object triples and entity classification for missing entity attributes. Relational Graph Convolutional Networks operate on graph-structured data and are specialized for highly multi-relational knowledge bases. Extends graph neural networks to highly multi-relational knowledge bases and uses an R-GCN encoder before factorization decoders such as DistMult. TITLE: Modeling Relational Data with Graph Convolutional Networks

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Knowledge graphs enable a wide variety of applications, including question answering and information retrieval. Despite the great effort invested in their creation and maintenance, even the largest (e.g., Yago, DBPedia or Wikidata) remain incomplete. We introduce Relational Graph Convolutional Networks (R-GCNs) and apply them to two standard knowledge base completion tasks: Link prediction (recovery of missing facts, i.e. subject-predicate-object triples) and entity classifica

title: Modern Cosmology
searchable_text:
Modern Cosmology Origin and evolution of the universe, including cosmic acceleration, microwave background anisotropies, and formation of galaxies and galaxy clusters. Review-based synthesis of modern cosmological evidence on acceleration, microwave background anisotropies, and structure formation from inflationary primordial fluctuations. Emphasis shifts to the most recent observations of cosmic acceleration, precise microwave background measurements, and inflation-seeded structure formation. TITLE: Modern Cosmology

YEAR: 2004

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
I review in these lectures our present understanding of the origin and evolution of the universe, making emphasis on the most recent observations of the acceleration of the universe, the precise measurements of the microwave background anisotropies, and the formation of structure like galaxies and clusters of galaxies from tiny primodial fluctuations generated during inflation.

title: Molecular Generation with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
searchable_text:
Molecular Generation with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) Generating novel, chemically sensible and synthesizable drug-like or fragment-like small molecules for virtual libraries in in silico drug discovery. Train a recurrent neural network with long short-term memory cells on existing compounds encoded as SMILES to learn chemical rules and generate molecules. Replaced hand-crafted chemical rules and fragment-based generation with an LSTM RNN trained directly on SMILES representations of existing compounds. TITLE: Molecular Generation with Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The potential number of drug like small molecules is estimated to be between 10^23 and 10^60 while current databases of known compounds are orders of magnitude smaller with approximately 10^8 compounds. This discrepancy has led to an interest in generating virtual libraries using hand crafted chemical rules and fragment based methods to cover a larger area of chemical space and generate chemical libraries for use in in silico drug discovery endeavors.
