title: Moment Tensor Potentials: A Class of Systematically Improvable Interatomic Potentials
searchable_text:
Moment Tensor Potentials: A Class of Systematically Improvable Interatomic Potentials Approximating quantum-mechanical interaction models with interatomic potentials for large-scale molecular materials simulations. Construct Moment Tensor Potentials as a systematically improvable class of interatomic potentials approximating a given quantum-mechanical interaction model. Introduces a new systematically improvable class of interatomic potentials, Moment Tensor Potentials, analyzed mathematically and tested on a quantum-mechanical database. TITLE: Moment Tensor Potentials: A Class of Systematically Improvable Interatomic Potentials

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Density functional theory offers a very accurate way of computing materials properties from first principles. However, it is too expensive for modelling large-scale molecular systems whose properties are, in contrast, computed using interatomic potentials. 
The present paper considers, from a mathematical point of view, the problem of constructing interatomic potentials that approximate a given quantum-mechanical interaction model. In particular, a n

title: More than a million ways to be pushed. A high-fidelity experimental dataset of planar pushing
searchable_text:
More than a million ways to be pushed. A high-fidelity experimental dataset of planar pushing Experimental understanding and benchmarking of how objects move under planar pushing in robotic manipulation. Automated industrial-robot planar pushing experiments capture time-stamped object and pusher poses plus interaction forces across controlled position-velocity-acceleration trajectories. Moves from reliance on approximate pushing models to a comprehensive, high-fidelity experimental dataset with dense, uniform measurements of positions and forces. TITLE: More than a million ways to be pushed. A high-fidelity experimental dataset of planar pushing

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Pushing is a motion primitive useful to handle objects that are too large, too heavy, or too cluttered to be grasped. It is at the core of much of robotic manipulation, in particular when physical interaction is involved. It seems reasonable then to wish for robots to understand how pushed objects move. In reality, however, robots often rely on approximations which yield models that are computable, but also restricted and inaccurate. Just how

title: MS MARCO: A Human Generated MAchine Reading COmprehension Dataset
searchable_text:
MS MARCO: A Human Generated MAchine Reading COmprehension Dataset Machine reading comprehension and question answering over real-world web search queries. Construct a large-scale dataset from real anonymized user queries, Bing-retrieved web passages, human-generated answers, and some queries with multiple answers. Compared with prior public reading-comprehension datasets, MS MARCO adds real anonymized queries, Bing web passages, human-generated answers, multiple-answer cases, and much larger scale. TITLE: MS MARCO: A Human Generated MAchine Reading COmprehension Dataset

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper presents our recent work on the design and development of a new, large scale dataset, which we name MS MARCO, for MAchine Reading COmprehension. This new dataset is aimed to overcome a number of well-known weaknesses of previous publicly available datasets for the same task of reading comprehension and question answering. In MS MARCO, all questions are sampled from real anonymized user queries. The context passages, from which answers in the dataset are deriv

title: Multi-Agent Actor-Critic for Mixed Cooperative-Competitive Environments
searchable_text:
Multi-Agent Actor-Critic for Mixed Cooperative-Competitive Environments Deep reinforcement learning in mixed cooperative-competitive multi-agent environments requiring coordination among agent populations. Actor-critic adapted to condition on other agents’ action policies, plus ensemble-policy training for robustness. Compared with traditional Q-learning and policy-gradient methods, the approach explicitly considers other agents’ policies and trains ensembles per agent. TITLE: Multi-Agent Actor-Critic for Mixed Cooperative-Competitive Environments

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We explore deep reinforcement learning methods for multi-agent domains. We begin by analyzing the difficulty of traditional algorithms in the multi-agent case: Q-learning is challenged by an inherent non-stationarity of the environment, while policy gradient suffers from a variance that increases as the number of agents grows. We then present an adaptation of actor-critic methods that considers action policies of other agents and is able to successfully learn poli

title: Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning in Sequential Social Dilemmas
searchable_text:
Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning in Sequential Social Dilemmas Temporally extended cooperation and defection in multi-agent social dilemmas, especially fruit Gathering and Wolfpack hunting under varying environmental factors such as resource abundance. Introduce sequential social dilemmas modeled as Markov games, analyzing multiple self-interested independent agents that learn policies via separate deep Q-networks. Moves beyond matrix-game social dilemmas where cooperation or defection is atomic, replacing them with temporally extended policy learning in sequential Markov games. TITLE: Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning in Sequential Social Dilemmas

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Matrix games like Prisoner's Dilemma have guided research on social dilemmas for decades. However, they necessarily treat the choice to cooperate or defect as an atomic action. In real-world social dilemmas these choices are temporally extended. Cooperativeness is a property that applies to policies, not elementary actions. We introduce sequential social dilemmas that share the mixed incentive structure of matrix game social dilemmas but also require agents to learn poli

title: Multi-column deep neural networks for image classification
searchable_text:
Multi-column deep neural networks for image classification Image classification benchmarks requiring human-level visual recognition, especially handwritten digit recognition and traffic sign recognition. Wide, deep convolutional winner-take-all neural columns with small receptive fields; only winners are trained, columns specialize on differently preprocessed inputs, and averaged predictions form the classifier. Uses multiple wide, deep convolutional winner-take-all neural columns trained on differently preprocessed inputs, averaging predictions, instead of traditional computer-vision and machine-learning methods. TITLE: Multi-column deep neural networks for image classification

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Traditional methods of computer vision and machine learning cannot match human performance on tasks such as the recognition of handwritten digits or traffic signs. Our biologically plausible, wide and deep artificial neural network architectures can. Small (often minimal) receptive fields of convolutional winner-take-all neurons yield large network depth, resulting in roughly as many sparsely connected neural layers as found in mammals between retina and visual cortex. Only wi

title: Multi-dimensional Recurrent Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Multi-dimensional Recurrent Neural Networks Multi-dimensional domains such as vision, video processing, medical imaging, and image segmentation tasks. Introduce multi-dimensional recurrent neural networks to apply recurrent neural modeling across more than one spatio-temporal dimension while avoiding multi-dimensional scaling problems. Changed RNNs from one-dimensional sequence learners with no direct multi-dimensional application into a direct multi-dimensional recurrent architecture. TITLE: Multi-dimensional Recurrent Neural Networks

YEAR: 2007

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recurrent neural networks (RNNs) have proved effective at one dimensional sequence learning tasks, such as speech and online handwriting recognition. Some of the properties that make RNNs suitable for such tasks, for example robustness to input warping, and the ability to access contextual information, are also desirable in multi-dimensional domains. However, there has so far been no direct way of applying RNNs to data with more than one spatio-temporal dimension. This paper introduces multi

title: Multi-path Summation for Decoding 2D Topological Codes
searchable_text:
Multi-path Summation for Decoding 2D Topological Codes Decoding 2D topological codes, especially rotated surface codes under depolarizing errors. Belief propagation plus a novel edge-weight generation algorithm improves minimum-weight perfect matching decoding for surface codes. Compared with prior matching-based decoders, it adds belief propagation and multi-path-derived edge weights instead of specialized suboptimal matching reductions. TITLE: Multi-path Summation for Decoding 2D Topological Codes

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Fault tolerance is a prerequisite for scalable quantum computing. Architectures based on 2D topological codes are effective for near-term implementations of fault tolerance. To obtain high performance with these architectures, we require a decoder which can adapt to the wide variety of error models present in experiments. The typical approach to the problem of decoding the surface code is to reduce it to minimum-weight perfect matching in a way that provides a suboptimal threshold

title: Multi-Scale Context Aggregation by Dilated Convolutions
searchable_text:
Multi-Scale Context Aggregation by Dilated Convolutions Semantic segmentation and dense prediction, where image-level classification network adaptations are structurally mismatched to pixel-level prediction. A dense-prediction context module uses dilated convolutions to aggregate multi-scale contextual information while preserving resolution and coverage. Replaces classification-network adaptation emphasis with a dense-prediction-specific module exploiting dilated convolutions for exponential receptive-field expansion without resolution loss. TITLE: Multi-Scale Context Aggregation by Dilated Convolutions

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
State-of-the-art models for semantic segmentation are based on adaptations of convolutional networks that had originally been designed for image classification. However, dense prediction and image classification are structurally different. In this work, we develop a new convolutional network module that is specifically designed for dense prediction. The presented module uses dilated convolutions to systematically aggregate multi-scale contextual information without losing resolut

title: Multiagent Bidirectionally-Coordinated Nets: Emergence of Human-level Coordination in Learning to Play StarCraft Combat Games
searchable_text:
Multiagent Bidirectionally-Coordinated Nets: Emergence of Human-level Coordination in Learning to Play StarCraft Combat Games Coordinating multiple AI agents as a team in StarCraft combat games to defeat enemy teams with arbitrary numbers of agents. Multiagent Bidirectionally-Coordinated Network using a vectorised actor-critic formulation for scalable intra-agent communication and coordination. Extends actor-critic into a vectorised multiagent bidirectional coordination network for scalable team communication and arbitrary-sized combat settings. TITLE: Multiagent Bidirectionally-Coordinated Nets: Emergence of Human-level Coordination in Learning to Play StarCraft Combat Games

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many artificial intelligence (AI) applications often require multiple intelligent agents to work in a collaborative effort. Efficient learning for intra-agent communication and coordination is an indispensable step towards general AI. In this paper, we take StarCraft combat game as a case study, where the task is to coordinate multiple agents as a team to defeat their enemies. To maintain a scalable yet effecti

title: Multilingual Speech Recognition with a Single End-to-End Model
searchable_text:
Multilingual Speech Recognition with a Single End-to-End Model Multilingual automatic speech recognition for Indian languages with little script overlap. Train one grapheme-based sequence-to-sequence ASR model across 9 Indian languages using the union of language-specific grapheme sets, optionally adding a language identifier input feature. Replaced separate language-specific sequence-to-sequence ASR models with one jointly trained multilingual grapheme model using a union grapheme inventory and optional language-ID conditioning. TITLE: Multilingual Speech Recognition with a Single End-to-End Model

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Training a conventional automatic speech recognition (ASR) system to support multiple languages is challenging because the sub-word unit, lexicon and word inventories are typically language specific. In contrast, sequence-to-sequence models are well suited for multilingual ASR because they encapsulate an acoustic, pronunciation and language model jointly in a single network. In this work we present a single sequence-to-sequence ASR model trained on 9 different Indian langu

title: Multivariate f-divergence Estimation With Confidence
searchable_text:
Multivariate f-divergence Estimation With Confidence Estimating f-divergence between two distributions from finite samples, including divergence-based tests of equality between empirical distributions. Establish asymptotic normality for an ensemble f-divergence estimator with O(1/T) MSE, enabling confidence-based divergence inference. Moves beyond prior convergence-rate results by deriving the estimator’s asymptotic distribution for confidence and inference tasks. TITLE: Multivariate f-divergence Estimation With Confidence

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The problem of f-divergence estimation is important in the fields of machine learning, information theory, and statistics. While several nonparametric divergence estimators exist, relatively few have known convergence properties. In particular, even for those estimators whose MSE convergence rates are known, the asymptotic distributions are unknown. We establish the asymptotic normality of a recently proposed ensemble estimator of f-divergence between two distributions from a finite

title: MXNet: A Flexible and Efficient Machine Learning Library for Heterogeneous Distributed Systems
searchable_text:
MXNet: A Flexible and Efficient Machine Learning Library for Heterogeneous Distributed Systems Development of machine learning algorithms, especially deep neural networks, across mobile devices and distributed GPU clusters. Embedded multi-language ML library unifying declarative symbolic expressions, imperative tensor computation, and automatic differentiation for efficient heterogeneous execution. It combines symbolic expression and tensor operations in a unified embedded host-language API for heterogeneous distributed systems. TITLE: MXNet: A Flexible and Efficient Machine Learning Library for Heterogeneous Distributed Systems

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
MXNet is a multi-language machine learning (ML) library to ease the development of ML algorithms, especially for deep neural networks. Embedded in the host language, it blends declarative symbolic expression with imperative tensor computation. It offers auto differentiation to derive gradients. MXNet is computation and memory efficient and runs on various heterogeneous systems, ranging from mobile devices to distributed GPU clusters.

title: Natural TTS Synthesis by Conditioning Wavenet on MEL Spectrogram Predictions
searchable_text:
Natural TTS Synthesis by Conditioning Wavenet on MEL Spectrogram Predictions Natural speech synthesis directly from text. A recurrent sequence-to-sequence network maps character embeddings to mel-scale spectrograms, then a modified WaveNet vocoder synthesizes time-domain waveforms from those spectrograms. WaveNet is conditioned on predicted mel spectrograms rather than linguistic, duration, and F0 features, using a compact acoustic intermediate representation. TITLE: Natural TTS Synthesis by Conditioning Wavenet on MEL Spectrogram Predictions

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper describes Tacotron 2, a neural network architecture for speech synthesis directly from text. The system is composed of a recurrent sequence-to-sequence feature prediction network that maps character embeddings to mel-scale spectrograms, followed by a modified WaveNet model acting as a vocoder to synthesize time-domain waveforms from those spectrograms. Our model achieves a mean opinion score (MOS) of 4.53 comparable to a MOS of 4.58 for professiona

title: Near-linear time approximation algorithms for optimal transport via Sinkhorn iteration
searchable_text:
Near-linear time approximation algorithms for optimal transport via Sinkhorn iteration Approximating general optimal transport distances such as earth mover's distance in machine learning, statistics, and computer vision. Analyze Sinkhorn iteration and derive Greenkhorn, a greedy coordinate descent variant, to obtain near-linear-time approximation guarantees for Sinkhorn optimal transport distances. Compared with prior empirically strong Sinkhorn approaches, this work proves near-linear approximation guarantees and introduces Greenkhorn with the same theoretical guarantees. TITLE: Near-linear time approximation algorithms for optimal transport via Sinkhorn iteration

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Computing optimal transport distances such as the earth mover's distance is a fundamental problem in machine learning, statistics, and computer vision. Despite the recent introduction of several algorithms with good empirical performance, it is unknown whether general optimal transport distances can be approximated in near-linear time. This paper demonstrates that this ambitious goal is in fact achieved by Cuturi's Sinkhorn Distances. This result re

title: Network Morphism
searchable_text:
Network Morphism Reusing and expanding well-trained neural networks into larger or structurally different networks without losing the learned network function. Network morphism equations plus algorithms transform a trained parent into a child with preserved function across depth, width, kernel-size, and subnet changes, using parametric activations for nonlinear neurons. Moves beyond isolated architecture edits by systematically preserving network function across diverse morphing types and nonlinear activations. TITLE: Network Morphism

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a systematic study on how to morph a well-trained neural network to a new one so that its network function can be completely preserved. We define this as network morphism in this research. After morphing a parent network, the child network is expected to inherit the knowledge from its parent network and also has the potential to continue growing into a more powerful one with much shortened training time. The first requirement for this network morphism is its ability to handle diverse morphing types of netwo

title: Neural Discrete Representation Learning
searchable_text:
Neural Discrete Representation Learning Unsupervised learning of useful discrete representations for generative modeling of images, videos, speech, speaker conversion, and phoneme discovery. A Vector Quantised-Variational AutoEncoder learns discrete latent codes via vector quantisation, paired with a learned autoregressive prior. Compared with VAEs, the encoder outputs discrete rather than continuous codes, and the prior is learned rather than static. TITLE: Neural Discrete Representation Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Learning useful representations without supervision remains a key challenge in machine learning. In this paper, we propose a simple yet powerful generative model that learns such discrete representations. Our model, the Vector Quantised-Variational AutoEncoder (VQ-VAE), differs from VAEs in two key ways: the encoder network outputs discrete, rather than continuous, codes; and the prior is learnt rather than static. In order to learn a discrete latent representation, we incorporate ideas from vec

title: Neural masses and fields in dynamic causal modeling
searchable_text:
Neural masses and fields in dynamic causal modeling Analysis of effective connectivity among neuronal subpopulations underlying invasive and non-invasive electrophysiological responses. Dynamic causal modeling using neural mass, conductance-based, and neural field differential-equation models to represent synaptic dynamics, ion-channel conductances, and spatial cortical propagation. Extends earlier convolution-based neural mass DCM by adding ion-channel conductance dynamics and cortical-surface neural fields governed by partial differential equations. TITLE: Neural masses and fields in dynamic causal modeling

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Dynamic causal modeling (DCM) provides a framework for the analysis of effective connectivity among neuronal subpopulations that subtend invasive (electrocorticograms and local field potentials) and non-invasive (electroencephalography and magnetoencephalography) electrophysiological responses. This paper reviews the suite of neuronal population models including neural masses, fields and conductance-based models that are used in DCM. These models are expressed in terms of sets of differential

title: Neural Network-based Graph Embedding for Cross-Platform Binary Code Similarity Detection
searchable_text:
Neural Network-based Graph Embedding for Cross-Platform Binary Code Similarity Detection Cross-platform binary code similarity detection for determining whether binary functions from different platforms are similar, with security uses such as malware detection and vulnerability search. Neural network computes numeric embeddings from each binary function’s control flow graph; similarity is detected by measuring distance between function embeddings. Replaces slow approximate graph-matching approaches with neural network graph embeddings over control flow graphs for efficient cross-platform function comparison. TITLE: Neural Network-based Graph Embedding for Cross-Platform Binary Code Similarity Detection

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The problem of cross-platform binary code similarity detection aims at detecting whether two binary functions coming from different platforms are similar or not. It has many security applications, including plagiarism detection, malware detection, vulnerability search, etc. Existing approaches rely on approximate graph-matching algorithms, which are inevitably slow and sometimes inaccurate, and hard to adapt to a new task. To address these issues,

title: Neural Network Dynamics for Model-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning with Model-Free Fine-Tuning
searchable_text:
Neural Network Dynamics for Model-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning with Model-Free Fine-Tuning Sample-efficient robotic locomotion reinforcement learning on MuJoCo tasks including trajectory following and high-speed swimmer, cheetah, hopper, and ant agents. Train deep neural network dynamics models, use them with model predictive control, then initialize a model-free learner for fine-tuning. Instead of only model-free deep RL or low-capacity model-based control, the work combines deep neural dynamics with MPC and uses the learned model to initialize model-free RL. TITLE: Neural Network Dynamics for Model-Based Deep Reinforcement Learning with Model-Free Fine-Tuning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Model-free deep reinforcement learning algorithms have been shown to be capable of learning a wide range of robotic skills, but typically require a very large number of samples to achieve good performance. Model-based algorithms, in principle, can provide for much more efficient learning, but have proven difficult to extend to expressive, high-capacity models such as deep neural networks. In this work, we demonstrate that neural network dyn

title: Neural Optimizer Search with Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Neural Optimizer Search with Reinforcement Learning Automated discovery of optimization methods for deep learning architectures. Train an RNN controller with reinforcement learning to emit a domain-specific-language string encoding mathematical optimizer update equations from primitive functions. Replaces manually designed optimizers with reinforcement-learning search over symbolic update equations generated by a recurrent controller. TITLE: Neural Optimizer Search with Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present an approach to automate the process of discovering optimization methods, with a focus on deep learning architectures. We train a Recurrent Neural Network controller to generate a string in a domain specific language that describes a mathematical update equation based on a list of primitive functions, such as the gradient, running average of the gradient, etc. The controller is trained with Reinforcement Learning to maximize the performance of a model after a few epochs. On

title: Neural Programmer-Interpreters
searchable_text:
Neural Programmer-Interpreters Learning compositional executable programs across perceptually diverse environments, including addition, sorting, canonicalizing 3D models, and their associated subprograms. A recurrent, compositional neural programmer-interpreter with a task-agnostic core, persistent key-value program memory, and domain-specific encoders that learns to represent, call, and execute programs. Compared with sequence-to-sequence LSTMs, NPI adds explicit compositional program calls, persistent program memory, domain-specific encoders, and environmental scratch-pad use for intermediate computation. TITLE: Neural Programmer-Interpreters

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose the neural programmer-interpreter (NPI): a recurrent and compositional neural network that learns to represent and execute programs. NPI has three learnable components: a task-agnostic recurrent core, a persistent key-value program memory, and domain-specific encoders that enable a single NPI to operate in multiple perceptually diverse environments with distinct affordances. By learning to compose lower-level programs to express higher-level programs, NPI reduces sample complexity and increase

title: Neural Turing Machines
searchable_text:
Neural Turing Machines Learning simple algorithmic sequence tasks from input-output examples, including copying, sorting, and associative recall. Couple neural networks to external memory resources accessed through attentional processes, forming an end-to-end differentiable memory-augmented architecture trainable by gradient descent. Compared with ordinary neural networks, the change is adding external memory resources and differentiable attentional interaction with that memory. TITLE: Neural Turing Machines

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We extend the capabilities of neural networks by coupling them to external memory resources, which they can interact with by attentional processes. The combined system is analogous to a Turing Machine or Von Neumann architecture but is differentiable end-toend, allowing it to be efficiently trained with gradient descent. Preliminary results demonstrate that Neural Turing Machines can infer simple algorithms such as copying, sorting, and associative recall from input and output examples.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Program Synthesis
searchable_text:
Neuro-Symbolic Program Synthesis Program synthesis from input-output examples, demonstrated on regular-expression-based string transformations. Encode input-output examples with a cross correlation I/O network, then use an R3NN to synthesize DSL programs by incrementally expanding partial programs. Instead of training a neural model per task to learn opaque mappings, train once to synthesize explicit DSL programs at test time from examples. TITLE: Neuro-Symbolic Program Synthesis

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recent years have seen the proposal of a number of neural architectures for the problem of Program Induction. Given a set of input-output examples, these architectures are able to learn mappings that generalize to new test inputs. While achieving impressive results, these approaches have a number of important limitations: (a) they are computationally expensive and hard to train, (b) a model has to be trained for each task (program) separately, and (c) it is hard to interpret or verify the correctness o

title: Non-strongly-convex smooth stochastic approximation with convergence rate O(1/n)
searchable_text:
Non-strongly-convex smooth stochastic approximation with convergence rate O(1/n) Non-strongly-convex smooth stochastic approximation for convex risk minimization using unbiased gradient estimates, especially supervised learning. Use averaged SGD with constant step-size for least-squares and local quadratic stochastic updates for logistic regression at SGD-like cost. Changed from O(1/√n) non-strongly-convex rates to O(1/n) algorithms for least-squares and logistic regression. TITLE: Non-strongly-convex smooth stochastic approximation with convergence rate O(1/n)

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We consider the stochastic approximation problem where a convex function has to be minimized, given only the knowledge of unbiased estimates of its gradients at certain points, a framework which includes machine learning methods based on the minimization of the empirical risk. We focus on problems without strong convexity, for which all previously known algorithms achieve a convergence rate for function values of O(1/√n) after n iterations. We consider an

title: Nonparametric predictive distributions based on conformal prediction
searchable_text:
Nonparametric predictive distributions based on conformal prediction Predictive distributions for nonparametric regression with guaranteed coverage for IID observations. Apply conformal prediction to construct valid predictive distribution functions; the Least Squares Prediction Machine extends Dempster–Hill predictive distributions to nonparametric regression. Generalizes classical Dempster–Hill predictive distributions from parametric least-squares settings to nonparametric regression via conformal prediction. TITLE: Nonparametric predictive distributions based on conformal prediction

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper applies conformal prediction to derive predictive distributions that are valid under a nonparametric assumption. Namely, we introduce and explore predictive distribution functions that always satisfy a natural property of validity in terms of guaranteed coverage for IID observations. The focus is on a prediction algorithm that we call the Least Squares Prediction Machine (LSPM). The LSPM generalizes the classical Dempster–Hill predictive distributions to n

title: Notions of optimal transport theory and how to implement them on a computer
searchable_text:
Notions of optimal transport theory and how to implement them on a computer Measuring distances and interpolation between functions or objects, and enforcing mass/volume conservation in computational physics simulations. Implement semi-discrete optimal transport by transporting a continuous function to Dirac masses and computing via computational geometry, especially Laguerre diagrams. It shifts from conceptual optimal transport notions to the semi-discrete case connecting theory with implementable Laguerre-diagram algorithms. TITLE: Notions of optimal transport theory and how to implement them on a computer

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This article gives an introduction to optimal transport, a mathematical theory that makes it possible to measure distances between functions (or distances between more general objects), to interpolate between objects or to enforce mass/volume conservation in certain computational physics simulations. Optimal transport is a rich scientific domain, with active research communities, both on its theoretical aspects and on more applicative considerations, such as g

title: Novel scheme for rapid parallel parameter estimation of gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences
searchable_text:
Novel scheme for rapid parallel parameter estimation of gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences Rapid parallel parameter estimation for gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences using waveform models and detector data. Spherical-harmonic waveform mode decomposition with fixed-intrinsic-parameter prefiltering, Monte Carlo integration over extrinsic parameters, and hierarchical sampling guided by existing searches. Likelihood evaluation becomes independent of waveform length or generation time by generating and prefiltering waveform modes once, then integrating extrinsic parameters efficiently in parallel. TITLE: Novel scheme for rapid parallel parameter estimation of gravitational waves from compact binary coalescences

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce a highly parallelizable architecture for estimating parameters of compact binary coalescence using gravitational-wave data and waveform models. Using a spherical harmonic mode decomposition, the waveform is expressed as a sum over modes that depend on the intrinsic parameters (e.g., masses) with coefficients that depend on the observer dependent extrinsic parameters (e.g., distance, sky position). The data is then

title: Numerical Bifurcation Theory for High-Dimensional Neural Models
searchable_text:
Numerical Bifurcation Theory for High-Dimensional Neural Models Bifurcation analysis of high-dimensional computational neuroscience models from discretised neural fields, including stationary, moving, and translating cortical patterns in one and two spatial dimensions. Numerical continuation via pseudo-arclength continuation to follow parametrised algebraic solution branches and detect bifurcations in discretised neural field models. Applies pseudo-arclength continuation to high-dimensional discretised neural field models and extends its use across stationary, moving, and translating neural pattern regimes. TITLE: Numerical Bifurcation Theory for High-Dimensional Neural Models

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Numerical bifurcation theory involves finding and then following certain types of solutions of differential equations as parameters are varied, and determining whether they undergo any bifurcations (qualitative changes in behaviour). The primary technique for doing this is numerical continuation, where the solution of interest satisfies a parametrised set of algebraic equations, and branches of solutions are followed as the parameter is varied. title: NUMERICAL METHODS FOR MATCHING FOR TEAMS AND WASSERSTEIN BARYCENTERS
searchable_text:
NUMERICAL METHODS FOR MATCHING FOR TEAMS AND WASSERSTEIN BARYCENTERS Equilibrium multi-population matching, especially Wasserstein barycenter problems in multi-marginal optimal transport with applications in image processing and statistics. Discrete-measure approximation with a linear programming algorithm and, for Wasserstein barycenters, an efficient nonsmooth optimization algorithm. Introduces two numerical algorithms for matching for teams, including a nonsmooth optimization method specialized to Wasserstein barycenters. TITLE: NUMERICAL METHODS FOR MATCHING FOR TEAMS AND WASSERSTEIN BARYCENTERS

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Equilibrium multi-population matching (matching for teams) is a problem from mathematical economics which is related to multi-marginal optimal transport. A special but important case is the Wasserstein barycenter problem, which has applications in image processing and statistics. Two algorithms are presented: a linear programming algorithm and an efficient nonsmooth optimization algorithm, which applies in the case of the Wasserstein barycenters. The measures are app

title: Numerical Relativity and Astrophysics
searchable_text:
Numerical Relativity and Astrophysics Strong-gravity astrophysical events such as compact binary mergers, black hole accretion, and stellar collapse with relativistic velocities and extreme gravitational fields. Numerically solve the full Einstein equations coupled to matter sources for dynamical strong-gravity systems beyond perturbative or analytical treatment. Moves from perturbative or analytical modeling to numerical relativity solving the full Einstein equations coupled to matter in dynamical strong-gravity regimes. TITLE: Numerical Relativity and Astrophysics

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Throughout the Universe many powerful events are driven by strong gravitational effects that require general relativity to fully describe them. These include compact binary mergers, black hole accretion, and stellar collapse, where velocities can approach the speed of light and extreme gravitational fields (ΦNewt/c2≃1) mediate the interactions. Many of these processes trigger emission across a broad range of the electromagnetic spectrum. Compact binaries further source strong gravitational wave em

title: Numerical solution of the Optimal Transportation problem using the Monge-Ampère equation
searchable_text:
Numerical solution of the Optimal Transportation problem using the Monge-Ampère equation Numerical solution of the elliptic Monge-Ampere PDE with Optimal Transportation boundary conditions. Combine a local representation of OT boundary conditions with a finite-difference Monge-Ampere scheme. Adds a local representation of OT boundary conditions to a finite-difference Monge-Ampere solver. TITLE: Numerical solution of the Optimal Transportation problem using the Monge-Ampère equation

YEAR: 2012

ABSTRACT:
A numerical method for the solution of the elliptic Monge-Ampere Partial Differential Equation, with boundary conditions corresponding to the Optimal Transportation (OT) problem, is presented. A local representation of the OT boundary conditions is combined with a finite difference scheme for the Monge-Ampere equation. title: Object-Proposal Evaluation Protocol is ‘Gameable’
searchable_text:
Object-Proposal Evaluation Protocol is ‘Gameable’ Evaluation of category-independent object proposal methods for vision pipelines using partially annotated benchmark datasets. Nearly fully annotated PASCAL testing, exhaustive and cross-dataset proposal evaluation, plus a diagnostic experiment for detecting category-bias capacity in object proposal algorithms. Moves beyond standard partially annotated object-proposal benchmarks by adding nearly fully annotated PASCAL, cross-dataset generalization tests, and a bias-capacity diagnostic. TITLE: Object-Proposal Evaluation Protocol is ‘Gameable’

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Object proposals have quickly become the de-facto preprocessing step in a number of vision pipelines (for object detection, object discovery, and other tasks). Their performance is usually evaluated on partially annotated datasets. title: Observation of a new boson at a mass of 125 GeV with the CMS experiment at the LHC
searchable_text:
Observation of a new boson at a mass of 125 GeV with the CMS experiment at the LHC Detecting and characterizing a standard model Higgs-boson candidate in LHC proton-proton collision events near 125 GeV. Search CMS proton-proton collision data at 7 and 8 TeV across five Higgs decay modes using integrated luminosities up to 5.1 and 5.3 fb−1. Shifted from Higgs searches to observation of a new 125 GeV boson signal in CMS data. TITLE: Observation of a new boson at a mass of 125 GeV with the CMS experiment at the LHC

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Results are presented from searches for the standard model Higgs boson in protonproton collisions at √ s = 7 and 8 TeV in the Compact Muon Solenoid experiment at the LHC, using data samples corresponding to integrated luminosities of up to 5.1 fb−1 at 7 TeV and 5.3 fb−1 at 8 TeV. The search is performed in five decay modes: γγ, ZZ, W+W−, τ+τ−, and bb. An excess of events is observed above the expected background, with a local significance of 5.0 standar

title: Off-policy evaluation for slate recommendation
searchable_text:
Off-policy evaluation for slate recommendation Evaluating context-dependent policies that recommend ordered slates, such as rankings in web search, ads, and recommendation. A practical off-policy estimator built from combinatorial bandits uses logged data to estimate ordered-slate recommendation policy performance. It weakens prior slate-evaluation heuristics for unbiasedness and claims exponential data savings over general unbiased estimators. TITLE: Off-policy evaluation for slate recommendation

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper studies the evaluation of policies that recommend an ordered set of items (e.g., a ranking) based on some context---a common scenario in web search, ads, and recommendation. We build on techniques from combinatorial bandits to introduce a new practical estimator that uses logged data to estimate a policy's performance. A thorough empirical evaluation on real-world data reveals that our estimator is accurate in a variety of settings, including as a subroutine in a learning-to-ra

title: On a Formal Model of Safe and Scalable Self-driving Cars
searchable_text:
On a Formal Model of Safe and Scalable Self-driving Cars Safety assurance and scalable engineering for self-driving cars intended for broad real-world deployment. A white-box, interpretable mathematical safety assurance model, Responsibility-Sensitive Safety (RSS), paired with a scalable system design for deployment across millions of self-driving cars. Adds standardized safety assurance and scalability as explicit design parameters beyond the prevailing focus on first road deployment. TITLE: On a Formal Model of Safe and Scalable Self-driving Cars

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In recent years, car makers and tech companies have been racing towards self driving cars. It seems that the main parameter in this race is who will have the first car on the road. The goal of this paper is to add to the equation two additional crucial parameters. The first is standardization of safety assurance --- what are the minimal requirements that every self-driving car must satisfy, and how can we verify these requirements. title: On Capacity of Large-Scale MIMO Multiple Access Channels with Distributed Sets of Correlated Antennas
searchable_text:
On Capacity of Large-Scale MIMO Multiple Access Channels with Distributed Sets of Correlated Antennas Uplink large-scale multiuser MIMO multiple access channels with one base station, several distributed antenna sets, correlated links, and line-of-sight components. Large-dimensional random matrix theory derives deterministic equivalents of ergodic sum rate and capacity-achieving input covariance matrices for two-sided spatially correlated MIMO channels with LOS. Extends capacity evaluation to large-scale distributed antenna-set MIMO MACs with two-sided spatial correlation and LOS using deterministic-equivalent RMT methods. TITLE: On Capacity of Large-Scale MIMO Multiple Access Channels with Distributed Sets of Correlated Antennas

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, a deterministic equivalent of ergodic sum rate and an algorithm for evaluating the capacity-achieving input covariance matrices for the uplink large-scale multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) antenna channels are proposed. We consider a large-scale MIMO system consisting of multiple users and one base station with several distributed antenna sets. Each link between a user and an antenna set forms a two-sided spatially

title: On Choosing and Bounding Probability Metrics
searchable_text:
On Choosing and Bounding Probability Metrics Choosing probability metrics for studying convergence of measures in statistical and probabilistic applications. Summarizes and derives bounds among important probability metrics/distances to transfer knowledge of one metric into bounds or insights for another. Adds new results on bounds among important probability metrics/distances beyond summarizing existing relationships. TITLE: On Choosing and Bounding Probability Metrics

YEAR: 2002

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
When studying convergence of measures, an important issue is the choice of probability metric. We provide a summary and some new results concerning bounds among some important probability metrics/distances that are used by statisticians and probabilists. Knowledge of other metrics can provide a means of deriving bounds for another one in an applied problem. Considering other metrics can also provide alternate insights. title: On deep learning-based channel decoding
searchable_text:
On deep learning-based channel decoding Channel decoding for random and structured codes such as polar codes, targeting short codeword lengths and MAP bit error rate performance. Use deep neural networks for one-shot decoding of random and structured channel codes, evaluated with normalized validation error. Revisits neural one-shot decoding while explicitly contrasting random versus structured codes and introducing normalized validation error to assess decoding potential and limitations. TITLE: On deep learning-based channel decoding

YEAR: 2017

ABSTRACT:
We revisit the idea of using deep neural networks for one-shot decoding of random and structured codes, such as polar codes. Although it is possible to achieve maximum a posteriori (MAP) bit error rate (BER) performance for both code families and for short codeword lengths, we observe that (i) structured codes are easier to learn and (ii) the neural network is able to generalize to codewords that it has never seen during training for structured, but not for random codes. title: On Large-Batch Training for Deep Learning: Generalization Gap and Sharp Minima
searchable_text:
On Large-Batch Training for Deep Learning: Generalization Gap and Sharp Minima Generalization degradation in large-batch training for deep learning models trained with SGD or variants. Numerically compare small- and large-batch SGD behavior to relate batch size, gradient-estimation noise, minimizer sharpness, and generalization. Shifts focus from standard small-batch SGD practice to diagnosing why larger batches degrade generalization via sharp versus flat minima. TITLE: On Large-Batch Training for Deep Learning: Generalization Gap and Sharp Minima

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The stochastic gradient descent (SGD) method and its variants are algorithms of choice for many Deep Learning tasks. These methods operate in a small-batch regime wherein a fraction of the training data, say $32$-$512$ data points, is sampled to compute an approximation to the gradient. It has been observed in practice that when using a larger batch there is a degradation in the quality of the model, as measured by its ability to generalize. We investigate

title: On Learning to Think: Algorithmic Information Theory for Novel Combinations of Reinforcement Learning Controllers and Recurrent Neural World Models
searchable_text:
On Learning to Think: Algorithmic Information Theory for Novel Combinations of Reinforcement Learning Controllers and Recurrent Neural World Models Reinforcement learning in partially observable environments, especially agents needing abstract planning and reasoning from initially unknown environments. RNN-based AIs learn predictive world models, self-generate tasks, and actively query the model for abstract reasoning, planning, and decision-making guided by algorithmic information theory. Compared with 1990 model-building RNN RL machines, the RNNAI actively queries its learned model for abstract reasoning, planning, and decision-making. TITLE: On Learning to Think: Algorithmic Information Theory for Novel Combinations of Reinforcement Learning Controllers and Recurrent Neural World Models

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper addresses the general problem of reinforcement learning (RL) in partially observable environments. In 2013, our large RL recurrent neural networks (RNNs) learned from scratch to drive simulated cars from high-dimensional video input. However, real brains are more powerful in many ways. In particular, they learn a predictive model of their initially unknown environment, and someh

title: On representing chemical environments
searchable_text:
On representing chemical environments Representing atomic neighborhood environments for fitting potential energy surfaces in chemical and atomistic systems. Use symmetry-invariant, differentiable atomic-neighborhood representations, including basis expansions of neighborhood density and SOAP, which directly defines similarity between environments. SOAP changes from constructing finite invariant descriptors to directly defining similarity between two neighborhood environments while retaining links to invariant descriptors. TITLE: On representing chemical environments

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We review some recently published methods to represent atomic neighborhood environments, and analyze their relative merits in terms of their faithfulness and suitability for fitting potential energy surfaces. title: On the accuracy and precision of numerical waveforms: effect of waveform extraction methodology

title: On the Bures–Wasserstein distance between positive definite matrices

title: On the difficulty of training recurrent neural networks
searchable_text:
On the difficulty of training recurrent neural networks Properly training recurrent neural networks affected by vanishing and exploding gradient problems. Gradient norm clipping for exploding gradients plus a soft constraint for vanishing gradients, justified through analytical, geometric, and dynamical-systems analysis. Compared with Bengio et al. (1994)'s problem characterization, this work adds multi-perspective analysis and proposes gradient norm clipping plus a soft vanishing-gradient constraint. title: ON THE INCLINATION AND HABITABILITY OF THE HD 10180 SYSTEM

A new radial-velocity orbital solution for HD 10180 allowing eccentric orbits for all planets, combined with inclination-dependent mass/radius analysis, phase-signature detectability, and Habitable Zone evaluation. The study provides a new HD 10180 orbital solution that allows eccentric orbits for all planets, rather than restricting eccentricity in the system fit. title: On the iterative decoding of sparse quantum codes

Heuristic modifications to belief propagation decoding, targeted at small Tanner-graph loops and degeneracy in sparse quantum codes. title: One-Shot Visual Imitation Learning via Meta-Learning
searchable_text:
One-Shot Visual Imitation Learning via Meta-Learning One-shot visual imitation learning for robots acquiring diverse skills quickly in complex unstructured environments from raw pixel inputs. Meta-imitation learning trains robots to learn new skills efficiently from a single demonstration, using high-capacity models for end-to-end visual skill acquisition. title: Online Reinforcement Learning in Stochastic Games
searchable_text:
Online Reinforcement Learning in Stochastic Games Online reinforcement learning in average-reward two-player zero-sum stochastic games with Markovian transitions and payoffs determined jointly by learner and adversary. UCSG algorithm for online average-reward stochastic games, evaluated against arbitrary opponents and optimistic best-response opponents to obtain regret and policy-learning guarantees. Compared with previous work in the same setting, UCSG provides an improved sublinear-regret guarantee and adds a polynomial sample-complexity route to ε-maximin policies. We propose the UCSG algorithm that achieves a sublinear regret compared to the game value when competing with an arbitrary opponent. This result improves previous ones under the same setting. title: Onsager-corrected deep learning for sparse linear inverse problems

title: Open Babel: An open chemical toolbox

title: Open Quantum Assembly Language

Open Quantum Assembly Language Implementing experiments with low-depth quantum circuits. A quantum assembly language representing universal physical circuits over the CNOT plus SU(2) basis using straight-line code with measurement, reset, feedback, and gate subroutines.  TITLE: Open Quantum Assembly Language

ABSTRACT:
This document describes a quantum assembly language (QASM) called OpenQASM that is used to implement experiments with low depth quantum circuits. OpenQASM represents universal physical circuits over the CNOT plus SU(2) basis with straight-line code that includes measurement, reset, fast feedback, and gate subroutines. The simple text language can be written by hand or by higher level tools and may be executed on the IBM Q Experience.

title: OpenAI Gym

OpenAI Gym Reinforcement learning research benchmarks and algorithm performance comparison. Toolkit providing reinforcement-learning benchmark problems behind a common interface, plus a website for sharing results and comparing algorithm performance. OpenAI Gym is a toolkit for reinforcement learning research. This whitepaper discusses the components of OpenAI Gym and the design decisions that went into the software.

title: Opponent Modeling in Deep Reinforcement Learning

Opponent Modeling in Deep Reinforcement Learning Multi-agent reinforcement learning settings where competing secondary agents adapt their strategies, evaluated in simulated soccer and trivia games. Neural opponent modeling jointly learns an agent policy and opponent behavior by encoding opponent observations into DQN, optionally multitasking explicit prediction, and using Mixture-of-Experts to discover strategy patterns. Moves beyond probabilistic models or parameterized strategies for specific applications to neural models that jointly learn policy and opponent behavior within DQN-style architectures. Most previous work focuses on developing probabilistic models or parameterized strategies for specific applications. Inspired by the recent success of deep reinforcement learning, we present neural-based models that jointly learn a policy and the behavior of opponents. Instead of

title: Optimal and Adaptive Off-policy Evaluation in Contextual Bandits
searchable_text:
Optimal and Adaptive Off-policy Evaluation in Contextual Bandits Off-policy evaluation: estimating a target policy’s value from data collected by another policy under the contextual bandit model. SWITCH estimator selectively uses an existing reward model, even if inconsistent, to improve the bias-variance tradeoff relative to IPS and DR in off-policy contextual bandit evaluation. Compared with IPS/DR, SWITCH adds optional use of an existing, not necessarily consistent reward model to adapt the bias-variance tradeoff. TITLE: Optimal and Adaptive Off-policy Evaluation in Contextual Bandits

ABSTRACT:
We study the off-policy evaluation problem---estimating the value of a target policy using data collected by another policy---under the contextual bandit model. We consider the general (agnostic) setting without access to a consistent model of rewards and establish a minimax lower bound on the mean squared error (MSE). title: Optimal Distributed Online Prediction Using Mini-Batches
searchable_text:
Optimal Distributed Online Prediction Using Mini-Batches Web-scale online prediction and distributed stochastic optimization where input rates exceed what a single processor can handle. Distributed mini-batching converts serial gradient-based online prediction algorithms into distributed algorithms while accounting for inter-node communication latency. Moves online prediction from single-processor serial gradient methods to a distributed mini-batch formulation with explicit communication-latency analysis. TITLE: Optimal Distributed Online Prediction Using Mini-Batches

Online prediction methods are typically presented as serial algorithms running on a single processor. In this work, we present the distributed mini-batch algorithm, a method of converting many serial gradient-based online prediction algorithms into distributed algorithms. We prove a regret bound

title: Optimal Entropy-Transport problems and a new Hellinger–Kantorovich distance between positive measures

title: Optimal Hamiltonian Simulation by Quantum Signal Processing.

title: Optimal Ordered Problem Solver
searchable_text:
Optimal Ordered Problem Solver Sequential problem solving where each new task may benefit from earlier solution-computing programs and learned search procedures. Recursive backtracking universal program search allocates time to candidates that compute solutions and can organize, adapt, reuse, copy-edit, or invoke prior solution code. Extends Levin's Universal Search from single isolated problems to ordered problem sequences with computable reuse and adaptation of previously acquired solutions. title: Optimal Transport for Diffeomorphic Registration

Optimal Transport for Diffeomorphic Registration Diffeomorphic matching/registration of imaging data, including surfaces, volumetric images, fibre bundles, and synthetic or real datasets. title: Optimal Transport in Competition with Reaction: The Hellinger-Kantorovich Distance and Geodesic Curves

title: Optimal Transport with Proximal Splitting

Optimal Transport with Proximal Splitting Computing discretized dynamic optimal transport, especially the L2 optimal transport geodesic between distributions on a uniform spatial grid. title: Optimal Transportation with Traffic Congestion and Wardrop Equilibria

Optimal Transportation with Traffic Congestion and Wardrop Equilibria Optimal transportation and traffic allocation in continuous space where congestion and Wardrop equilibria matter. Uses traffic intensity to formulate a congested continuous optimal-transport variant and characterize Wardrop-type equilibria variationally. title: OptLayer - Practical Constrained Optimization for Deep Reinforcement Learning in the Real World

OptLayer - Practical Constrained Optimization for Deep Reinforcement Learning in the Real World Safe deep reinforcement learning for robot reaching and real-world robotic control where unconstrained trial-and-error can harm the robot or environment. OptLayer takes possibly unsafe neural-network actions and outputs the closest actions satisfying chosen constraints, ensuring only safe actions execute while penalizing unsafe predictions during training. title: Oscillations of relative inclination angles in compact extrasolar planetary systems

Oscillations of relative inclination angles in compact extrasolar planetary systems Compact extrasolar planetary systems with multiple Kepler transiting planets and the origin of observed single-versus-multiple transit multiplicity differences. title: Over-the-air Function Computation in Sensor Networks

Over-the-air Function Computation in Sensor Networks Computing aggregation functions such as sum, average, or max over sensor measurements in sensor networks. title: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks

Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks Sequential multi-task learning for deep neural networks, including handwritten digit classification and Atari 2600 reinforcement learning tasks. Train sequentially by protecting or slowing learning on weights important for previous tasks, inspired by synaptic consolidation. Instead of accepting catastrophic forgetting as inevitable, it selectively protected weights important to prior tasks during later learning. title: OVERVIEW OF THE KEPLER SCIENCE PROCESSING PIPELINE
searchable_text:
OVERVIEW OF THE KEPLER SCIENCE PROCESSING PIPELINE Monthly Kepler spacecraft photometric data processing for ∼156,000 target stars to detect transiting exoplanet signatures and reject astrophysical or instrumental false positives. Pixel-level calibration, background estimation, aperture photometry, systematic-error removal, wavelet-based adaptive matched filtering, false-positive statistical tests, centroid checks, and transit model fitting to identify planetary candidates.  TITLE: OVERVIEW OF THE KEPLER SCIENCE PROCESSING PIPELINE

The raw data are first calibrated at the pixel level to correct for bias, smear induced by a shutterless readout, and other detector and electronic effects. A background sky flux is estimated

title: PackNet: Adding Multiple Tasks to a Single Network by Iterative Pruning
searchable_text:
PackNet: Adding Multiple Tasks to a Single Network by Iterative Pruning Sequentially adding multiple tasks to a single deep neural network while avoiding catastrophic forgetting. Iteratively prune and retrain a deep network to free redundant parameters, then reuse them to learn additional tasks without overwriting previously allocated weights. Instead of using proxy losses to preserve older-task accuracy, PackNet optimizes directly for the current task while preserving prior task parameters through pruning-based allocation. TITLE: PackNet: Adding Multiple Tasks to a Single Network by Iterative Pruning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper presents a method for adding multiple tasks to a single deep neural network while avoiding catastrophic forgetting. Inspired by network pruning techniques, we exploit redundancies in large deep networks to free up parameters that can then be employed to learn new tasks. By performing iterative pruning and network re-training, we are able to sequentially "pack" multiple tasks into a single network while ensuring minimal drop in performance and minimal st

title: Parameter estimation for compact binaries with ground-based gravitational-wave observations using the LALInference software library
searchable_text:
Parameter estimation for compact binaries with ground-based gravitational-wave observations using the LALInference software library Recovering physical parameters of compact binary coalescence sources from ground-based gravitational-wave observations. Bayesian parameter estimation library integrating multiple independent samplers plus general and compact-binary-specific sampling techniques for compact binary coalescence parameter space. Consolidates several previous methods into a well-tested LALInference toolkit and cross-compares three independent sampling algorithms on simulated advanced-detector compact-binary systems. TITLE: Parameter estimation for compact binaries with ground-based gravitational-wave observations using the LALInference software library

The Advanced LIGO and Advanced Virgo gravitational-wave (GW) detectors will begin operation in the coming years, with compact binary coalescence events a likely source for the first detections. The gravitational waveforms emitted directly encode information about the sources, including the masses and spins of the compact objects. Recovering the physical parameters of the sources from the GW observations i

title: Parton distributions for the LHC run II
searchable_text:
Parton distributions for the LHC run II Determination of parton distribution functions for LHC Run II using HERA, ATLAS, CMS and LHCb datasets. Global PDF fitting at LO, NLO and NNLO with electroweak corrections, using a closure-test-validated methodology on pseudo-data from a known underlying law. NNPDF3.0 is presented as the first PDF set determined with a closure-test-validated methodology and includes new HERA-II and LHC datasets. TITLE: Parton distributions for the LHC run II

NNPDF3.0 uses a global dataset including HERA-II deep-inelastic inclusive cross-sections, the combined HERA charm data, jet production from ATLAS and CMS, vector boson rapidity and transverse momentum distributions from ATLAS, CMS and LHCb, W +c data from CMS and top quark pair production total cross sections from ATLAS and CMS. title: Pixel Recurrent Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Pixel Recurrent Neural Networks Unsupervised modeling of natural image distributions with an expressive, tractable, scalable generative image model. A deep neural network sequentially predicts raw pixel values along both spatial dimensions, modeling their discrete probability and complete image dependencies with two-dimensional recurrent layers and residual connections. Introduced fast two-dimensional recurrent layers and effective residual connections in deep recurrent networks, improving over previous state-of-the-art natural image likelihood models. TITLE: Pixel Recurrent Neural Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Modeling the distribution of natural images is a landmark problem in unsupervised learning. This task requires an image model that is at once expressive, tractable and scalable. We present a deep neural network that sequentially predicts the pixels in an image along the two spatial dimensions. Our method models the discrete probability of the raw pixel values and encodes the complete set of dependencies in the image. Architectural novelties include fast two-dimensional recurrent layers and an effective

title: PixelCNN++: Improving the PixelCNN with Discretized Logistic Mixture Likelihood and Other Modifications
searchable_text:
PixelCNN++: Improving the PixelCNN with Discretized Logistic Mixture Likelihood and Other Modifications Tractable-likelihood generative modeling of natural images, demonstrated on CIFAR-10. PixelCNN variant using discretized logistic mixture pixel likelihood, whole-pixel conditioning, multiresolution downsampling, shortcut connections, and dropout. Replaces 256-way softmax and R/G/B sub-pixel conditioning with logistic mixture likelihood and whole-pixel conditioning, adding downsampling, shortcut connections, and dropout. TITLE: PixelCNN++: Improving the PixelCNN with Discretized Logistic Mixture Likelihood and Other Modifications

YEAR: 2017

PixelCNNs are a recently proposed class of powerful generative models with tractable likelihood. title: PKDGRAV3: beyond trillion particle cosmological simulations for the next era of galaxy surveys

PKDGRAV3: beyond trillion particle cosmological simulations for the next era of galaxy surveys Large-scale cosmological N-body simulations with trillion-particle counts for galaxy-survey-era cosmology. Fast Multipole Method with individual and adaptive particle time steps efficiently deployed on GPU-accelerated supercomputer nodes. For the first time, FMM plus individual/adaptive particle time steps were efficiently deployed on GPU-accelerated supercomputers. TITLE: PKDGRAV3: beyond trillion particle cosmological simulations for the next era of galaxy surveys

title: Planet Hunters: the first two planet candidates identified by the public using the Kepler public archive data
searchable_text:
Planet Hunters: the first two planet candidates identified by the public using the Kepler public archive data Detection of exoplanet candidates in NASA Kepler public archive data. Public volunteers inspect NASA Kepler public-release data to flag exoplanet candidates, followed by false-positive vetting using spectra, centroid analysis, adaptive optics imaging, stellar modeling, and transit modeling. Moved exoplanet candidate discovery into a citizen-science model using public Kepler archive data rather than relying solely on professional search teams. TITLE: Planet Hunters: the first two planet candidates identified by the public using the Kepler public archive data

YEAR: 2011

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Planet Hunters is a new citizen science project designed to engage the public in an exoplanet search using NASA Kepler public release data. In the first month after launch, users identified two new planet candidates which survived our checks for false positives. The follow-up effort included analysis of Keck HIRES spectra of the host stars, analysis of pixel centroid offsets in the Kepler data and adaptive optics imaging at Ke

title: PLANET HUNTERS. VIII. CHARACTERIZATION OF 41 LONG-PERIOD EXOPLANET CANDIDATES FROM KEPLER ARCHIVAL DATA
searchable_text:
PLANET HUNTERS. VIII. CHARACTERIZATION OF 41 LONG-PERIOD EXOPLANET CANDIDATES FROM KEPLER ARCHIVAL DATA Long-period exoplanet candidates at orbital distances larger than 1 AU in Kepler systems, especially candidates with only one or two visible transits. Planet Hunters searched Kepler Q0–Q17 archival transits, estimated single-transit periods from transit duration and stellar properties, and used follow-up imaging/spectroscopy to validate and characterize host stars. It expands the incomplete >1 AU exoplanet census by adding 41 long-period Planet Hunters candidates from Kepler Q0–Q17 and validating 7 with follow-up characterization. TITLE: PLANET HUNTERS. VIII. CHARACTERIZATION OF 41 LONG-PERIOD EXOPLANET CANDIDATES FROM KEPLER ARCHIVAL DATA

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The census of exoplanets is incomplete for orbital distances larger than 1 AU. Here, we present 41 long-period planet candidates in 38 systems identified by Planet Hunters based on Kepler archival data (Q0–Q17). Among them, 17 exhibit only one transit, 14 have two visible transits, and 10 have more than three visible transits. For planet candidates with only one visible transit, we estimate their orbital periods based on transit dur

title: PLANET OCCURRENCE WITHIN 0.25 AU OF SOLAR-TYPE STARS FROM KEPLER
searchable_text:
PLANET OCCURRENCE WITHIN 0.25 AU OF SOLAR-TYPE STARS FROM KEPLER Occurrence rates of close-in planets (P < 50 days, ≲0.25 AU) versus radius, orbital period, and stellar temperature around solar-type Kepler targets. Estimate planet occurrence by binning Kepler candidates in radius–period space, correcting target-by-target detectability and geometric transit probability, then dividing counts by eligible solar-type stars. Compared with Doppler-era occurrence constraints, Kepler enabled a large transit-candidate census with per-star detection-efficiency and transit-probability corrections, extending robust close-in statistics to ∼2 R⊕ planets. TITLE: PLANET OCCURRENCE WITHIN 0.25 AU OF SOLAR-TYPE STARS FROM KEPLER

ABSTRACT:
We report the distribution of planets as a function of planet radius, orbital period, and stellar effective temperature for orbital periods less than 50 days around solar-type (GK) stars. These results are based on the 1235 planets (formally “planet candidates”) from the Kepler mission that include a nearly complete set of detected planets as small as 2 R⊕. For each of the 156,000 target stars, we assess the detectability of planets as a function of planet radius, Rp, an

title: PLANETARY CANDIDATES FROM THE FIRST YEAR OF THE K2 MISSION
searchable_text:
PLANETARY CANDIDATES FROM THE FIRST YEAR OF THE K2 MISSION Planetary candidate discovery among stars observed along the ecliptic plane during the first year of the K2 mission. Process first-year K2 Campaigns 0–3 public light-curve data and search targets for periodic eclipse signals consistent with planetary transits. ABSTRACT:
The Kepler Space Telescope is currently searching for planets transiting stars along the ecliptic plane as part of its extended K2 mission. We processed the publicly released data from the first year of K2 observations (Campaigns 0, 1, 2, and 3) and searched for periodic eclipse signals consistent with planetary transits. title: PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER IV: PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q8 (22 MONTHS)
searchable_text:
PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER IV: PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q8 (22 MONTHS) Kepler transiting exoplanet candidate identification and parameter updating from nearly two years of high-precision photometry. Update Kepler candidate catalogs from Q1–Q8 flux time series, re-evaluating KOIs and vetting transit signals with pixel-level image-position shifts to reject background eclipsing binaries. Compared with earlier KOIs 1-1609, the study adds Q1–Q8 data, retests background false positives, finds additional multiple systems, and contributes 472 new candidates. TITLE: PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER IV: PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q8 (22 MONTHS)

ABSTRACT:
We provide updates to the Kepler planet candidate sample based upon nearly two years of high-precision photometry (i.e., Q1–Q8). From an initial list of nearly 13,400 threshold crossing events, 480 new host stars are identified from their flux time series as consistent with hosting transiting planets. Potential transit signals are subjected to further analysis using the pixel-level data, which allows background eclipsing binaries to be identified through

title: PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER. V. PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q12 (36 MONTHS)
searchable_text:
PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER. V. PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q12 (36 MONTHS) Updating the Kepler exoplanet candidate catalog from 3 years of observations, including candidate discovery, transit-parameter revision, and catalog fidelity assessment. Apply false-positive exclusion tests to Kepler Q1–Q12 data, then revise transit parameters and posterior distributions using Markov Chain Monte Carlo for the cumulative Kepler Objects of Interest catalog. Compared with the prior 2-year Kepler sample of 2842 candidates, this release uses 3 years of Q1–Q12 data and adds 855 planetary candidates. TITLE: PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER. V. PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q12 (36 MONTHS)

ABSTRACT:
The Kepler mission discovered 2842 exoplanet candidates with 2 yr of data. We provide updates to the Kepler planet candidate sample based upon 3 yr (Q1–Q12) of data. Through a series of tests to exclude false-positives, primarily caused by eclipsing binary stars and instrumental systematics, 855 additional planetary candidates have been discovered, bringing the total number known to 3697. title: PLANETARY CANDIDATES OBSERVED BY KEPLER. VI. PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q16 (47 MONTHS)
searchable_text:

PLANET SAMPLE FROM Q1–Q16 (47 MONTHS) Kepler Q1–Q16 planet-candidate cataloging and vetting for KOIs, especially long-period candidates relevant to occurrence-rate calculations. Catalog construction from nearly four years of Kepler high-precision photometry, with re-vetting of published long-period KOIs and assessment of detection/vetting products. We present the sixth catalog of Kepler candidate planets based on nearly four years of high precision photometry. title: Planetary Candidates Observed by Kepler. VIII. A Fully Automated Catalog with Measured Completeness and Reliability Based on Data Release 25
searchable_text:
Planetary Candidates Observed by Kepler. VIII. A Fully Automated Catalog with Measured Completeness and Reliability Based on Data Release 25 Kepler Q1–Q17 transiting exoplanet candidate cataloging and vetting, including KOI identification, planet-candidate selection, transit fits, completeness, and reliability characterization. Automated Robovetter classification of DR25 threshold crossing events, calibrated by simulated data sets to measure completeness and reliability. Introduces a fully automated DR25 KOI catalog with measured completeness and reliability, adding 219 new candidates including 10 high-reliability terrestrial-size habitable-zone candidates. We present the Kepler Object of Interest (KOI) catalog of transiting exoplanets based on searching 4 yr of Kepler time series photometry (Data Release 25, Q1–Q17). The catalog contains 8054 KOIs, of which 4034 are planet candidates with periods between 0.25 and 632 days. title: Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models
searchable_text:
Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models Neural language modeling for predicting rare or unseen words, exploiting longer contexts, and handling realistic vocabularies and larger corpora. A pointer sentinel mixture architecture that either reproduces a word from recent context or generates one via a standard softmax classifier. Compared with standard softmax LSTMs, the model adds a pointer pathway to copy words from recent context alongside ordinary softmax word production. TITLE: Pointer Sentinel Mixture Models

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recent neural network sequence models with softmax classifiers have achieved their best language modeling performance only with very large hidden states and large vocabularies. Even then they struggle to predict rare or unseen words even if the context makes the prediction unambiguous. We introduce the pointer sentinel mixture architecture for neural sequence models which has the ability to either reproduce a word from the recent context or produce a word from a standard softmax classifier. title: PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification and Segmentation
searchable_text:
PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification and Segmentation 3D point-cloud object classification, part segmentation, and scene semantic parsing. A neural network, PointNet, directly consumes point clouds while respecting permutation invariance of input points. Instead of converting irregular point clouds into voxel grids or image collections, PointNet directly processes point sets. TITLE: PointNet: Deep Learning on Point Sets for 3D Classification and Segmentation

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Point cloud is an important type of geometric data structure. Due to its irregular format, most researchers transform such data to regular 3D voxel grids or collections of images. This, however, renders data unnecessarily voluminous and causes issues. In this paper, we design a novel type of neural network that directly consumes point clouds, which well respects the permutation invariance of points in the input. Our network, named PointNet, provides a unified

title: Positive contraction mappings for classical and quantum Schrödinger systems
searchable_text:
Positive contraction mappings for classical and quantum Schrödinger systems Schrodinger systems for Markov chains, discrete time/space processes, and non-commutative probability-space analogues matching endpoint marginals. Use the Hilbert metric to formulate the Schrodinger bridge/steering problem as a contractive positive map whose fixed point gives the solution. Extends earlier Pavon and Ticozzi work by establishing Schrodinger-system solutions for Markov chains via a Hilbert-metric contraction viewpoint. TITLE: Positive contraction mappings for classical and quantum Schrödinger systems

ABSTRACT:
The classical Schrodinger bridge seeks the most likely probability law for a diffusion process, in path space, that matches marginals at two end points in time; the likelihood is quantified by the relative entropy between the sought law and a prior. Jamison proved that the new law is obtained through a multiplicative functional transformation of the prior.
