We present TriviaQA, a challenging reading comprehension dataset containing over 650K question-answer-evidence triples. TriviaQA includes 95K question-answer pairs authored by trivia enthusiasts and independently gathered evidence documents, six per question on average, that provide high quality distant supervision for answering the questions. We show that, in comparison to other recently introduced large-scale datasets, TriviaQA (1) has relativel

title: Tsallis Regularized Optimal Transport and Ecological Inference
searchable_text:
Tsallis Regularized Optimal Transport and Ecological Inference Ecological inference: reconstructing joint distributions from marginals, especially ethnic-group and voter-preference distributions in political science when side information is available. Tsallis regularized optimal transport unifies Monge-Kantorovitch and Sinkhorn-Cuturi, interpolating distortions from Wasserstein to Kullback-Leibler and enabling efficient optimal-plan algorithms with convergence proofs. It changes optimal transport by unifying Monge-Kantorovitch and Sinkhorn-Cuturi under Tsallis regularization and applying optimal transport to ecological inference for the first time. TITLE: Tsallis Regularized Optimal Transport and Ecological Inference

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 Optimal transport is a powerful framework for computing distances between probability distributions. We unify the two main approaches to optimal transport, namely Monge-Kantorovitch and Sinkhorn-Cuturi, into what we define as Tsallis regularized optimal transport (TROT). TROT interpolates a rich family of distortions from Wasserstein to Kullback-Leibler, encompassing as well Pearson, Neyman and Hellinger divergences, to name a few. We show that metric properties known

title: Tying Word Vectors and Word Classifiers: A Loss Framework for Language Modeling
searchable_text:
Tying Word Vectors and Word Classifiers: A Loss Framework for Language Modeling Recurrent neural network language modeling: predicting sequences of words. A loss framework for language modeling that ties input embedding and output projection matrices, jointly aligning word vectors and word classifiers. Replaces conventional one-hot classification with separate input/output word representations by tying the input embedding and output projection matrices. TITLE: Tying Word Vectors and Word Classifiers: A Loss Framework for Language Modeling

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Recurrent neural networks have been very successful at predicting sequences of words in tasks such as language modeling. However, all such models are based on the conventional classification framework, where the model is trained against one-hot targets, and each word is represented both as an input and as an output in isolation. This causes inefficiencies in learning both in terms of utilizing all of the information and in terms of the number of parameters

title: Unbalanced Optimal Transport: Geometry and Kantorovich Formulation
searchable_text:
Unbalanced Optimal Transport: Geometry and Kantorovich Formulation Defining optimal-transport-like distances between arbitrary positive Radon measures with unequal total mass. Convex unbalanced optimal transport distances using equivalent dynamic geodesic and static Kantorovich coupling formulations for transport, creation, and destruction of mass. Extends dynamically introduced WFR by adding a static Kantorovich formulation and replacing diffeomorphism-group geometry with a semi-direct-product Lagrangian submersion. TITLE: Unbalanced Optimal Transport: Geometry and Kantorovich Formulation

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This article presents a new class of "optimal transportation"-like distances between arbitrary positive Radon measures. These distances are defined by two equivalent alternative formulations: (i) a "fluid dynamic" formulation defining the distance as a geodesic distance over the space of measures (ii) a static "Kantorovich" formulation where the distance is the minimum of an optimization program over pairs of couplings describing the transfer (transport, creation and d

title: Understanding deep learning requires rethinking generalization
searchable_text:
Understanding deep learning requires rethinking generalization Explaining generalization behavior in large deep neural networks, especially state-of-the-art convolutional networks for image classification. Systematic experiments plus a theoretical construction test whether large neural networks can memorize random labels and unstructured inputs despite regularization. Instead of attributing generalization to model-family properties or regularization, it demonstrates memorization capacity through random-label/random-noise experiments and finite-sample expressivity theory. TITLE: Understanding deep learning requires rethinking generalization

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Despite their massive size, successful deep artificial neural networks can exhibit a remarkably small difference between training and test performance. Conventional wisdom attributes small generalization error either to properties of the model family, or to the regularization techniques used during training. 
Through extensive systematic experiments, we show how these traditional approaches fail to explain why large neural networks generalize well in practice. Specifically

title: Universal Function Approximation by Deep Neural Nets with Bounded Width and ReLU Activations
searchable_text:
Universal Function Approximation by Deep Neural Nets with Bounded Width and ReLU Activations Minimal width and depth requirements for ReLU networks approximating continuous functions on the unit cube [0,1]^d. Exploit the convexity of ReLU activations to construct bounded-width deep networks that approximate convex functions.  TITLE: Universal Function Approximation by Deep Neural Nets with Bounded Width and ReLU Activations

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This article concerns the expressive power of depth in neural nets with ReLU activations and a bounded width. We are particularly interested in the following questions: What is the minimal width w min ( d ) so that ReLU nets of width w min ( d ) (and arbitrary depth) can approximate any continuous function on the unit cube [ 0 , 1 ] d arbitrarily well? For ReLU nets near this minimal width, what can one say about the depth necessary to approxi

title: Unrolled Generative Adversarial Networks
searchable_text:
Unrolled Generative Adversarial Networks Stabilizing Generative Adversarial Network training, especially mode collapse, recurrent generator instability, and insufficient diversity or coverage of the data distribution. Define the generator objective with respect to an unrolled optimization of the discriminator, interpolating between current-discriminator training and optimal-discriminator training. Changed GAN generator training from using only the current discriminator to using an unrolled discriminator optimization in the generator objective. TITLE: Unrolled Generative Adversarial Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce a method to stabilize Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) by defining the generator objective with respect to an unrolled optimization of the discriminator. This allows training to be adjusted between using the optimal discriminator in the generator's objective, which is ideal but infeasible in practice, and using the current value of the discriminator, which is often unstable and leads to poor solutions. We show how this technique solves the common problem of mode collapse, sta

title: Unsupervised Learning for Physical Interaction through Video Prediction
searchable_text:
Unsupervised Learning for Physical Interaction through Video Prediction Learning physical object motion and robot pushing interaction dynamics from video without labeled object information, including prediction under future robot actions. Action-conditioned video prediction that explicitly models pixel motion by predicting a distribution over pixel motion from previous frames. Replaces label-dependent dynamics learning with unsupervised, action-conditioned video prediction that explicitly predicts pixel motion distributions. TITLE: Unsupervised Learning for Physical Interaction through Video Prediction

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A core challenge for an agent learning to interact with the world is to predict how its actions affect objects in its environment. Many existing methods for learning the dynamics of physical interactions require labeled object information. However, to scale real-world interaction learning to a variety of scenes and objects, acquiring labeled data becomes increasingly impractical. To learn about physical object motion without labels, we develop an action-conditione

title: Unsupervised Pretraining for Sequence to Sequence Learning
searchable_text:
Unsupervised Pretraining for Sequence to Sequence Learning Improving sequence-to-sequence models for machine translation and abstractive summarization. Initialize seq2seq encoder and decoder weights from two pretrained language models, then fine-tune the full model on labeled data. Compared with purely supervised seq2seq training, the model begins from unsupervised language-model pretrained encoder and decoder weights before supervised fine-tuning. TITLE: Unsupervised Pretraining for Sequence to Sequence Learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This work presents a general unsupervised learning method to improve the accuracy of sequence to sequence (seq2seq) models. In our method, the weights of the encoder and decoder of a seq2seq model are initialized with the pretrained weights of two language models and then fine-tuned with labeled data. We apply this method to challenging benchmarks in machine translation and abstractive summarization and find that it significantly improves the subsequent supervised models. Our

title: Unsupervised representation learning of structured radio communication signals
searchable_text:
Unsupervised representation learning of structured radio communication signals Radio communication signals in raw sampled time series representation, especially structured digital modulation signal representations. Unsupervised representation learning on raw sampled radio time series using convolutional autoencoders to learn modulation basis functions. Shifted from analytic communication bases toward unsupervised learned representations from raw sampled radio signals using convolutional autoencoders. TITLE: Unsupervised representation learning of structured radio communication signals

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We explore unsupervised representation learning of radio communication signals in raw sampled time series representation. We demonstrate that we can learn modulation basis functions using convolutional autoencoders and visually recognize their relationship to the analytic bases used in digital communications. We also propose and evaluate quantitative metrics for quality of encoding using domain relevant performance metrics.

title: UPPER BOUNDS FOR SUNFLOWER-FREE SETS
searchable_text:
UPPER BOUNDS FOR SUNFLOWER-FREE SETS Upper bounds for sunflower-free families of subsets of {1,...,n} and sunflower-free sets in (Z/DZ)^n. Apply the polynomial method to sunflower-free set systems, and use character-based analogues over (Z/DZ)^n for D>2. Instead of using polynomial-method breakthroughs for progression-free sets, the method is applied directly to the Erdős–Szemerédi sunflower problem and extended via characters. TITLE: UPPER BOUNDS FOR SUNFLOWER-FREE SETS

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A collection of $k$ sets is said to form a $k$ -sunflower, or $\unicode[STIX]{x1D6E5}$ -system, if the intersection of any two sets from the collection is the same, and we call a family of sets ${\mathcal{F}}$ sunflower-free if it contains no $3$ -sunflowers. Following the recent breakthrough of Ellenberg and Gijswijt (‘On large subsets of $\mathbb{F}_{q}^{n}$ with no three-term arithmetic progression’, Ann. of Math. (2) 185 (2017), 339–343); (‘Progression-free sets in $\mathbb{Z}_{4}^{n}$ are expo

title: User-friendly guarantees for the Langevin Monte Carlo with inaccurate gradient
searchable_text:
User-friendly guarantees for the Langevin Monte Carlo with inaccurate gradient Approximate sampling from smooth, strongly log-concave probability densities when gradient evaluations may be inaccurate. Nonasymptotic Wasserstein-2 error bounds for first-order LMC with varying step sizes, deterministic/stochastic inaccurate gradients, and Hessian-leveraging second-order LMC discretizations. It extends prior constant-step first-order LMC guarantees with optimized varying step sizes, inaccurate-gradient analysis, and second-order Hessian-based LMC bounds. TITLE: User-friendly guarantees for the Langevin Monte Carlo with inaccurate gradient

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this paper, we study the problem of sampling from a given probability density function that is known to be smooth and strongly log-concave. We analyze several methods of approximate sampling based on discretizations of the (highly overdamped) Langevin diffusion and establish guarantees on its error measured in the Wasserstein-2 distance. Our guarantees improve or extend the state-of-the-art results in three directions. First, we provide an upper bound on

title: Using Fast Weights to Attend to the Recent Past
searchable_text:
Using Fast Weights to Attend to the Recent Past Sequence processing requiring attention to recent past inputs without storing explicit copies of neural activity patterns. Use fast weights as variables changing slower than neural activities but faster than standard weights to store temporary memories and attend to the recent past. Adds fast synaptic variables between neural activities and standard weights, replacing explicit stored activity copies with temporary memory in fast weights. TITLE: Using Fast Weights to Attend to the Recent Past

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Until recently, research on artificial neural networks was largely restricted to systems with only two types of variable: Neural activities that represent the current or recent input and weights that learn to capture regularities among inputs, outputs and payoffs. There is no good reason for this restriction. Synapses have dynamics at many different time-scales and this suggests that artificial neural networks might benefit from variables that change slower than activities but much faste

title: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation
searchable_text:
V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation 3D medical image segmentation of MRI volumes depicting the prostate. Volumetric fully convolutional CNN trained end-to-end on MRI volumes, predicting whole-volume segmentation and optimized with a Dice-coefficient objective plus augmentation. Changed from mostly 2D medical CNN processing to whole-volume 3D segmentation with a volumetric fully convolutional network and Dice-based objective. TITLE: V-Net: Fully Convolutional Neural Networks for Volumetric Medical Image Segmentation

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have been recently employed to solve problems from both the computer vision and medical image analysis fields. Despite their popularity, most approaches are only able to process 2D images while most medical data used in clinical practice consists of 3D volumes. In this work we propose an approach to 3D image segmentation based on a volumetric, fully convolutional, neural network. Our CNN is trained end-to-end on MR

title: VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. II. REFINED STATISTICAL FRAMEWORK AND DESCRIPTIONS OF SYSTEMS OF SPECIAL INTEREST
searchable_text:
VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. II. REFINED STATISTICAL FRAMEWORK AND DESCRIPTIONS OF SYSTEMS OF SPECIAL INTEREST Validation of Kepler multiple transiting planet candidate systems and assessment of false positives, split binary systems, and period-distribution anomalies. Refined statistical framework extrapolating false-positive abundance from single-signal Kepler targets to multiple transiting systems to validate candidates as bona fide exoplanets. Extends Lissauer et al. 2012 from demonstrating high reliability of multis to a refined procedure validating large numbers of multi-planet candidates. TITLE: VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. II. REFINED STATISTICAL FRAMEWORK AND DESCRIPTIONS OF SYSTEMS OF SPECIAL INTEREST

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We extend the statistical analysis performed by Lissauer et al. in 2012, which demonstrates that the overwhelming majority of Kepler candidate multiple transiting systems (multis) represents true transiting planets, and we develop therefrom a procedure to validate large numbers of planet candidates in multis as bona fide exoplanets. We show that this statistical framework correctly estimates the abundanc

title: VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. III. LIGHT CURVE ANALYSIS AND ANNOUNCEMENT OF HUNDREDS OF NEW MULTI-PLANET SYSTEMS
searchable_text:
VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. III. LIGHT CURVE ANALYSIS AND ANNOUNCEMENT OF HUNDREDS OF NEW MULTI-PLANET SYSTEMS Kepler transiting exoplanet candidates in candidate multi-planet systems, specifically establishing which signals are reliable validated planets. Validate Kepler multi-planet candidates by exploiting multiplicity-driven low false-positive rates, removing identified false positives, and deriving properties from light curves plus supporting spectroscopy and imaging. The work announces hundreds of new validated multi-planet systems and nearly doubles the number of verified exoplanets compared with previously verified planet catalogs. TITLE: VALIDATION OF KEPLER'S MULTIPLE PLANET CANDIDATES. III. LIGHT CURVE ANALYSIS AND ANNOUNCEMENT OF HUNDREDS OF NEW MULTI-PLANET SYSTEMS

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
The Kepler mission has discovered more than 2500 exoplanet candidates in the first two years of spacecraft data, with approximately 40% of those in candidate multi-planet systems. The high rate of multiplicity combined with the low rate of identified false positives indicates that the multiplanet systems contain very few false positive signals due to other systems not gravitationally bound to the target

title: Validity of the Born approximation for beyond Gaussian weak lensing observables
searchable_text:
Validity of the Born approximation for beyond Gaussian weak lensing observables Validity and parameter-bias assessment of beyond-Gaussian weak-lensing convergence observables for upcoming galaxy surveys. Born-approximation convergence reconstruction using the density field rather than full gravitational-potential ray tracing, benchmarked against simulations and post-Born corrections. Compared with full ray-tracing, this evaluates the cheaper density-field Born procedure for non-Gaussian weak-lensing statistics and quantifies both biases and computing-time savings. TITLE: Validity of the Born approximation for beyond Gaussian weak lensing observables

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Accurate forward modeling of weak lensing (WL) observables from cosmological parameters is necessary for upcoming galaxy surveys. Because WL probes structures in the non-linear regime, analytical forward modeling is very challenging, if not impossible. Numerical simulations of WL features rely on ray-tracing through the outputs of $N$-body simulations, which requires knowledge of the gravitational potential and accurate solvers for light ray trajectories.

title: Value Iteration Networks
searchable_text:
Value Iteration Networks Predicting outcomes requiring planning-based reasoning, including reinforcement-learning policies, path planning, and natural-language search. Embed a differentiable planning module approximating value iteration as a convolutional neural network, trained end-to-end by backpropagation. Value iteration is transformed into a differentiable neural-network module, enabling end-to-end training rather than using a separate non-differentiable planner. TITLE: Value Iteration Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce the value iteration network (VIN): a fully differentiable neural network with a `planning module' embedded within. VINs can learn to plan, and are suitable for predicting outcomes that involve planning-based reasoning, such as policies for reinforcement learning. Key to our approach is a novel differentiable approximation of the value-iteration algorithm, which can be represented as a convolutional neural network, and trained end-to-end using standard backpropagation. We evaluate VIN based policie

title: Value Prediction Network
searchable_text:
Value Prediction Network Reinforcement learning tasks requiring careful planning in stochastic environments where accurate observation-prediction models are difficult, plus Atari games with short-lookahead planning. A deep RL architecture integrating model-free and model-based RL, learning abstract dynamics states trained for option-conditional predictions of future values rather than future observations. Instead of predicting future observations like typical model-based RL, VPN predicts future values from abstract states and combines model-free and model-based methods in one neural network. TITLE: Value Prediction Network

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper proposes a novel deep reinforcement learning (RL) architecture, called Value Prediction Network (VPN), which integrates model-free and model-based RL methods into a single neural network. In contrast to typical model-based RL methods, VPN learns a dynamics model whose abstract states are trained to make option-conditional predictions of future values (discounted sum of rewards) rather than of future observations. Our experimental results show that VPN has several advantages over both model-free and

title: Variational Walkback: Learning a Transition Operator as a Stochastic Recurrent Net
searchable_text:
Variational Walkback: Learning a Transition Operator as a Stochastic Recurrent Net Learning generative transition operators whose repeated application produces samples matching image data distributions without relying on energy-based detailed-balance Markov chains. Principled variational training learns a stochastic recurrent transition operator by making multi-step trajectories from data “walk back” quickly to the originating data points. Instead of indirectly learning an energy-based Markov chain constrained by detailed balance, it directly learns arbitrarily parameterized non-equilibrium transition operators with asymmetric dynamics. TITLE: Variational Walkback: Learning a Transition Operator as a Stochastic Recurrent Net

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose a novel method to directly learn a stochastic transition operator whose repeated application provides generated samples. Traditional undirected graphical models approach this problem indirectly by learning a Markov chain model whose stationary distribution obeys detailed balance with respect to a parameterized energy function. The energy function is then modified so the model and data distributions match, with no guarantee on the number of st

title: Vector quantile regression beyond correct specification
searchable_text:
Vector quantile regression beyond correct specification Model dependence of a random vector on explanatory variables to capture the whole conditional distribution. Formulate vector quantile regression as an optimal transport problem with an added mean-independence condition. Extends VQR beyond prior focus on correct specification and compares it with Koenker-Bassett quantile regression under univariate dependence. TITLE: Vector quantile regression beyond correct specification

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper studies vector quantile regression (VQR), which is a way to model the dependence of a random vector of interest with respect to a vector of explanatory variables so to capture the whole conditional distribution, and not only the conditional mean. The problem of vector quantile regression is formulated as an optimal transport problem subject to an additional mean-independence condition. This paper provides a new set of results on VQR beyond the case with correct specifi

title: Vegas revisited : Adaptive Monte Carlo integration beyond factorization
searchable_text:
Vegas revisited : Adaptive Monte Carlo integration beyond factorization Adaptive Monte Carlo integration for ill-behaved physics integrals with nonfactorizable singularities. Combine Vegas adaptive Monte Carlo integration with multichannel sampling to handle integrands whose singular structure is not factorizable. Extends Vegas by adding multichannel sampling, moving beyond factorization-based adaptation. TITLE: Vegas revisited : Adaptive Monte Carlo integration beyond factorization

YEAR: 1998

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present a new adaptive Monte Carlo integration algorithm for ill-behaved integrands with nonfactorizable singularities. The algorithm combines Vegas with multichannel sampling and performs significantly better than Vegas for a large class of integrals appearing in physics.

title: Venn-Abers Predictors
searchable_text:
Venn-Abers Predictors Binary prediction problems requiring calibrated probability-type predictions for labels of test objects. Venn-Abers predictors: isotonic-regression-based Venn predictors producing calibrated probability-type predictions for binary labels under an iid data assumption. Introduces an isotonic-regression-based class of Venn predictors, extending prior Venn prediction for binary prediction problems. TITLE: Venn-Abers Predictors

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper continues study, both theoretical and empirical, of the method of Venn prediction, concentrating on binary prediction problems. Venn predictors produce probability-type predictions for the labels of test objects which are guaranteed to be well calibrated under the standard assumption that the observations are generated independently from the same distribution. We give a simple formalization and proof of this property. We also introduce Venn-Abers predictors, a new class of Venn predictors based on the

title: Vers un théorème de la limite centrale dans l'espace de Wasserstein ?
searchable_text:
Vers un théorème de la limite centrale dans l'espace de Wasserstein ? Wasserstein-space barycenters for interpolating among probability measures, with relevance to image processing and statistical learning. Conjecture a central limit theorem for Wasserstein barycenters and prove it in a few special cases. Adds a central-limit-theorem conjecture for Wasserstein barycenters beyond their role as interpolation tools. TITLE: Vers un théorème de la limite centrale dans l'espace de Wasserstein ?

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Resume Les barycentres dans l'espace de Wasserstein constituent une maniere naturelle d'interpoler entre plusieurs mesures de probabillite, utile dans differents domaines appliques comme le traitement d'images ou l'apprentissage statistique. Nous conjecturons que ces barycentres obeissent a un theoreme de la limite centrale que nous demontrons dans quelques cas (tres) particuliers.

title: Version history, similar report, and structure: putting them together for improved bug localization
searchable_text:
Version history, similar report, and structure: putting them together for improved bug localization Automatic bug localization: given a bug report, output a ranked list of source code files likely needing fixes. AmaLgam integrates version-history bug prediction, similar-report analysis, and structural source-code analysis to rank likely buggy files for a submitted bug report. Compared with prior single-source approaches, AmaLgam combines version history, similar bug reports, and structure in one localization method. TITLE: Version history, similar report, and structure: putting them together for improved bug localization

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
During the evolution of a software system, a large number of bug reports are submitted. Locating the source code files that need to be fixed to resolve the bugs is a challenging problem. Thus, there is a need for a technique that can automatically figure out these buggy files. A number of bug localization solutions that take in a bug report and output a ranked list of files sorted based on their likelihood to be buggy have been proposed

title: Video Pixel Networks
searchable_text:
Video Pixel Networks Modeling and generating raw-pixel videos, especially Moving MNIST and action-conditional robotic pushing videos, including motion involving novel objects. A probabilistic Video Pixel Network estimates the discrete joint distribution of raw video pixels using a neural architecture that encodes video as a four-dimensional time-space-color dependency chain. Instead of prior video models, VPN directly models the discrete joint distribution of raw pixel values with video-specific time, space, and color dependencies. TITLE: Video Pixel Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose a probabilistic video model, the Video Pixel Network (VPN), that estimates the discrete joint distribution of the raw pixel values in a video. The model and the neural architecture reflect the time, space and color structure of video tensors and encode it as a four-dimensional dependency chain. The VPN approaches the best possible performance on the Moving MNIST benchmark, a leap over the previous state of the art, and the generated videos show only minor deviations from the ground truth. The VPN also p

title: View Synthesis by Appearance Flow
searchable_text:
View Synthesis by Appearance Flow Novel view synthesis: generating images of the same object or scene from arbitrary viewpoints given one or more input images. Train a CNN to predict appearance flows: 2-D coordinate vectors that copy pixels from the input view to reconstruct the target view. Replaces prior CNN pixel synthesis from scratch with learned copying from the input image via appearance flow, and extends naturally to multiple input views. TITLE: View Synthesis by Appearance Flow

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We address the problem of novel view synthesis: given an input image, synthesizing new images of the same object or scene observed from arbitrary viewpoints. We approach this as a learning task but, critically, instead of learning to synthesize pixels from scratch, we learn to copy them from the input image. Our approach exploits the observation that the visual appearance of different views of the same instance is highly correlated, and such correlation could be explicitly learned by training a convol

title: Vision-and-Language Navigation: Interpreting Visually-Grounded Navigation Instructions in Real Environments
searchable_text:
Vision-and-Language Navigation: Interpreting Visually-Grounded Navigation Instructions in Real Environments Interpreting visually-grounded natural language navigation instructions in real buildings. Frame navigation as a visually grounded sequence-to-sequence problem and benchmark it in Matterport3D Simulator, a large-scale reinforcement learning environment based on real imagery. Introduces Matterport3D Simulator and the first Room-to-Room benchmark dataset for visually-grounded natural-language navigation in real buildings. TITLE: Vision-and-Language Navigation: Interpreting Visually-Grounded Navigation Instructions in Real Environments

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A robot that can carry out a natural-language instruction has been a dream since before the Jetsons cartoon series imagined a life of leisure mediated by a fleet of attentive robot helpers. It is a dream that remains stubbornly distant. However, recent advances in vision and language methods have made incredible progress in closely related areas. This is significant because a robot interpreting a natural-language navigation inst

title: Visual Dialog
searchable_text:
Visual Dialog Answering natural-language questions in multi-turn human-AI dialogs grounded in visual image content and prior conversational context. Neural encoder-decoder Visual Dialog models combine image, dialog history, and current question using Late Fusion, Hierarchical Recurrent, or Memory Network encoders with generative or discriminative decoders. Introduces Visual Dialog as a general visual conversational task, with a novel two-person chat collection protocol, VisDial dataset, neural baselines, and retrieval-based evaluation. TITLE: Visual Dialog

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We introduce the task of Visual Dialog, which requires an AI agent to hold a meaningful dialog with humans in natural, conversational language about visual content. Specifically, given an image, a dialog history, and a question about the image, the agent has to ground the question in image, infer context from history, and answer the question accurately. Visual Dialog is disentangled enough from a specific downstream task so as to serve as a general test of machine intelligence, while being grounded in vision enough to al

title: Visual Genome: Connecting Language and Vision Using Crowdsourced Dense Image Annotations
searchable_text:
Visual Genome: Connecting Language and Vision Using Crowdsourced Dense Image Annotations Image understanding for cognitive tasks such as image description and visual question answering requiring object interaction and relationship reasoning. Crowdsource dense image annotations of objects, attributes, pairwise relationships, region descriptions, and QA, then canonicalize entities and phrases to WordNet synsets for relationship-aware vision-language modeling. Shifted from perceptual-task datasets toward dense, relationship-centric language-vision annotations with objects, attributes, pairwise relationships, region descriptions, QA pairs, and WordNet synset canonicalization. TITLE: Visual Genome: Connecting Language and Vision Using Crowdsourced Dense Image Annotations

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Despite progress in perceptual tasks such as image classification, computers still perform poorly on cognitive tasks such as image description and question answering. Cognition is core to tasks that involve not just recognizing, but reasoning about our visual world. However, models used to tackle the rich content in images for cognitive tasks are still being trained using the same datasets designed for perceptual tasks. To achieve success at cogni

title: Visualisation and 'diagnostic classifiers' reveal how recurrent and recursive neural networks process hierarchical structure
searchable_text:
Visualisation and 'diagnostic classifiers' reveal how recurrent and recursive neural networks process hierarchical structure Learning and processing artificial languages with hierarchical, compositional semantics, specifically computing meanings of nested arithmetic expressions. Formulate hypotheses about hidden-state information, derive time-step feature predictions, and train diagnostic classifiers to test them in recurrent networks processing nested arithmetic expressions. Moves from visualising small recursive-network solutions to hypothesis-driven diagnostic classification of recurrent hidden states at each time step. TITLE: Visualisation and 'diagnostic classifiers' reveal how recurrent and recursive neural networks process hierarchical structure

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We investigate how neural networks can learn and process languages with hierarchical, compositional semantics. To this end, we define the artificial task of processing nested arithmetic expressions, and study whether different types of neural networks can learn to compute their meaning. We find that recursive neural networks can find a generalising solution to this problem, and we visualise this solution by brea

title: VOEvent Standard for Fast Radio Bursts
searchable_text:
VOEvent Standard for Fast Radio Bursts Real-time alerting for fast radio burst detections to enable follow-up searches for repeat bursts or associated multi-wavelength and multi-messenger emission. Disseminate FRB triggers using Virtual Observatory Events with standardized event parameters placed within the VOEvent structure and automated FRBCAT ingestion. Applies the established VOEvent transient-alert format, already used across electromagnetic and multi-messenger astronomy, specifically to FRB trigger dissemination and FRBCAT updates. TITLE: VOEvent Standard for Fast Radio Bursts

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Author(s): Petroff, E; Houben, L; Bannister, K; Burke-Spolaor, S; Cordes, J; Falcke, H; Haren, RV; Karastergiou, A; Kramer, M; Law, C; Leeuwen, JV; Lorimer, D; Martinez-Rubi, O; Rachen, J; Spitler, L; Weltman, A | Abstract: Fast radio bursts are a new class of transient radio phenomena currently detected as millisecond radio pulses with very high dispersion measures. As new radio surveys begin searching for FRBs a large population is expected to be detected in real-time, triggering a range of mul

title: VQA: Visual Question Answering
searchable_text:
VQA: Visual Question Answering Free-form Visual Question Answering: answering natural-language questions about images with accurate natural-language responses. Define an open-ended image-and-language QA benchmark with natural-language answers, automatic evaluation, a large dataset, and baseline comparisons against human performance. Moves beyond generic image captioning by requiring systems to answer selective, open-ended questions about image regions, background details, and context. TITLE: VQA: Visual Question Answering

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We propose the task of free-form and open-ended Visual Question Answering (VQA). Given an image and a natural language question about the image, the task is to provide an accurate natural language answer. Mirroring real-world scenarios, such as helping the visually impaired, both the questions and answers are open-ended. Visual questions selectively target different areas of an image, including background details and underlying context. As a result, a system that succeeds at VQA typically needs a more de

title: Wandering Bumps in Stochastic Neural Fields
searchable_text:
Wandering Bumps in Stochastic Neural Fields Effects of noise on stationary pulse solutions in spatially extended neural fields with translational symmetry and spatial heterogeneity. Model neural fields as spatially extended Langevin equations with multiplicative or additive noise, then use small-noise expansion to approximate bump diffusion under symmetry and heterogeneity. Introduces random fluctuations and symmetry-breaking heterogeneous inputs or synapses to stationary bump neural fields, shifting analysis from deterministic stationary bumps to stochastic wandering and pinning. TITLE: Wandering Bumps in Stochastic Neural Fields

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
We study the effects of noise on stationary pulse solutions (bumps) in spatially extended neural fields. The dynamics of a neural field is described by an integrodifferential equation whose integral term characterizes synaptic interactions between neurons in different spatial locations of the network. Translationally symmetric neural fields support a continuum of stationary bump solutions, which may be centered at any spatial location. Random fluctuations are introduced by modeling the system as a spa

title: Weight Normalization: A Simple Reparameterization to Accelerate Training of Deep Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Weight Normalization: A Simple Reparameterization to Accelerate Training of Deep Neural Networks Training deep neural networks across supervised image recognition, generative modelling, deep reinforcement learning, recurrent models such as LSTMs, and noise-sensitive applications. Reparameterize neural-network weight vectors to decouple their length from their direction, improving optimization conditioning. Compared with batch normalization, it keeps the normalization-inspired speed-up but avoids minibatch-example dependencies and is simpler with lower computational overhead. TITLE: Weight Normalization: A Simple Reparameterization to Accelerate Training of Deep Neural Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present weight normalization: a reparameterization of the weight vectors in a neural network that decouples the length of those weight vectors from their direction. By reparameterizing the weights in this way we improve the conditioning of the optimization problem and we speed up convergence of stochastic gradient descent. Our reparameterization is inspired by batch normalization but does not introduce any dependencies between the examp

title: When Long-Range Zero-Lag Synchronization is Feasible in Cortical Networks
searchable_text:
When Long-Range Zero-Lag Synchronization is Feasible in Cortical Networks Conditions permitting or prohibiting long-range zero-lag neuronal synchronization between distant cortical populations despite synaptic and conduction delays. Analytical methods and computer simulations of two neuronal oscillators interacting via a relay oscillator, testing synaptic coupling, neuron type, and STDP. Beyond prior relay-synchrony results, the study compares type I and type II neurons and adds STDP plus its learning window as a parameter-shaping mechanism. TITLE: When Long-Range Zero-Lag Synchronization is Feasible in Cortical Networks

YEAR: 2012

DOMAIN: ['corpus']

ABSTRACT:
Many studies have reported long-range synchronization of neuronal activity between brain areas, in particular in the beta and gamma bands with frequencies in the range of 14–30 and 40–80 Hz, respectively. Several studies have reported synchrony with zero phase lag, which is remarkable considering the synaptic and conduction delays inherent in the connections between distant brain areas. This result has led to many speculations about the possible functional role of zero-la

title: When Will AI Exceed Human Performance? Evidence from AI Experts
searchable_text:
When Will AI Exceed Human Performance? Evidence from AI Experts Anticipating when AI will exceed human performance in specific activities, all tasks, and all human jobs to inform policy and planning. A large survey of machine learning researchers eliciting beliefs about AI progress and timelines for outperforming humans across tasks.  TITLE: When Will AI Exceed Human Performance? Evidence from AI Experts

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Advances in artificial intelligence (AI) will transform modern life by reshaping transportation, health, science, finance, and the military. To adapt public policy, we need to better anticipate these advances. Here we report the results from a large survey of machine learning researchers on their beliefs about progress in AI. Researchers predict AI will outperform humans in many activities in the next ten years, such as translating languages (by 2024), writing high-school

title: Why Does Deep and Cheap Learning Work So Well?
searchable_text:
Why Does Deep and Cheap Learning Work So Well? Explaining why deep neural networks efficiently learn practical functions arising from physical or hierarchical data-generating processes. Use information-theoretic analysis and no-flattening theorems to link physical structure—symmetry, locality, compositionality, polynomial log-probability and hierarchy—to cheap, efficient deep neural-network approximations. Shifted from generic universal-approximation guarantees to physics-informed function classes and depth-separation/no-flattening arguments explaining exponential efficiency gains. TITLE: Why Does Deep and Cheap Learning Work So Well?

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We show how the success of deep learning could depend not only on mathematics but also on physics: although well-known mathematical theorems guarantee that neural networks can approximate arbitrary functions well, the class of functions of practical interest can frequently be approximated through “cheap learning” with exponentially fewer parameters than generic ones. We explore how properties frequently encountered in physics such as symmetry, locality, compositionality, and polynomial lo

title: Why is Posterior Sampling Better than Optimism for Reinforcement Learning?
searchable_text:
Why is Posterior Sampling Better than Optimism for Reinforcement Learning? Finite-horizon episodic Markov decision processes, targeting Bayesian expected regret over horizon, states, actions, and elapsed time. Use posterior sampling for reinforcement learning to analyze and bound Bayesian expected regret in finite-horizon episodic Markov decision processes. Improves the best previous reinforcement-learning regret bound from Õ(H S √(A T)) to Õ(H √(S A T)). TITLE: Why is Posterior Sampling Better than Optimism for Reinforcement Learning?

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Computational results demonstrate that posterior sampling for reinforcement learning (PSRL) dramatically outperforms algorithms driven by optimism, such as UCRL2. We provide insight into the extent of this performance boost and the phenomenon that drives it. We leverage this insight to establish an $\tilde{O}(H\sqrt{SAT})$ Bayesian expected regret bound for PSRL in finite-horizon episodic Markov decision processes, where $H$ is the horizon, $S$ is the number of

title: Why is Tanimoto index an appropriate choice for fingerprint-based similarity calculations?
searchable_text:
Why is Tanimoto index an appropriate choice for fingerprint-based similarity calculations? Selecting appropriate metrics for fingerprint-based molecular similarity calculations in cheminformatics across molecule size, selection method, and data pretreatment variation. Compare eight similarity/distance metrics on many 100-compound fingerprint datasets by ranking compounds against random references; evaluate rankings using SRD and factor effects using ANOVA. Unlike prior specific-scenario comparisons, this study used a general design that avoided a priori compound knowledge and bias from examining only one or few scenarios. TITLE: Why is Tanimoto index an appropriate choice for fingerprint-based similarity calculations?

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
AbstractBackgroundCheminformaticians are equipped with a very rich toolbox when carrying out molecular similarity calculations. A large number of molecular representations exist, and there are several methods (similarity and distance metrics) to quantify the similarity of molecular representations. In this work, eight well-known similarity/distance metrics are compared on a large dataset of molecular fingerprints with sum of ranking differences

title: Wide Residual Networks
searchable_text:
Wide Residual Networks Image recognition and detection benchmarks using residual networks, including CIFAR, SVHN, COCO, and ImageNet. Decrease depth and increase width of residual networks, producing wide residual networks. Compared with thin, very deep ResNets, WRNs trade depth for greater width in residual blocks. TITLE: Wide Residual Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep residual networks were shown to be able to scale up to thousands of layers and still have improving performance. However, each fraction of a percent of improved accuracy costs nearly doubling the number of layers, and so training very deep residual networks has a problem of diminishing feature reuse, which makes these networks very slow to train. To tackle these problems, in this paper we conduct a detailed experimental study on the architecture of ResNet blocks, based on which we propose a novel architectu

title: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System
searchable_text:
XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System Large-scale tree boosting for machine learning challenges, including sparse data and datasets scaling to billions of examples. Scalable end-to-end tree boosting with sparsity-aware sparse-data handling, weighted quantile sketch approximate tree learning, and cache, compression, and sharding optimizations. Adds a novel sparsity-aware algorithm and weighted quantile sketch, plus cache, compression, and sharding insights, to existing tree boosting systems. TITLE: XGBoost: A Scalable Tree Boosting System

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Tree boosting is a highly effective and widely used machine learning method. In this paper, we describe a scalable end-to-end tree boosting system called XGBoost, which is used widely by data scientists to achieve state-of-the-art results on many machine learning challenges. We propose a novel sparsity-aware algorithm for sparse data and weighted quantile sketch for approximate tree learning. More importantly, we provide insights on cache access patterns, data compression and sharding to build

title: ZOO: Zeroth Order Optimization Based Black-box Attacks to Deep Neural Networks without Training Substitute Models
searchable_text:
ZOO: Zeroth Order Optimization Based Black-box Attacks to Deep Neural Networks without Training Substitute Models Black-box adversarial example generation against deep neural network image classifiers when only inputs and output confidence scores are accessible. Zeroth-order stochastic coordinate descent estimates target-model gradients from confidence-score queries, aided by dimension reduction, hierarchical attack, and importance sampling. Replaces substitute-model transfer attacks with direct zeroth-order gradient estimation on the targeted DNN. TITLE: ZOO: Zeroth Order Optimization Based Black-box Attacks to Deep Neural Networks without Training Substitute Models

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Deep neural networks (DNNs) are one of the most prominent technologies of our time, as they achieve state-of-the-art performance in many machine learning tasks, including but not limited to image classification, text mining, and speech processing. However, recent research on DNNs has indicated ever-increasing concern on the robustness to adversarial examples, especially for security-critical tasks such as traffic sign iden

title: Learning to Communicate with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Learning to Communicate with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Cooperative multi-agent environments where partially observing agents must learn communication protocols to share task-relevant information and maximize shared utility. RIAL applies deep Q-learning; DIAL uses centralized training to backpropagate error derivatives through noisy communication channels, then executes agents decentrally. Introduces RIAL and DIAL; DIAL makes inter-agent communication differentiable during learning, extending deep Q-learning with centralized gradient flow through messages. TITLE: Learning to Communicate with Deep Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We consider the problem of multiple agents sensing and acting in environments with the goal of maximising their shared utility. In these environments, agents must learn communication protocols in order to share information that is needed to solve the tasks. By embracing deep neural networks, we are able to demonstrate end-to-end learning of protocols in complex environments inspired by communication riddles and multi-agent computer vision problems wit

title: How transferable are features in deep neural networks?
searchable_text:
How transferable are features in deep neural networks? Feature reuse and transferability across datasets and tasks in deep neural networks trained on natural images. Layerwise transfer experiments quantify how general or specific neurons are in a deep convolutional network by transferring bottom, middle, or top features between tasks. Moved beyond noting generic first-layer Gabor/color features to experimentally quantifying the layerwise transition from general to task-specific representations. TITLE: How transferable are features in deep neural networks?

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many deep neural networks trained on natural images exhibit a curious phenomenon in common: on the first layer they learn features similar to Gabor filters and color blobs. Such first-layer features appear not to be specific to a particular dataset or task, but general in that they are applicable to many datasets and tasks. Features must eventually transition from general to specific by the last layer of the network, but this transition has not been studied extensi

title: Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning Deep reinforcement learning on the Atari 2600 benchmark, targeting data efficiency and final performance. Combine six independent extensions to the DQN algorithm into a single deep reinforcement learning agent. Instead of evaluating DQN extensions independently, the paper empirically studies their combination and ablates the components. TITLE: Rainbow: Combining Improvements in Deep Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 The deep reinforcement learning community has made several independent improvements to the DQN algorithm. However, it is unclear which of these extensions are complementary and can be fruitfully combined. This paper examines six extensions to the DQN algorithm and empirically studies their combination. Our experiments show that the combination provides state-of-the-art performance on the Atari 2600 benchmark, both in terms of data efficiency and final p

title: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network
searchable_text:
Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network Deploying high-performing neural-network ensembles where prediction cost and serving complexity make full ensembles impractical, including MNIST and commercial acoustic modeling. Compress an ensemble’s predictive knowledge into a single deployable model via distillation, optionally augmented by rapidly trained specialist models for fine-grained confusing classes. Compared with Caruana’s ensemble compression, this work develops a different compression technique and adds ensembles containing full models plus many parallel-trained specialist models. TITLE: Distilling the Knowledge in a Neural Network

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
A very simple way to improve the performance of almost any machine learning algorithm is to train many different models on the same data and then to average their predictions. Unfortunately, making predictions using a whole ensemble of models is cumbersome and may be too computationally expensive to allow deployment to a large number of users, especially if the individual models are large neural nets. Caruana and his collaborators have shown that it is possible to compress t

title: Geometric Deep Learning: Going beyond Euclidean data
searchable_text:
Geometric Deep Learning: Going beyond Euclidean data Large, complex non-Euclidean scientific data such as social, sensor, brain, regulatory networks, and meshed surfaces. Use deep neural networks for geometric data by accounting for non-Euclidean structure and its invariances. Moves deep learning beyond Euclidean or grid-like domains toward networks, meshes, and other geometric data structures. TITLE: Geometric Deep Learning: Going beyond Euclidean data

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many scientific fields study data with an underlying structure that is non-Euclidean. Some examples include social networks in computational social sciences, sensor networks in communications, functional networks in brain imaging, regulatory networks in genetics, and meshed surfaces in computer graphics. In many applications, such geometric data are large and complex (in the case of social networks, on the scale of billions) and are natural targets for machine-learni

title: Reading Wikipedia to Answer Open-Domain Questions
searchable_text:
Reading Wikipedia to Answer Open-Domain Questions Open-domain factoid question answering where answers are text spans in Wikipedia articles. Combines bigram-hashing/TF-IDF Wikipedia retrieval with a multi-layer recurrent neural reader that detects answer spans in retrieved paragraphs. Shifts from standalone retrieval or comprehension toward a complete Wikipedia-based system combining TF-IDF/bigram search, neural reading, and multitask distant supervision. TITLE: Reading Wikipedia to Answer Open-Domain Questions

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
This paper proposes to tackle open-domain question answering using Wikipedia as the unique knowledge source: the answer to any factoid question is a text span in a Wikipedia article. This task of machine reading at scale combines the challenges of document retrieval (finding the relevant articles) with that of machine comprehension of text (identifying the answer spans from those articles). Our approach combines a search component based on bigram hashing and TF-IDF matc

title: Curiosity-Driven Exploration by Self-Supervised Prediction
searchable_text:
Curiosity-Driven Exploration by Self-Supervised Prediction Reinforcement learning exploration in high-dimensional visual environments with sparse or absent extrinsic rewards, evaluated on VizDoom and Super Mario Bros. Curiosity is intrinsic reward from prediction error over action consequences in learned visual features from a self-supervised inverse dynamics model. Replaces direct pixel-level future prediction with prediction in an inverse-dynamics-learned visual feature space focused on controllable consequences of actions. TITLE: Curiosity-Driven Exploration by Self-Supervised Prediction

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In many real-world scenarios, rewards extrinsic to the agent are extremely sparse, or absent altogether. In such cases, curiosity can serve as an intrinsic reward signal to enable the agent to explore its environment and learn skills that might be useful later in its life. We formulate curiosity as the error in an agent's ability to predict the consequence of its own actions in a visual feature space learned by a self-supervised inverse dynamics model. Our form

title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
searchable_text:
Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning Learning control policies for Atari 2600 games directly from high-dimensional sensory input in the Arcade Learning Environment. A convolutional neural network trained with a variant of Q-learning maps raw pixels to a value function estimating future rewards. Introduced a deep learning reinforcement learning model that learns control policies directly from raw high-dimensional sensory input without game-specific architecture or algorithm adjustment. TITLE: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning

YEAR: 2013

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning. The model is a convolutional neural network, trained with a variant of Q-learning, whose input is raw pixels and whose output is a value function estimating future rewards. We apply our method to seven Atari 2600 games from the Arcade Learning Environment, with no adjustment of the architecture or learning algorithm. We

title: Summarizing Source Code using a Neural Attention Model
searchable_text:
Summarizing Source Code using a Neural Attention Model Generating high-level natural-language summaries for source code, especially C# snippets and SQL queries, for uses such as code search and retrieval. CODE-NN uses LSTM networks with attention to generate sentences describing C# code snippets and SQL queries, trained on automatically collected StackOverflow data. Introduces the first completely data-driven, end-to-end neural attention approach for high-level source-code summarization. TITLE: Summarizing Source Code using a Neural Attention Model

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
High quality source code is often paired with high level summaries of the computation it performs, for example in code documentation or in descriptions posted in online forums. Such summaries are extremely useful for applications such as code search but are expensive to manually author, hence only done for a small fraction of all code that is produced. In this paper, we present the first completely datadriven approach for generating high level summaries of source c

title: Hindsight Experience Replay
searchable_text:
Hindsight Experience Replay Sparse-reward reinforcement learning for robotic object manipulation tasks: pushing, sliding, and pick-and-place using only binary task-completion rewards. Hindsight Experience Replay: an off-policy RL-compatible replay technique for sample-efficient learning from sparse, binary rewards, acting as an implicit curriculum without complicated reward engineering. Introduced HER to enable learning from sparse binary rewards while avoiding complicated reward engineering, combinable with arbitrary off-policy RL algorithms. TITLE: Hindsight Experience Replay

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Dealing with sparse rewards is one of the biggest challenges in Reinforcement Learning (RL). We present a novel technique called Hindsight Experience Replay which allows sample-efficient learning from rewards which are sparse and binary and therefore avoid the need for complicated reward engineering. It can be combined with an arbitrary off-policy RL algorithm and may be seen as a form of implicit curriculum. 
We demonstrate our approach on the task of manipulating objects with a robotic arm

title: Trust Region Policy Optimization
searchable_text:
Trust Region Policy Optimization Optimizing large nonlinear control policies, including neural networks, for simulated robotic locomotion and Atari gameplay from screen images. Optimize control policies via Trust Region Policy Optimization, a practical algorithm derived by approximating a theoretically justified monotonic-improvement scheme. Converted a theoretically justified monotonic policy-optimization scheme into a practical trust-region algorithm usable for large nonlinear policies. TITLE: Trust Region Policy Optimization

YEAR: 2015

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
In this article, we describe a method for optimizing control policies, with guaranteed monotonic improvement. By making several approximations to the theoretically-justified scheme, we develop a practical algorithm, called Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO). This algorithm is effective for optimizing large nonlinear policies such as neural networks. Our experiments demonstrate its robust performance on a wide variety of tasks: learning simulated robotic swimming, hopping, and walki

title: Learning Multiagent Communication with Backpropagation
searchable_text:
Learning Multiagent Communication with Backpropagation Fully cooperative multi-agent AI tasks requiring collaboration and communication among multiple agents. CommNet: a simple neural multi-agent model using continuous communication learned jointly with agent policies via backpropagation. Replaces manually specified, fixed communication protocols with communication learned alongside the agents’ policy. TITLE: Learning Multiagent Communication with Backpropagation

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Many tasks in AI require the collaboration of multiple agents. Typically, the communication protocol between agents is manually specified and not altered during training. In this paper we explore a simple neural model, called CommNet, that uses continuous communication for fully cooperative tasks. The model consists of multiple agents and the communication between them is learned alongside their policy. We apply this model to a diverse set of tasks, demonstrating t

title: DeepFix: Fixing Common C Language Errors by Deep Learning
searchable_text:
DeepFix: Fixing Common C Language Errors by Deep Learning Automatically fixing common programming errors in student-written C programs, especially errors caused by inexperience or lack of attention to language details. A multi-layered sequence-to-sequence neural network with attention predicts erroneous C program locations and the required corrected statements. DeepFix shifts from compiler-message-dependent repair toward an end-to-end neural repair system that locates and fixes multiple errors without external tools. TITLE: DeepFix: Fixing Common C Language Errors by Deep Learning

YEAR: 2017

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:

 
 The problem of automatically fixing programming errors is a very active research topic in software engineering. This is a challenging problem as fixing even a single error may require analysis of the entire program. In practice, a number of errors arise due to programmer's inexperience with the programming language or lack of attention to detail. We call these common programming errors. These are analogous to grammatical errors in natural languages. Compiler

title: Embedding Entities and Relations for Learning and Inference in Knowledge Bases
searchable_text:
Embedding Entities and Relations for Learning and Inference in Knowledge Bases Knowledge-base representation learning for link prediction and mining Horn rules requiring compositional relational reasoning. Learn low-dimensional entity vectors and bilinear/linear relation mappings; use bilinear relation matrices whose composition via multiplication supports link prediction and logical rule extraction. Compared with NTN and TransE, it unified embedding models under one framework and added bilinear relation-composition rule mining from learned embeddings. Abstract: We consider learning representations of entities and relations in KBs using the neural-embedding approach. We show that most existing models, including NTN (Socher et al., 2013) and TransE (Bordes et al., 2013b), can be generalized under a unified learning framework, where entities are low-dimensional vectors learned from a neural network and relations are bilinear and/or linear mapping functions. title: Topological quantum memory
searchable_text:
Topological quantum memory Protecting encoded quantum information and supporting fault-tolerant universal quantum computation in topological surface-code architectures. Surface-code quantum memory using 2D qubits on nontrivial topology, homology-cycle logical operations, and error-recovery protocols modeled via 3D Z(2) lattice gauge theory with quenched disorder. Extended Kitaev's surface codes from construction to concrete recovery protocols, threshold and phase-transition analysis, robust recovery without measurement or fast classical processing, and universal fault-tolerant computation procedures. TITLE: Topological quantum memory

YEAR: 2001

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
We analyze surface codes, the topological quantum error-correcting codes introduced by Kitaev. In these codes, qubits are arranged in a two-dimensional array on a surface of nontrivial topology, and encoded quantum operations are associated with nontrivial homology cycles of the surface. We formulate protocols for error recovery, and study the efficacy of these protocols. title: RAPPOR: Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response
searchable_text:
RAPPOR: Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response Crowdsourcing anonymous statistics from end-user client software, especially population statistics over client-side strings, while protecting each client’s privacy. Applies randomized response in a novel, aggregatable way to collect differentially private client reports and enable efficient high-utility population analysis without report linkability. Randomized response is adapted into Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response for aggregatable, unlinkable, high-utility collection of client-side string statistics. TITLE: RAPPOR: Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response

YEAR: 2014

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Randomized Aggregatable Privacy-Preserving Ordinal Response, or RAPPOR, is a technology for crowdsourcing statistics from end-user client software, anonymously, with strong privacy guarantees. In short, RAPPORs allow the forest of client data to be studied, without permitting the possibility of looking at individual trees. By applying randomized response in a novel manner, RAPPOR provides the mechanisms for such collection as well as for efficient, hig

title: BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking from Implicit Feedback
searchable_text:
BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking from Implicit Feedback Personalized item ranking from implicit feedback such as clicks and purchases for websites, movies, products, and similar item recommendation settings. BPR-Opt, a Bayesian maximum-posterior ranking criterion, optimized with stochastic gradient descent and bootstrap sampling for recommender models such as MF and adaptive kNN. Replaced standard implicit-feedback MF/kNN training with a generic Bayesian criterion directly optimized for personalized ranking. TITLE: BPR: Bayesian Personalized Ranking from Implicit Feedback

ABSTRACT:
Item recommendation is the task of predicting a personalized ranking on a set of items (e.g. websites, movies, products). In this paper, we investigate the most common scenario with implicit feedback (e.g. clicks, purchases). There are many methods for item recommendation from implicit feedback like matrix factorization (MF) or adaptive k-nearest-neighbor (kNN). title: Towards Evaluating the Robustness of Neural Networks
searchable_text:
Towards Evaluating the Robustness of Neural Networks Robustness evaluation of neural networks against adversarial examples, especially defenses intended for security-critical applications. Introduce three adversarial-example attack algorithms tailored to three prior distance metrics, plus high-confidence transferability testing, to evaluate and break defensive distillation. Replaced prior less-effective adversarial example generation with attacks tailored to three literature distance metrics and a high-confidence transferability test against defensive distillation. TITLE: Towards Evaluating the Robustness of Neural Networks

YEAR: 2016

DOMAIN: ['golden_trace', 'corpus_recovered']

ABSTRACT:
Neural networks provide state-of-the-art results for most machine learning tasks. Unfortunately, neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples: given an input x and any target classification t, it is possible to find a new input x' that is similar to x but classified as t.
